reassess: Recheck core temp continuously; aim 1-2C rise per hour
Avoid rough handling and overzealous rewarming (afterdrop)
If in arrest, continue CPR and rewarm; 'not dead until warm and dead'
Reassess electrolytes and glucose during rewarming
disposition: Severe/moderate hypothermia or arrest: ICU admission/transfer with active rewarming
Mild hypothermia: rewarm, observe, discharge if normalized and safe environment
Transfer for ECMO/bypass rewarming capability if severe/refractory
Address social/housing factors before discharge
discharge: Ensure normothermia and stable vitals before discharge
Cold-exposure prevention and adequate shelter/clothing counseling
Treat associated frostbite and provide wound care instructions
Return precautions for recurrent cold injury or confusion
followup: PCP follow-up; endocrine workup if hypothyroid cause found
Social work for housing/exposure risk
Frostbite/wound clinic referral if tissue injury
Substance-use resources if intoxication contributed

redFlags: Core temp <28C or cardiac arrest/VF
Hemodynamic instability or malignant dysrhythmia
Afterdrop with deterioration during rewarming
Concurrent trauma or severe frostbite
Failure to rewarm without extracorporeal support
compliance: EMTALA: stabilize and begin rewarming as part of medical screening exam before transfer
Severe hypothermia requiring ECMO/bypass mandates appropriate transfer to a facility with that capability
Document observation-vs-transfer decision and capability limits of the FSED
Report suspected adult/elder neglect or self-neglect (vulnerable adult) and consider homelessness resources per Texas reporting
Continue active rewarming during interfacility transport and document handoff

name: Carbon Monoxide Exposure
summary: Tissue hypoxia from carboxyhemoglobin; nonspecific headache/nausea to syncope/seizure; pulse oximetry falsely normal; consider concurrent cyanide in fires.

triage: Remove from source; ensure scene safety reported
High-flow O2 immediately on arrival
History: enclosed space, heater/generator, fire, multiple sick contacts
Note standard SpO2 is unreliable (cannot detect COHb)
Assess for neuro symptoms, chest pain, pregnancy status
nursing: Apply 100% O2 via non-rebreather immediately
Cardiac monitor, SpO2 (recognize limitation), CO-oximetry if available
IV access
12-lead ECG
Document time O2 started (affects COHb half-life)
keyOrders: 100% high-flow O2 via non-rebreather (or intubation if needed)
Accucheck; assess for concurrent cyanide in fire victims
*Pregnancy test if indicated (fetal risk affects HBO decision)
labs: VBG/ABG with lactate (elevated lactate suggests cyanide)
*Cyanide level / empiric treatment if indicated in fire/smoke
Pregnancy test in people of childbearing potential
imaging: 12-lead ECG for ischemia
*CXR if indicated for smoke inhalation/aspiration
*CT head if indicated for persistent neuro deficit
meds: Oxygen 100% non-rebreather (continue until COHb normal and asymptomatic)
IV crystalloid for hypotension
Hydroxocobalamin 5 g IV if concurrent cyanide suspected (fire victims)
*Mannitol/seizure management if indicated for cerebral edema/seizure
Continue O2 in pregnancy 5x longer than nonpregnant
reassess: Trend COHb and symptoms on 100% O2
Reassess neuro status and cardiac symptoms
Determine hyperbaric oxygen (HBO) candidacy
Monitor for delayed neuropsychiatric sequelae counseling
disposition: Consider HBO transfer for: LOC, neuro deficit, COHb >25% (>15-20% if pregnant), cardiac ischemia, metabolic acidosis
Admit/observe symptomatic patients on O2 until improved
Discharge asymptomatic with low COHb after normalization
Transfer to hyperbaric facility per criteria
discharge: Confirm safe environment; CO source remediated and detector installed
Counsel on delayed neurologic sequelae (weeks)
Return precautions for headache, confusion, chest pain
Verify no other household members affected
followup: PCP/neurology follow-up for delayed cognitive symptoms
Neuropsychiatric assessment if persistent deficits
OB follow-up if pregnant
Home CO detector and source repair confirmation

redFlags: Loss of consciousness, seizure, or focal neuro deficit
Myocardial ischemia / chest pain / elevated troponin
Metabolic acidosis or elevated lactate (consider cyanide)
Pregnancy with significant exposure
COHb markedly elevated or symptoms persist despite O2
compliance: EMTALA: initiate 100% O2 and screening before any transfer
If HBO indicated, arrange appropriate transfer to hyperbaric facility with physician handoff; FSED typically lacks HBO
Document observation-vs-transfer rationale based on HBO criteria
Report suspected intentional CO exposure (assault/suicide) and consider public-health notification for source/multiple victims
Coordinate with fire/EMS and notify others potentially exposed at the source

name: Electrical Injury
summary: Injury spectrum from minor burns to arrhythmia, deep tissue/myonecrosis, and cardiac arrest; low-voltage vs high-voltage vs lightning differ in management.

triage: Confirm scene safe and source de-energized
Determine voltage (low <1000V vs high), AC/DC, lightning, contact duration
Assess ABCs; immediate 12-lead ECG and rhythm
Look for entry/exit wounds and associated trauma/fall
Spinal precautions if fall or tetanic contraction
nursing: Cardiac monitor, continuous SpO2
12-lead ECG on arrival
Establish IV access x2; aggressive fluids if deep burns
Insert Foley to monitor urine output/myoglobinuria
Wound care and burn assessment; spinal immobilization if indicated
keyOrders: IV crystalloid titrated to urine output (higher for deep tissue injury)
labs: CK (rhabdomyolysis - often underestimated by surface burn)
Troponin and ECG for cardiac injury
*Coagulation/type & screen if indicated for major injury
imaging: 12-lead ECG (repeat)
*C-spine/long-bone imaging if indicated for fall/tetany fractures
*CT head/trauma series if indicated with LOC or mechanism
*Imaging of affected limb if indicated for deep injury
meds: Normal saline/LR IV to target urine output 1-1.5 mL/kg/hr (more with rhabdo)
Analgesia: morphine 0.1 mg/kg IV or fentanyl 1 mcg/kg IV titrated
Tetanus prophylaxis (Tdap) if not up to date
Sodium bicarbonate per protocol for significant rhabdomyolysis
*ACLS antiarrhythmics if indicated for dysrhythmia
reassess: Reassess rhythm and repeat ECG; monitor for delayed arrhythmia
Trend CK, potassium, and urine output for rhabdo/AKI
Reassess perfusion/compartments of injured limbs
Reevaluate hidden deep-tissue injury beyond surface burn
disposition: High-voltage, abnormal ECG, arrhythmia, rhabdo, or LOC: admit/transfer with monitoring
Low-voltage, asymptomatic, normal ECG, no injury: may discharge after observation
Transfer to burn/trauma center for significant burns or deep injury
ICU for dysrhythmia or critical organ involvement
discharge: Burn wound care and dressing instructions
Return precautions for palpitations, dark urine, worsening pain/swelling
Confirm normal ECG and asymptomatic before low-voltage discharge
Activity limitations per injury
followup: Burn clinic / wound care follow-up
Cardiology if any ECG changes
PCP for renal recheck if rhabdo
Cataract/neuro follow-up for high-voltage/lightning (delayed sequelae)

redFlags: Cardiac arrest, ventricular arrhythmia, or abnormal ECG
High-voltage or lightning injury
Rhabdomyolysis with rising CK / AKI / hyperkalemia
Compartment syndrome or extensive deep-tissue burns
LOC, associated trauma, or spinal injury
compliance: EMTALA: complete medical screening exam, ECG, and stabilization before transfer
Significant burns/high-voltage injury require appropriate transfer to a burn/trauma center per transfer agreements
Document observation-vs-transfer decision for low-voltage asymptomatic patients
Report suspected assault, occupational injury, or child/elder abuse per Texas mandatory reporting
Coordinate occupational/workplace injury documentation

name: Drowning / Submersion
summary: Respiratory impairment from submersion/immersion; hypoxia is primary insult; risk of ARDS, arrhythmia, and hypothermia; outdated terms 'wet/dry/near' discouraged.

triage: Assess airway, breathing, oxygenation immediately
History: duration of submersion, water type/temp, witnessed, resuscitation given
Measure core temperature (associated hypothermia)
Spinal precautions if diving/trauma mechanism
Note any LOC, seizure, or arrhythmia
nursing: Apply high-flow O2; support ventilation as needed
Cardiac monitor, continuous SpO2, core temp
IV access
Position to protect airway; suction as needed
Begin rewarming if hypothermic
keyOrders: Supplemental/high-flow O2; CPAP or intubation for respiratory failure
imaging: CXR (may lag behind clinical picture; repeat as needed)
12-lead ECG
*CT head/C-spine if indicated for trauma or persistent AMS
*Repeat CXR if indicated for evolving ARDS
meds: Oxygen / positive-pressure ventilation as primary therapy
Albuterol 2.5-5 mg nebulized for bronchospasm
IV crystalloid for hypotension
Warmed fluids if hypothermic
*Vasopressors/ACLS if indicated for shock or arrest
Antibiotics only for documented infection (not prophylactic)
reassess: Serial respiratory assessment and SpO2 over observation window
Watch for delayed pulmonary edema/ARDS for 4-8 hr
Reassess mental status and oxygenation trends
Continue rewarming and reassess temperature
disposition: Symptomatic, hypoxic, or abnormal exam: admit/transfer (ICU if intubated)
Asymptomatic with normal exam, SpO2, and CXR: observe 4-8 hr then discharge
Transfer for mechanical ventilation/ICU or ECMO needs
Any cardiac arrest or persistent neuro deficit: critical-care transfer
discharge: Discharge only after observation period with normal oxygenation and exam
Return precautions for cough, dyspnea, fever within 24-48 hr
Water-safety counseling
Avoid driving/strenuous activity until cleared
followup: PCP follow-up 24-48 hr
Pulmonary referral if respiratory symptoms persisted
Counsel on supervision/barriers and CPR training
Address substance use if contributory

redFlags: Hypoxia or respiratory distress requiring ventilatory support
Cardiac arrest or malignant dysrhythmia
Altered mental status or seizure
Severe hypothermia
Evolving pulmonary edema/ARDS during observation
compliance: EMTALA: stabilize airway/oxygenation as part of medical screening exam before transfer
Patients needing ventilation/ICU require appropriate transfer with documented handoff and continued O2 en route
Document mandated observation window and observation-vs-transfer rationale given delayed ARDS risk
Report suspected child drowning neglect, abuse, or unsafe-pool conditions per Texas mandatory reporting; consider drowning as possible non-accidental in children
Coordinate with EMS/law enforcement for fatalities and possible medical examiner notification

name: Envenomation / Stings
summary: Local and systemic effects from snake (crotalid), arthropod, or marine venom; manage local tissue injury, coagulopathy, and anaphylaxis; antivenom for pit vipers.

triage: Identify offending species/region if possible (do not bring live animal)
Mark and time the leading edge of swelling/erythema
Assess for systemic signs: hypotension, bleeding, neuro symptoms
Immobilize affected limb at heart level; remove constricting items
Screen for anaphylaxis to stings (see anaphylaxis pathway)
nursing: Cardiac monitor, SpO2, frequent vitals
IV access in unaffected limb
Serial limb circumference measurements with time stamps
Avoid tourniquets, incision, suction, or ice on snakebites
Prepare antivenom and epinephrine for reactions
keyOrders: Serial swelling margin marking q15-30 min
*Crotalidae antivenom (CroFab/Anavip) if indicated for progressive envenomation
imaging: Usually none for envenomation site
*Plain film if indicated for retained stinger/foreign body or marine spine
*Doppler/imaging if indicated to assess perfusion vs compartment
meds: Crotalidae antivenom (CroFab 4-6 vials initial, or Anavip) for progressive crotalid envenomation
Analgesia: fentanyl 1 mcg/kg IV or morphine, titrate
Tetanus prophylaxis (Tdap) if not current
Epinephrine 0.3-0.5 mg IM for anaphylaxis to stings
Antihistamines/antiemetics as adjuncts
*Antibiotics only if indicated for established wound infection
reassess: Serial limb exam, swelling progression, and coagulation studies
Reassess for antivenom indication (worsening local/systemic/labs)
Monitor for antivenom infusion reactions
Reevaluate compartment pressure if tense, painful swelling (true compartment syndrome rare)
disposition: Significant/progressive crotalid envenomation: admit/transfer for antivenom and serial labs
Dry bite or minor sting, stable, no progression after 8 hr observation: discharge
Transfer if antivenom unavailable or for ICU-level toxicity
Consult poison control / toxicology for all envenomations
discharge: Observe minimum 8 hr (dry bite) with normal labs before discharge
Return precautions for spreading swelling, bleeding, gum bleeding, bruising (delayed coagulopathy)
Wound care and tetanus documentation
Avoid NSAIDs/aspirin if coagulopathy risk
followup: Recheck coagulation labs in 2-3 days and ~1 week for crotalid (recurrent coagulopathy)
Wound check / hand specialist for severe local injury
Poison control follow-up guidance
PCP follow-up

redFlags: Progressive swelling beyond the bitten extremity or rapid spread
Coagulopathy: low fibrinogen, prolonged PT/PTT, active bleeding
Systemic toxicity: hypotension, neuro deficits, respiratory compromise
Anaphylaxis to sting
Antivenom infusion reaction
compliance: EMTALA: medical screening exam and stabilization before any transfer
If antivenom is unavailable or toxicity exceeds FSED capability, arrange appropriate transfer to an antivenom-stocked facility with toxicology support
Contact Texas Poison Center Network (1-800-222-1222) and document guidance
Document observation-vs-transfer decision and minimum observation window
Report animal bites (rabies-vector exposure) to local public health per Texas reporting requirements

name: Chemical Exposure
summary: Dermal/ocular/inhalational/ingestion exposure to caustics, irritants, or systemic toxins; decontamination and identifying the agent drive management.

triage: Ensure decontamination BEFORE entry to protect staff (don PPE)
Identify agent, concentration, route, and time of exposure (SDS if available)
Assess airway for inhalational/caustic injury; ocular exposures need immediate irrigation
Vitals + SpO2; note respiratory distress or stridor
Contact poison control early for agent-specific guidance
nursing: Remove contaminated clothing; copious water irrigation of skin/eyes
Continuous eye irrigation (e.g., Morgan lens) for ocular caustics until pH neutral
Cardiac monitor, SpO2, IV access
Avoid neutralizing agents (exothermic); use water/saline
Isolate contaminated materials per hazmat protocol
keyOrders: High-flow O2 for inhalation; prepare airway equipment
Ocular pH testing before and after irrigation
*Agent-specific antidote per poison control if indicated
labs: *Co-oximetry / methemoglobin / cyanide if indicated by agent
*Acetaminophen and salicylate levels if indicated for ingestion
*Osmolar gap/anion gap if indicated for toxic alcohols
imaging: *CXR if indicated for inhalational injury or aspiration
*Upright chest/abdomen if indicated for caustic ingestion perforation
12-lead ECG for cardiotoxic agents
*Endoscopy referral if indicated for significant caustic ingestion
meds: Copious water/saline irrigation is primary therapy for dermal/ocular
Calcium gluconate gel/IV for hydrofluoric acid exposure
Albuterol 2.5-5 mg nebulized for bronchospasm
IV crystalloid for hypotension
Agent-specific antidotes per poison control (e.g., hydroxocobalamin, atropine/pralidoxime for organophosphates)
Analgesia and antiemetics as needed
reassess: Recheck ocular pH after irrigation until neutral (7.0-7.5)
Reassess airway/respiratory status for delayed inhalational injury
Reassess burn depth/extent after decontamination
Monitor for systemic toxicity per agent (delayed effects)
disposition: Caustic ingestion, airway involvement, or systemic toxicity: admit/transfer
Minor dermal/ocular exposure fully decontaminated and asymptomatic: discharge after observation
Transfer to burn center for significant chemical burns or to facility with endoscopy/toxicology
ICU/poison-center-guided disposition for systemic toxins
discharge: Confirm complete decontamination and symptom resolution
Wound/burn care and eye care instructions
Return precautions for delayed respiratory or visual symptoms
Provide agent name/SDS info for occupational records
followup: Ophthalmology within 24 hr for ocular caustic injury
Burn clinic / wound care for chemical burns
Occupational health and PCP follow-up
Poison control follow-up guidance

redFlags: Airway compromise, stridor, or inhalational injury
Caustic ingestion with drooling, vomiting, or chest/abdominal pain (perforation)
Ocular caustic (alkali) injury with persistent abnormal pH or vision loss
Systemic toxicity: arrhythmia, acidosis, altered mentation
Hydrofluoric acid exposure with hypocalcemia/arrhythmia
compliance: EMTALA: decontaminate and complete medical screening exam/stabilization before transfer
Caustic ingestion or major chemical burns require appropriate transfer to facilities with endoscopy/burn/toxicology capability
Contact Texas Poison Center Network (1-800-222-1222) and document agent and guidance
Document decontamination, observation-vs-transfer rationale, and staff PPE/hazmat protocol
Report occupational chemical exposures, hazmat releases, and suspected intentional poisoning/assault per Texas and OSHA requirements

name: Right Upper Quadrant Pain

HIDA scan referral if acalculous cholecystitis suspected* if indicated

Morphine 0.1 mg/kg IV or hydromorphone 0.5-1 mg IV for severe pain
Ondansetron 4 mg IV
Piperacillin-tazobactam 3.375 g IV for cholangitis/cholecystitis*
IV crystalloid resuscitation if septic* if indicated
reassess: Recheck pain after analgesia and US results
Trend vitals for evolving sepsis
Reassess Murphy sign
Review LFT trend
disposition: Surgical consult/transfer for acute cholecystitis or cholangitis
Cholangitis needs urgent biliary decompression (ERCP) facility
Discharge uncomplicated biliary colic with surgical follow-up
Document EMTALA stabilization before transfer
discharge: Low-fat diet instructions
Return for fever, jaundice, persistent vomiting, worsening pain
Analgesia and antiemetic prescriptions
General surgery referral for elective cholecystectomy
followup: Surgery clinic within 1 week for biliary colic
PCP for LFT recheck if elevated
Hepatology if hepatitis suspected*
ER return for red-flag symptoms

redFlags: Charcot triad (fever, jaundice, RUQ pain) / Reynolds pentad
Hypotension or sepsis physiology
Marked transaminase or bilirubin elevation
Peritoneal signs
Severe pain unrelieved by analgesia

Observation acceptable for stable biliary colic; transfer if surgical admission needed

name: Right Lower Quadrant Pain

nursing: IV access and labs first stick
NPO pending surgical evaluation
Serial pain and abdominal exams

Type & screen if surgery likely* if indicated

Graded-compression US as alternative to limit radiation*
MRI abdomen if pregnant and US nondiagnostic* if indicated
meds: Morphine 0.1 mg/kg IV or fentanyl 1 mcg/kg IV
Ondansetron 4 mg IV

Trend WBC and vitals
Reassess for evolving peritonitis
Confirm pregnancy status before disposition
disposition: Surgical consult/transfer for confirmed appendicitis
GYN consult/transfer for ovarian torsion or ectopic
Discharge nonspecific pain if stable, tolerating PO, reassuring serial exam
Document EMTALA stabilization before transfer
discharge: Return precautions: worsening/migrating pain, fever, vomiting
Advance diet as tolerated
Short course antiemetic/analgesia
Follow-up within 24 h for unresolved pain
followup: Surgery clinic if appendicitis managed nonoperatively elsewhere

PCP recheck in 1-2 days

redFlags: Peritoneal signs / rebound suggesting perforated appendicitis
Positive hCG with pain (ectopic)

Sepsis physiology
Inability to tolerate PO with rising WBC

name: Epigastric Pain
summary: Epigastric pain spanning gastritis/PUD/pancreatitis to life threats: ACS, aortic dissection, perforated ulcer.

triage: Vitals; obtain ECG within 10 min for any cardiac concern
Characterize pain: burning vs tearing vs band-like radiation
Cardiac risk factors, NSAID/alcohol use, prior PUD
Screen for melena/hematemesis
Notify MD immediately for ischemic ECG or instability
nursing: Cardiac monitor and pulse oximetry
IV access and labs first stick
NPO until cardiac/surgical cause excluded

imaging: Chest X-ray (free air, mediastinum)
CT abdomen with contrast for pancreatitis/perforation*
CT angiography chest if dissection suspected*
RUQ/abdominal US if biliary cause* if indicated

Pantoprazole 40 mg IV for PUD/gastritis
Ondansetron 4 mg IV
Morphine/fentanyl IV titrated for severe pain (e.g., pancreatitis)
IV crystalloid resuscitation for pancreatitis/hypovolemia* if indicated
reassess: Reassess after GI cocktail or ACS workup
Trend troponin per protocol
Recheck vitals and pain score

Observe for serial troponin if intermediate cardiac risk
Discharge gastritis/dyspepsia if pain resolved and stable
Document EMTALA stabilization before transfer
discharge: Start PPI; avoid NSAIDs/alcohol
Return for chest pressure, vomiting blood, black stool, syncope
H. pylori testing referral*
PCP follow-up 1-3 days
followup: PCP for dyspepsia and possible EGD referral
Cardiology if cardiac workup indeterminate
GI for refractory symptoms

redFlags: Ischemic ECG changes or positive troponin
Tearing pain radiating to back / pulse deficit (dissection)
Rigid abdomen / free air (perforation)
Hematemesis or melena with hemodynamic change

name: Nausea & Vomiting
summary: Symptom of broad differential; assess for obstruction, DKA, CNS cause, pregnancy, and dehydration.

Character of vomitus (bilious, bloody, feculent)
Associated headache, abdominal pain, chest pain
Pregnancy status; diabetic history
Notify MD for bilious/bloody emesis or altered mentation
nursing: IV access; labs first stick
Antiemetic per protocol
Strict I&O; monitor for ongoing losses
NPO or sips pending evaluation
Reassess hydration and symptom relief

imaging: Abdominal X-ray/obstruction series if SBO suspected*
CT abdomen/pelvis for refractory or pathologic vomiting*
CT/MRI head if neuro signs or worst headache*
RUQ US if biliary cause* if indicated

OB for hyperemesis gravidarum*
GI for recurrent/cyclic vomiting

name: Diarrhea

Discharge only after confirmed passage and tolerating PO
Document EMTALA stabilization before transfer
discharge: Soft diet, thorough chewing, avoid large meat boluses
Return for recurrent impaction, chest pain, fever, inability to swallow
Counsel on underlying esophageal disease evaluation
Arrange GI follow-up
followup: GI for EGD, biopsy (eosinophilic esophagitis), stricture dilation
PCP for reflux management
Allergy/GI for EoE workup*
ER return for recurrent or persistent symptoms

redFlags: Inability to handle secretions / drooling (airway risk)
Respiratory distress or aspiration
Chest pain, fever, subcutaneous emphysema (perforation)
Disk battery or sharp object impaction (emergent)
Complete obstruction persisting beyond several hours
compliance: EMTALA screening exam and stabilization documented before transfer
Unresolved impaction requires timely transfer to endoscopy-capable hospital with accepting physician (urgent if complete/airway risk)
FSED observation acceptable only briefly while monitoring for spontaneous passage in a stable patient
Texas FSED fee/insurance disclosure; document transfer mode and physician-to-physician communication

name: Fever
summary: Elevated core temperature; identify source vs occult/serious bacterial infection and rule out sepsis.

triage: Vitals incl. temperature (oral/temporal/rectal) and SpO2
Screen for sepsis (qSOFA/SIRS), neutropenia history, immunosuppression
Ask onset, travel, sick contacts, localizing symptoms
Assign ESI 2 if hemodynamically unstable or toxic-appearing
nursing: Continuous vitals if febrile + tachycardic/hypotensive
Antipyretic per protocol once ordered
Cooling measures for hyperthermia >40C
Strict I/O; isolation precautions if infectious source suspected*
keyOrders: IV access if ill-appearing or needs fluids/antibiotics
Cardiac monitor + continuous SpO2 if unstable*
Sepsis bundle activation if SIRS + suspected infection* if indicated
labs: Blood cultures x2 before antibiotics if sepsis/toxic*
Respiratory viral panel / influenza / COVID PCR* if indicated
imaging: Chest X-ray if respiratory symptoms or no clear source
Targeted imaging to suspected source (CT abdomen/pelvis, soft tissue US)* if indicated
Lumbar puncture if meningismus/altered* if indicated
meds: Acetaminophen 650-1000 mg PO/IV q6h (max 3-4 g/day)
Ibuprofen 400-600 mg PO q6h if no contraindication*
Empiric antibiotics targeted to source if bacterial suspected*
IV crystalloid bolus 30 mL/kg if hypotensive/septic*
reassess: Recheck temperature and vitals after antipyretic in 30-60 min
Reassess for evolving sepsis / hemodynamic instability
Trend lactate if elevated*
Reassess mental status and source localization
disposition: Discharge if source identified, stable, low-risk
Observe for borderline vitals or pending cultures*
Admit/transfer for sepsis, neutropenic fever, or unclear serious source
Transfer to higher level if ICU-level care needed
discharge: Antipyretic instructions and hydration
Specific return precautions for worsening fever or new symptoms
Course of antibiotics if prescribed with adherence counseling*
Culture follow-up plan and contact info
followup: PCP within 24-48 h if not improving
Pending culture callback protocol
Specialist referral based on identified source*

redFlags: Hypotension, altered mental status, or qSOFA >=2 (sepsis)
Neutropenic fever (ANC <500)
Meningismus, petechial/purpuric rash, or new focal neuro deficit
Fever in immunocompromised, post-transplant, or recent chemotherapy
Temp >41C (hyperthermia) or rapid clinical deterioration
compliance: EMTALA medical screening exam to exclude emergency condition before disposition
Initiate stabilization (fluids/antibiotics) prior to any appropriate transfer for sepsis
Observation vs transfer: transfer if neutropenic fever or sepsis requiring ICU/admission FSED cannot provide
Texas mandatory reportable conditions (e.g., suspected meningococcal, certain notifiable infections) reported to DSHS/local health authority

name: Sepsis / SIRS
summary: Life-threatening organ dysfunction from dysregulated host response to infection; time-critical resuscitation.

triage: Immediate vitals; flag SIRS criteria and qSOFA
Identify suspected infection source
ESI 1-2; activate sepsis alert / bundle
Note immunosuppression, indwelling devices, recent procedures
nursing: Continuous cardiac, BP, SpO2 monitoring
Two large-bore IVs; draw cultures before antibiotics
Strict hourly I/O and urine output
Begin Surviving Sepsis 1-hour bundle timing
keyOrders: Activate sepsis bundle; start clock at recognition
IV crystalloid 30 mL/kg bolus for hypotension or lactate >=4
12-lead ECG; cardiac monitor; O2 to keep SpO2 >=94%
Foley for accurate urine output if shock*
labs: CBC with diff, CMP, coags, procalcitonin
Type and screen if surgical source possible*
imaging: Chest X-ray
CT or US directed at suspected source (abdomen, soft tissue)* if indicated
POCUS for volume status / IVC and cardiac function*
meds: Broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 h (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g IV; add vancomycin 15-20 mg/kg IV if MRSA risk)
Norepinephrine 0.05-0.1 mcg/kg/min IV titrated for MAP >=65 after fluids
Source-specific coverage (e.g., add metronidazole 500 mg IV for intra-abdominal)*
Stress-dose hydrocortisone 50 mg IV q6h in refractory shock*
reassess: Reassess perfusion: MAP, lactate clearance, urine output, mental status
Dynamic fluid responsiveness assessment after boluses
Escalate to vasopressors if MAP <65 despite fluids
Reassess airway and ventilatory needs
disposition: Admit to ICU or transfer to facility with critical care/ICU
Arrange ALS transport with monitoring and pressor capability
FSED rarely an endpoint; expedite stabilization then transfer
Continuous physician-to-physician handoff
discharge: Discharge not appropriate for true sepsis
If SIRS from non-infectious benign cause resolved, discharge with precautions*
Document rationale clearly if not transferred*
followup: Receiving ICU/admitting team for ongoing care
Culture and sensitivity follow-up by accepting facility
PCP after hospitalization for recovery and source clearance

redFlags: Hypotension refractory to fluids (septic shock) / pressor requirement
Lactate >=4 mmol/L or worsening lactate clearance
Altered mental status, new oliguria, or hypoxemic respiratory failure
DIC, thrombocytopenia, or coagulopathy
Rapidly progressing soft-tissue infection (necrotizing) requiring emergent surgery
compliance: EMTALA: perform MSE and begin stabilization (antibiotics, fluids, pressors) before transfer
Appropriate-transfer criteria: stabilize within FSED capability, secure accepting ICU bed and physician, send records
Observation vs transfer: sepsis requires admission/ICU not available at FSED, so transfer is standard
Report notifiable infectious causes (e.g., meningococcemia) to Texas DSHS as required

name: Hyperglycemia / DKA
summary: Severe hyperglycemia with ketoacidosis (DKA) or hyperosmolar state (HHS); fluid, electrolyte, and insulin management.

triage: Capillary glucose and vitals immediately
Assess hydration, mentation, Kussmaul respirations, fruity breath
Identify trigger (infection, missed insulin, MI)
ESI 1-2 if altered, hypotensive, or glucose critically high
nursing: Two IV lines; begin fluid resuscitation
Continuous cardiac monitor (potassium shifts)
Strict hourly I/O and serial glucose checks
Foley if obtunded or shock for output monitoring*
keyOrders: IV access x2; cardiac monitor; O2 if hypoxic
Hold insulin until K >=3.3 mEq/L confirmed
labs: CBC, serum osmolality, anion gap calculation
imaging: Chest X-ray if infection suspected as trigger*
ECG counts as key cardiac evaluation
Imaging directed at precipitating source* if indicated
meds: Isotonic crystalloid: NS 15-20 mL/kg (~1-1.5 L) first hour, then adjust to corrected Na
Regular insulin infusion 0.1 units/kg/h IV (start after K >=3.3); avoid bolus or use 0.1 u/kg bolus per protocol
Potassium replacement 20-30 mEq/L IV when K 3.3-5.2 mEq/L
Add dextrose (D5) to fluids when glucose ~200 mg/dL; treat precipitant
reassess: Recheck glucose hourly; electrolytes/VBG q2-4h
Monitor for hypokalemia as insulin drives K intracellular
Track anion gap closure and mental status
Reassess fluid status and urine output
disposition: DKA/HHS require admission to ICU/step-down or transfer
Arrange monitored ALS transfer with insulin infusion if FSED
Stabilize gap and hemodynamics before transport
Isolated mild hyperglycemia without acidosis may discharge after correction*
discharge: Only for mild hyperglycemia without ketoacidosis once stable*
Insulin/oral hypoglycemic adjustment and adherence counseling
Sick-day rules and glucose monitoring instructions
Return for vomiting, ketones, AMS, or persistent high sugars
followup: Endocrinology/PCP within 1-3 days for diabetes management
Diabetes education referral*
Admitting team continues DKA/HHS resolution if transferred

redFlags: Altered mental status or coma (severe DKA/HHS)
Hemodynamic instability or shock
Severe hypokalemia (<3.3) or hyperkalemia with ECG changes
pH <7.0, severe anion-gap acidosis, or refractory acidosis
Cerebral edema (esp. pediatric) or precipitating MI/sepsis
compliance: EMTALA MSE; begin fluids/insulin/electrolyte stabilization before transfer
Appropriate transfer: secure ICU/admitting bed, accepting physician, monitored transport for insulin drip
Observation vs transfer: true DKA/HHS exceeds FSED capability and warrants transfer to hospital with ICU
Document stabilization and risk/benefit of transfer per EMTALA

name: Hypoglycemia
summary: Low serum glucose causing neuroglycopenic/adrenergic symptoms; rapid correction and identify cause.

triage: Immediate capillary glucose and vitals
Assess mental status and ability to protect airway
Ask insulin/sulfonylurea use, intake, alcohol
ESI 2 if altered or unable to take PO
nursing: IV access if AMS or unable to take oral glucose
Continuous monitoring until glucose normalized
Serial glucose checks q15-30 min after treatment
Provide complex carb meal once alert*
keyOrders: Capillary glucose immediately and serial rechecks
Cardiac monitor if AMS or comorbid cardiac disease*
labs: Consider C-peptide, insulin, sulfonylurea screen if unexplained*
imaging: Usually none
CT head if persistent AMS after glucose correction* if indicated
meds: Oral glucose 15-20 g if alert and able to swallow
Dextrose 50% (D50) 25 g IV if unable to take PO/AMS
Glucagon 1 mg IM/SC if no IV access
Octreotide 50-100 mcg SC for refractory sulfonylurea-induced hypoglycemia*
reassess: Recheck glucose 15 min after treatment; repeat dextrose if still low
Reassess mental status returns to baseline
Ensure sustained euglycemia with a meal before discharge
Watch for rebound, especially long-acting agents
disposition: Discharge if short-acting cause corrected, baseline mentation, tolerating PO
Observe/admit for sulfonylurea or long-acting insulin (recurrence risk)
Transfer if requires prolonged dextrose infusion/admission
Admit if no clear reversible cause or recurrent episodes
discharge: Identify and correct trigger (missed meal, dose error)
Counsel on recognizing/treating lows; prescribe glucagon kit*
Eat a meal before leaving; adjust diabetes regimen with provider
Return for recurrence, confusion, or inability to eat
followup: PCP/endocrinology for regimen adjustment within days
Diabetes education referral*
Sulfonylurea/long-acting cases need admission, not outpatient follow-up alone

redFlags: Persistent altered mental status despite glucose normalization
Sulfonylurea or long-acting insulin overdose (prolonged/recurrent hypoglycemia)
Seizure or focal deficit
Hypoglycemia without diabetes (occult sepsis, adrenal insufficiency, insulinoma)
Intentional overdose / suicidal ideation
compliance: EMTALA MSE; correct glucose and confirm stability before disposition
Observation vs transfer: sulfonylurea/long-acting insulin require admission for monitored observation, transfer if FSED cannot admit
Stabilize before transfer with dextrose infusion as needed
Report suspected intentional overdose for psychiatric evaluation; mandatory safety/abuse reporting if applicable

name: Electrolyte Abnormality
summary: Derangement of Na, K, Ca, or Mg; acuity driven by severity, rate of change, and cardiac/neuro effects.

triage: Vitals and capillary glucose
Identify symptoms: weakness, palpitations, confusion, seizures
Ask diuretics, renal disease, recent fluids, GI losses
ESI 1-2 if arrhythmia, severe hyperkalemia, or seizure risk
nursing: Cardiac monitor for potassium/calcium abnormalities
IV access
Strict I/O; seizure precautions for severe hyponatremia*
Serial neuro checks for sodium disorders*
labs: Serum and urine osmolality + urine Na for hyponatremia workup*
Digoxin level if on digoxin with hyperkalemia*
imaging: Usually none
Chest X-ray or POCUS for volume status in hyponatremia*
Imaging for underlying cause as indicated*
meds: Hyperkalemia: calcium gluconate 1-2 g IV (membrane stabilization), insulin 10 u IV + D50 25 g, albuterol 10-20 mg neb, +/- furosemide
Hypokalemia: KCl 10-20 mEq/h IV (monitored) or 40-60 mEq PO; replace Mg
Symptomatic severe hyponatremia: 3% hypertonic saline 100-150 mL IV bolus, correct <8-10 mEq/L/24h
Hypocalcemia: calcium gluconate IV; hypomagnesemia: magnesium sulfate 1-2 g IV*
reassess: Repeat ECG and electrolytes after treatment
Monitor sodium correction rate to avoid osmotic demyelination
Reassess cardiac rhythm and neuro status
Trend potassium for rebound
disposition: Discharge only for mild, asymptomatic, corrected abnormalities with clear cause
Admit/transfer for severe or symptomatic derangements
Transfer for hyperkalemia needing dialysis or ICU monitoring
Observe with serial labs if borderline*
discharge: Address cause (diet, medication adjustment)
Oral repletion instructions if mild*
Return for palpitations, weakness, confusion, or seizures
Recheck labs with provider per plan
followup: PCP/nephrology with repeat labs in 1-3 days
Medication reconciliation (diuretics, ACE/ARB, K supplements)
Endocrine referral for recurrent calcium/sodium disorders*

redFlags: ECG changes of hyperkalemia (peaked T, widened QRS, sine wave)
Severe symptomatic hyponatremia (seizure, coma)
Hyperkalemia requiring emergent dialysis
Hypocalcemia with tetany, laryngospasm, or QT prolongation
Any electrolyte-induced arrhythmia or hemodynamic instability
compliance: EMTALA MSE; stabilize cardiac-threatening abnormalities before transfer
Appropriate transfer for dialysis-requiring hyperkalemia or ICU-level monitoring
Observation vs transfer: severe/symptomatic cases transfer to acute hospital; document stabilizing treatment
Avoid over-rapid sodium correction; document neuro monitoring per standard of care

name: Influenza-like Illness
summary: Acute febrile respiratory illness (fever, cough, myalgias); identify influenza vs other viral/bacterial cause and complications.

triage: Vitals incl. SpO2 and temperature
Symptom onset, exposures, vaccination status, comorbidities
Mask/respiratory isolation per protocol
ESI 2 if hypoxic, dyspneic, or high-risk decompensation
nursing: Place in respiratory isolation/droplet precautions
Antipyretic per protocol
Encourage oral hydration; IV if dehydrated*
Continuous SpO2 if hypoxic*
keyOrders: IV access if dehydrated or ill-appearing*
labs: Often none for low-risk well-appearing patients
CBC, BMP if dehydrated or high-risk*
Chest imaging-driven labs if pneumonia suspected*
imaging: Chest X-ray if hypoxia, focal exam, or pneumonia concern*
Generally not needed for uncomplicated ILI
Further imaging only for complications* if indicated
meds: Oseltamivir 75 mg PO BID x5 days if influenza + within 48 h or high-risk/severe
Acetaminophen 650-1000 mg PO/IV q6h for fever/myalgia
Ibuprofen 400-600 mg PO q6h if no contraindication*
IV fluids and antiemetic if dehydrated*
reassess: Recheck SpO2 and work of breathing
Reassess hydration and ability to take PO
Confirm no signs of pneumonia/secondary bacterial infection
Reassess high-risk patients for deterioration
disposition: Discharge if well-appearing, normoxic, tolerating PO
Admit/transfer for hypoxia, pneumonia, or decompensated comorbidity
Observe high-risk borderline patients*
Transfer if requires oxygen/inpatient respiratory support
discharge: Supportive care: rest, fluids, antipyretics
Antiviral course instructions if prescribed*
Return for dyspnea, chest pain, persistent high fever, or dehydration
Isolation/return-to-work guidance and droplet precautions at home
followup: PCP if not improving in 3-5 days or symptoms worsen
Public health reporting of influenza per surveillance*
High-risk patients close follow-up*

redFlags: Hypoxia (SpO2 <94%) or respiratory distress
Signs of pneumonia or secondary bacterial infection
Dehydration with inability to tolerate oral intake
Altered mental status or hemodynamic instability
High-risk host (pregnancy, immunocompromised, severe COPD/CHF) decompensating
compliance: EMTALA MSE to exclude pneumonia/sepsis/hypoxia before discharge
Maintain droplet/respiratory isolation per Texas FSED infection control
Observation vs transfer: hypoxic or complicated cases transfer to facility with inpatient/oxygen capability
Report influenza outbreaks/novel strains to Texas DSHS per public health rules

name: COVID-19
summary: SARS-CoV-2 infection ranging from mild URI to hypoxemic respiratory failure and thromboembolic complications.

triage: Vitals incl. SpO2 (resting and ambulatory if borderline)
Symptom onset, vaccination, risk factors, comorbidities
Place in isolation with appropriate PPE
ESI 2 if hypoxic, dyspneic, or high-risk
nursing: Airborne/droplet isolation per protocol
Continuous SpO2; supplemental O2 to keep SpO2 >=94%
Antipyretics per protocol
IV access if ill-appearing or needs fluids*
keyOrders: Supplemental O2 if hypoxic; cardiac monitor if unstable*
labs: CBC, CMP, CRP, D-dimer if moderate-severe*
imaging: Chest X-ray if hypoxic, dyspneic, or moderate-severe*
CT pulmonary angiogram if PE suspected (elevated D-dimer, sudden hypoxia)* if indicated
Generally none for mild disease
meds: Supportive: acetaminophen 650-1000 mg PO/IV q6h for fever
Supplemental oxygen titrated to SpO2 >=94%
Nirmatrelvir-ritonavir (Paxlovid) 300/100 mg PO BID x5d if high-risk, early, eligible*
Dexamethasone 6 mg IV/PO daily if hypoxic requiring oxygen; consider remdesivir/anticoagulation per severity*
reassess: Recheck resting and ambulatory SpO2 and work of breathing
Reassess for clinical deterioration / silent hypoxia
Reassess hydration and PO tolerance
Evaluate for thromboembolic signs if sudden change
disposition: Discharge if normoxic, well-appearing, reliable
Admit/transfer for hypoxia, respiratory distress, or PE
Observe borderline ambulatory desaturation*
Transfer to facility with oxygen/inpatient/ICU capacity if needed
discharge: Home isolation guidance and symptom monitoring
Pulse oximeter use and return-if-desaturating instructions
Antiviral course adherence if prescribed*
Return for dyspnea, chest pain, SpO2 <94%, confusion, or dehydration
followup: PCP follow-up; telehealth monitoring for high-risk*
Public health reporting of COVID-19 per Texas DSHS
Pulmonary follow-up for severe/post-COVID symptoms*

redFlags: Hypoxia or silent desaturation (resting/ambulatory SpO2 <94%)
Respiratory distress or progressive dyspnea
Suspected pulmonary embolism (sudden hypoxia, pleuritic chest pain)
Hemodynamic instability or signs of MIS/cytokine storm
High-risk host with rapid clinical deterioration
compliance: EMTALA MSE; assess oxygenation before discharge
Maintain airborne/droplet isolation and PPE per FSED infection control
Observation vs transfer: hypoxic/severe cases transfer to hospital with oxygen and inpatient/ICU capability
Mandatory COVID-19 case reporting to Texas DSHS

name: Foreign Body Ingestion
summary: Ingested object; risk stratify by type (button battery, magnets, sharp, food bolus), location, and airway compromise.

triage: Vitals and airway assessment; assess drooling/inability to handle secretions
Identify object type, size, time, and symptoms
Flag button battery, magnets, sharp objects, or airway compromise as high acuity
ESI 1-2 if airway compromise or high-risk object
nursing: NPO
Upright positioning; suction available for drooling
Continuous SpO2; IV access if intervention likely*
Monitor secretions and respiratory status
keyOrders: 12-lead ECG/cardiac monitor if procedural sedation planned*
Emergent GI/ENT consult for high-risk objects or obstruction
labs: CBC, BMP, type and screen if surgical/perforation concern*
Coags if anticoagulated and procedure likely*
imaging: X-rays (neck/chest/abdomen) to localize radiopaque object
Identify button battery 'double-ring' / magnet number on imaging
CT if perforation, migration, or non-radiopaque object suspected* if indicated
meds: Honey 10 mL PO for button battery if <12 h and >=12 mo old, en route to emergent endoscopy*
Glucagon 1-2 mg IV for esophageal food bolus (limited evidence)*
Analgesia/antiemetic as needed (e.g., ondansetron 4 mg IV)*
Avoid blind catheter/bougie; avoid inducing vomiting
reassess: Reassess airway, drooling, and respiratory status frequently
Reassess pain and any signs of perforation
Confirm imaging localization guides urgency
Reassess after spontaneous passage of low-risk object*
disposition: Discharge low-risk blunt distal-esophageal/gastric object that is asymptomatic
Emergent transfer for button battery in esophagus, magnets, sharp object, or obstruction
Transfer if GI/ENT endoscopy or surgery unavailable at FSED
Observe pending passage of low-risk objects*
discharge: Diet advancement once tolerating; stool watch for object passage
Return for chest/abdominal pain, vomiting, fever, melena, or dysphagia
Strict return for any new symptoms suggesting impaction/perforation
Avoid additional ingestions; pediatric safe-storage counseling*
followup: GI follow-up for retained or high-risk objects
Repeat imaging to confirm passage if indicated*
PCP follow-up for symptom resolution

redFlags: Button battery lodged in esophagus (caustic injury - emergent removal)
Multiple magnets (bowel necrosis/perforation risk)
Sharp or long object, or signs of perforation (fever, chest/abdominal pain, crepitus)
Airway compromise, drooling, inability to handle secretions
Signs of obstruction, mediastinitis, or GI bleeding
compliance: EMTALA MSE to exclude airway/esophageal emergency before disposition
Emergent appropriate transfer to facility with GI/ENT/surgical endoscopy for high-risk objects
Observation vs transfer: low-risk passable objects may be observed/discharged; high-risk requires transfer
Report suspected child abuse/neglect or intentional ingestion (self-harm) per Texas mandatory reporting

name: Lymphadenopathy
summary: Enlarged lymph nodes; differentiate benign reactive/infectious from suppurative, malignant, or systemic causes.

triage: Vitals incl. temperature
Localized vs generalized; assess for airway compromise from neck mass
Ask duration, B symptoms (fever, night sweats, weight loss), exposures
ESI 2 if airway compromise, sepsis, or toxic appearance
nursing: IV access if systemic illness or abscess drainage planned*
Antipyretic/analgesia per protocol
Monitor airway if large neck mass*
Warm compresses for suppurative node*
keyOrders: Focused exam documentation (size, mobility, tenderness, location)
IV access if ill-appearing or procedure planned*
Consult ENT/surgery for fluctuant abscess or airway threat*
labs: Monospot/EBV, CMV, HIV, RPR per exposure history*
imaging: Soft-tissue ultrasound of node/region (abscess vs solid)
CT neck/chest with contrast for deep/large mass or airway concern* if indicated
Chest X-ray for hilar adenopathy/systemic concern*
meds: Antibiotics for suppurative lymphadenitis (e.g., cephalexin 500 mg PO QID or clindamycin 300-450 mg PO QID covering MRSA if abscess)*
Acetaminophen 650-1000 mg PO q6h / ibuprofen 400-600 mg PO q6h for pain
IV antibiotics (e.g., vancomycin + ceftriaxone) if systemic/deep infection*
Incision and drainage analgesia/local anesthetic if abscess*
reassess: Reassess airway if neck mass and any compromise
Reassess for systemic toxicity / sepsis
Review labs/imaging for abscess vs malignancy red flags
Confirm follow-up plan for persistent nodes
disposition: Discharge benign reactive or drained simple abscess if stable
Admit/transfer for deep space infection, sepsis, or airway threat
Transfer if surgical drainage/ENT unavailable at FSED
Expedited outpatient workup for suspected malignancy*
discharge: Antibiotic course and wound care if drained*
Return for enlarging mass, fever, difficulty breathing/swallowing
Counsel that persistent/hard/fixed nodes need workup
Avoid manipulation; warm compresses if reactive*
followup: PCP in 2-4 weeks to reassess persistent nodes
Hematology-oncology referral if B symptoms or suspicious features*
ENT/surgery follow-up for excisional biopsy if persistent*

redFlags: Airway compromise from enlarging neck mass
Signs of deep neck space infection or sepsis
Hard, fixed, matted, or rapidly enlarging nodes (malignancy)
B symptoms (fever, drenching night sweats, weight loss)
Supraclavicular adenopathy (high malignancy risk)
compliance: EMTALA MSE to exclude airway/deep-space infection emergency
Stabilize airway and begin antibiotics before transfer for deep neck infection
Observation vs transfer: suppurative deep infections or airway threats transfer to facility with ENT/surgery
Report reportable infectious causes (e.g., TB) to Texas DSHS per public health rules

name: Generalized Body Pain / Myalgia
summary: Diffuse muscle/body pain; differentiate benign viral myalgia from rhabdomyolysis, sepsis, or serious systemic disease.

triage: Vitals incl. temperature and SpO2
Ask fever, recent exertion, dark urine, statin use, trauma, toxins
Screen for infectious/sepsis features and focal neuro signs
ESI 2 if toxic, hypotensive, or rhabdomyolysis suspected
nursing: IV access if dehydrated or rhabdomyolysis suspected*
Antipyretic/analgesia per protocol
Strict I/O if rhabdo concern*
Continuous vitals if ill-appearing*
labs: Urinalysis (blood positive, no RBCs suggests myoglobin)
Lactate, blood cultures if septic appearance*
TSH, ESR/CRP for systemic/inflammatory causes* if indicated
imaging: Usually none
Chest X-ray if respiratory infection suspected*
Targeted imaging if focal pathology (e.g., necrotizing infection) suspected* if indicated
meds: Acetaminophen 650-1000 mg PO/IV q6h
Ibuprofen 400-600 mg PO q6h if no renal/GI contraindication*
IV crystalloid for dehydration or rhabdomyolysis (aggressive NS for myoglobinuria)*
Treat underlying infection/cause specifically*
reassess: Reassess vitals and pain control
Review CK/renal function if rhabdomyolysis suspected
Reassess for evolving sepsis or neuro findings
Confirm hydration and ability to ambulate
disposition: Discharge if benign viral/musculoskeletal cause and stable
Admit/transfer for significant rhabdomyolysis, AKI, or sepsis
Transfer if requires IV hydration/monitoring beyond FSED capability
Observe borderline CK or hydration cases*
discharge: Supportive care: rest, hydration, analgesics
Return for dark urine, decreased urination, weakness, fever, or worsening pain
Activity modification and follow-up guidance
Stop offending agent (e.g., statin) pending evaluation*
followup: PCP if persistent or unexplained pain
Rheumatology referral for suspected inflammatory myopathy*
Recheck CK/renal function if borderline rhabdomyolysis*

redFlags: Rhabdomyolysis with markedly elevated CK or acute kidney injury
Dark/tea-colored urine (myoglobinuria)
Fever with toxic appearance or sepsis features
Focal weakness or progressive neuromuscular deficit
Severe localized pain out of proportion (necrotizing infection/compartment syndrome)
compliance: EMTALA MSE to exclude rhabdomyolysis, sepsis, or serious systemic cause before discharge
Stabilize with IV hydration and begin treatment before any transfer
Observation vs transfer: significant rhabdomyolysis or AKI requires transfer to facility with admission/dialysis capability
Report suspected abuse or assault if injury-related myalgia points to interpersonal violence per Texas reporting

name: Hematuria
summary: Gross or microscopic blood in urine; differentiate UTI, stone, trauma, malignancy, and clot retention from pseudohematuria.

triage: Vital signs; assess for hemodynamic instability and clot retention
Gross vs microscopic; timing in stream; presence of clots
Pain (flank, suprapubic), trauma, anticoagulation, recent instrumentation
Smoking history and age (malignancy risk stratification)
nursing: Urine collection for UA; note color and clots
IV access if gross hematuria with clots or instability
Bladder scan; prepare for catheter/irrigation if retention*
Monitor vitals and urine output
keyOrders: IV access for significant gross hematuria
Three-way Foley with continuous bladder irrigation if clot retention* if indicated
Type and screen if transfusion possible* if indicated
labs: Urinalysis with microscopy; urine culture if infection suspected
Urine cytology not done in ED (outpatient)*
imaging: CT urogram (CT with/without contrast) for evaluation of source in gross painless hematuria* if indicated
Non-contrast CT if stone suspected* if indicated
Renal/bladder ultrasound as alternative if contrast contraindicated* if indicated
FAST/CT for trauma-related hematuria* if indicated
meds: Analgesia: ketorolac 15-30 mg IV or acetaminophen 1 g IV if pain present
Reverse/hold anticoagulation per agent if bleeding significant* if indicated
Antibiotics if concurrent UTI per cystitis regimen* if indicated
IV crystalloid for volume support if anemic/unstable* if indicated
Tranexamic acid only on urology guidance* if indicated
reassess: Reassess hemodynamics and serial hemoglobin if significant bleeding
Confirm clearing of irrigation and resolution of clot retention
Re-evaluate pain control
Check post-void residual after catheter removal*
disposition: Discharge: stable microscopic or minor gross hematuria without retention/anemia
Observation: gross hematuria requiring irrigation, monitoring of hemoglobin
Admit/transfer: hemodynamic instability, ongoing transfusion need, clot retention not cleared, trauma
Urgent urology consult/transfer for significant gross hematuria
discharge: Strict return precautions for inability to void, large clots, or dizziness
Maintain hydration; avoid strenuous activity if exertional hematuria
Bring medication list including anticoagulants to follow-up
Emphasize importance of outpatient hematuria workup even if resolved
followup: Urology referral for gross hematuria and for microscopic hematuria with risk factors
Primary care for blood pressure, renal function, anticoagulation management
Outpatient cystoscopy and CT urogram if not completed
Nephrology if glomerular pattern (dysmorphic RBCs, proteinuria, casts)

redFlags: Hemodynamic instability or symptomatic anemia from urinary blood loss
Clot retention with inability to void (urologic emergency)
Gross hematuria after significant trauma (possible renal/bladder injury)
Painless gross hematuria in patient >35 or smoker (malignancy concern)
Hematuria with acute kidney injury or glomerulonephritis pattern
compliance: EMTALA screening and stabilization; transfusion/irrigation stabilization documented before transfer
Appropriate-transfer to urology-capable facility for clot retention or trauma needing operative care
Observation acceptable for irrigation and serial hemoglobin; transfer if uncontrolled

name: Urinary Retention
summary: Acute inability to void with bladder distension; identify obstructive (BPH, clot, stricture), neurogenic, and medication causes; exclude cauda equina.

Duration of inability to void, prior episodes, BPH history
Neuro symptoms: saddle anesthesia, leg weakness, back pain (cauda equina)
Medication review (anticholinergics, opioids, decongestants)

Immediate urethral catheterization for decompression
Measure and document drained volume
Monitor for post-obstructive diuresis
keyOrders: Foley catheter placement (leave to gravity drainage)
Record drained urine volume and characteristics
Monitor urine output hourly after decompression*
labs: BMP for renal function and electrolytes
PSA not drawn acutely in ED (outpatient)*
imaging: Bladder ultrasound to confirm retention
Renal ultrasound if hydronephrosis or AKI suspected* if indicated
MRI lumbar spine emergently if cauda equina suspected* if indicated
CT for pelvic mass or stone if suspected* if indicated

Analgesia: acetaminophen 1 g PO/IV for catheter discomfort
Antibiotics only if concurrent UTI per regimen* if indicated
Stop offending medications (anticholinergics/opioids) when feasible
IV fluids to monitor and replace post-obstructive diuresis if large output*
reassess: Reassess comfort after decompression
Monitor for post-obstructive diuresis (>200 mL/h) and replace fluids
Re-evaluate renal function if elevated
Assess neuro exam if cauda equina was on differential
disposition: Discharge: successful decompression with leg bag and outpatient follow-up (trial of void planned)
Observation: significant post-obstructive diuresis or AKI requiring monitoring
Admit/transfer: cauda equina, sepsis, severe AKI, hematuria with clot retention
Emergent transfer for suspected cauda equina to facility with neurosurgery/MRI
discharge: Leg bag care and catheter hygiene instructions
Tamsulosin counseling (orthostatic hypotension)
Return for no urine output, fever, severe pain, or catheter dislodgement
Trial-of-void plan and timing communicated
followup: Urology within 3-7 days for trial of void and catheter removal
Primary care for medication review and BPH management
Neurosurgery/spine if neurogenic cause identified
Document expected post-obstructive diuresis monitoring needs

redFlags: Cauda equina syndrome: saddle anesthesia, bilateral leg weakness, back pain - surgical emergency
Acute kidney injury or significant post-obstructive diuresis
Urosepsis with retention
Inability to pass catheter (possible stricture/false passage) requiring urology
Gross hematuria with clot retention
compliance: EMTALA screening and stabilization; decompress bladder as part of stabilization before transfer

Observation acceptable for post-obstructive diuresis monitoring; transfer if severe AKI/sepsis

FSED must arrange urology follow-up or transfer if catheter cannot be placed on site

name: Testicular Pain
summary: Acute testicular pain - time-critical exclusion of testicular torsion versus epididymitis, torsed appendage, and referred pain.

triage: IMMEDIATE physician notification for sudden severe pain (possible torsion)
Onset/duration (torsion typically <6h, abrupt), nausea/vomiting
Prior episodes, trauma, recent activity, sexual history
Vital signs; note that torsion is a time-dependent emergency
nursing: Expedite to physician; do not delay for labs
IV access and analgesia/antiemetic
Prepare for emergent scrotal ultrasound
Keep patient NPO in case of operative intervention
keyOrders: Immediate scrotal Doppler ultrasound (do not delay surgical consult if high suspicion)
Urgent urology consultation if torsion suspected
labs: Urine NAAT gonorrhea/chlamydia if epididymitis suspected* if indicated
CBC if systemic infection* if indicated
imaging: Color Doppler scrotal ultrasound (decreased/absent flow suggests torsion)
Do NOT delay surgical exploration for imaging if clinical suspicion high
Consider repeat/dynamic ultrasound if intermittent torsion suspected* if indicated
meds: Morphine 0.1 mg/kg IV or hydromorphone 0.5-1 mg IV for pain
Ondansetron 4 mg IV for nausea
Epididymitis (STI likely): ceftriaxone 500 mg IM x1 plus doxycycline 100 mg PO BID x10d
Epididymitis (enteric/older): levofloxacin 500 mg PO daily x10d
Manual detorsion by physician as temporizing measure if surgery delayed*
reassess: Continuous reassessment of pain and exam while awaiting urology
Document time of symptom onset and time of evaluation (testicular viability window)
Reassess after manual detorsion attempt (pain relief, restored lie)*
Confirm antibiotic response if epididymitis
disposition: Emergent OR/urology: confirmed or suspected torsion (surgical detorsion within 6h goal)
Discharge: epididymitis with controlled pain on antibiotics and reliable follow-up
Observation: equivocal cases pending urology re-evaluation
Emergent transfer to surgical/urology-capable facility if none on site
discharge: Epididymitis: complete antibiotics, scrotal support, NSAIDs, ice
Return immediately for worsening pain, swelling, or fever
STI partner treatment and abstinence counseling if applicable
Strict torsion return precautions if diagnosis uncertain
followup: Urology follow-up for all epididymitis and post-torsion patients
STI reporting and partner notification if gonorrhea/chlamydia
Primary care follow-up in 1-2 weeks
Re-evaluate if symptoms not improving in 48-72h

redFlags: Testicular torsion - sudden severe pain, high-riding testis, absent cremasteric reflex, absent Doppler flow
Time since onset approaching/exceeding 6 hours (viability threatened)
Signs of Fournier gangrene (necrotizing infection, crepitus, systemic toxicity)
Failed manual detorsion or recurrent torsion
Pediatric difference: torsion peaks in neonates and adolescents - treat as torsion until excluded
compliance: EMTALA screening with emergent surgical evaluation; torsion is a true emergency requiring rapid stabilization/transfer
If no on-site urology/OR, initiate immediate appropriate-transfer with accepting surgeon - do not delay for full workup
Document onset time, exam, ultrasound, and detorsion attempts for medicolegal/transfer record
Report confirmed gonorrhea/chlamydia to Texas DSHS
If sexual assault suspected, follow Texas mandatory reporting and preserve evidence

name: Scrotal Swelling

triage: Vital signs; flag fever, toxicity, or skin necrosis (Fournier)
Onset, pain, trauma, fever, reducibility (hernia)

Assess for associated severe pain suggesting torsion
nursing: Expedite exam if painful/toxic-appearing
IV access if systemic illness or surgical concern
Prepare for scrotal ultrasound
Mark erythema borders and monitor for spread*
keyOrders: IV access if febrile/toxic or surgical concern
Surgical/urology consult if torsion, hernia, or necrotizing infection suspected
labs: BMP and lactate if systemic illness/Fournier* if indicated
Blood cultures if septic* if indicated
CRP/procalcitonin if necrotizing infection suspected* if indicated
imaging: Color Doppler scrotal ultrasound (flow, mass, fluid, hernia contents)
CT/ultrasound for soft tissue gas if Fournier gangrene suspected* if indicated
Inguinal/abdominal imaging if incarcerated hernia* if indicated

Broad-spectrum antibiotics for Fournier: piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g IV plus vancomycin 15-20 mg/kg IV plus clindamycin 900 mg IV*
Antibiotics for infectious epididymo-orchitis per regimen* if indicated
IV crystalloid resuscitation if septic* if indicated
Antiemetic ondansetron 4 mg IV if needed
reassess: Serial exams for spreading erythema/crepitus (necrotizing infection)
Reassess hemodynamics and sepsis criteria
Re-evaluate reducibility/viability if hernia
Confirm pain control and ultrasound findings reviewed
disposition: Emergent OR/transfer: Fournier gangrene, torsion, strangulated hernia
Discharge: stable benign cause (hydrocele, small varicocele) with urology follow-up
Observation: epididymo-orchitis needing IV antibiotics/monitoring
Admit/transfer for surgical management or sepsis
discharge: Scrotal support, ice, NSAIDs for benign causes
Return immediately for worsening pain, fever, spreading redness, or skin changes
Antibiotic instructions if infection treated
Emphasize urology evaluation of any solid mass (tumor concern)
followup: Urology referral for masses, varicocele, persistent swelling
Primary care follow-up for benign self-limited causes
Surgical follow-up after hernia or Fournier management
STI reporting/partner treatment if applicable

redFlags: Fournier gangrene - rapidly spreading necrosis, crepitus, pain out of proportion, sepsis (surgical emergency)

Systemic toxicity or septic shock

name: Acute Kidney Injury
summary: Acute decline in renal function; categorize prerenal, intrinsic, and postrenal causes; identify life-threatening hyperkalemia, acidosis, and volume overload.

triage: Vital signs; assess volume status and mental status
Urine output, oliguria/anuria, recent illness, vomiting/diarrhea
Medication/nephrotoxin review (NSAIDs, ACEi/ARB, contrast, antibiotics)
Screen for hyperkalemia symptoms and obtain early ECG

IV access; strict intake/output and daily weight

Urine electrolytes for FENa/FEUrea* if indicated
CK if rhabdomyolysis suspected; consider lactate* if indicated
imaging: Renal/bladder ultrasound for hydronephrosis and kidney size
Chest X-ray if volume overload/pulmonary edema* if indicated
CT only if obstruction or stone needs delineation (mind contrast)* if indicated

Hyperkalemia: insulin 10 units IV with dextrose 25 g IV, plus albuterol 10-20 mg nebulized
IV isotonic crystalloid for prerenal/hypovolemic AKI (avoid if overloaded)
Furosemide 40-80 mg IV for volume overload (not to treat AKI itself)*
Sodium bicarbonate for severe acidosis per indication* if indicated
Stop nephrotoxins; renally dose all medications
reassess: Recheck potassium and repeat ECG after hyperkalemia treatment
Reassess volume status, urine output, and respiratory status
Trend creatinine and acid-base
Determine need for emergent dialysis
disposition: Admit/transfer: hyperkalemia refractory to therapy, severe acidosis, volume overload, uremia, oliguria/anuria
Observation: mild prerenal AKI responding to fluids with reliable follow-up
Discharge: mild AKI with clear reversible cause, corrected labs, and prompt follow-up
Emergent transfer to facility with dialysis/nephrology and ICU capability
discharge: Stop offending nephrotoxins; avoid NSAIDs
Maintain hydration as advised; clear sick-day medication guidance
Return for decreased urination, swelling, shortness of breath, or confusion
Provide list of medications to hold and recheck timing

name: Low Back Pain

CBC, ESR, CRP if infection/abscess suspected*  if indicated
Blood cultures if febrile/septic*  if indicated

Transfer with continuous monitoring and IV access
followup: Operative fasciotomy and wound management at receiving facility
Renal monitoring for rhabdomyolysis-induced AKI
Orthopedic follow-up post-decompression

redFlags: Pain out of proportion and pain with passive stretch (earliest signs)
Tense, firm compartment on palpation
Paresthesia / sensory deficit progressing to paralysis
Compartment pressure >30 mmHg or delta pressure <30 mmHg
Pulselessness/pallor (late, ominous findings)
compliance: EMTALA: stabilize (relieve constriction, fluids, analgesia) and emergent appropriate transfer to surgical facility - limb-threatening emergency
FSED lacks fasciotomy capability - document time-critical necessity and risk/benefit of transfer
Document serial exams, pressures, and times; use highest-level transport
Report suspected abuse if crush mechanism inconsistent with history (Texas mandatory reporting)

name: Septic Joint
summary: Bacterial infection of a joint space (commonly S. aureus, gonococcal); joint-threatening emergency requiring urgent arthrocentesis, antibiotics, and surgical drainage.

triage: Assess hot, swollen, painful joint with restricted ROM
Vital signs incl. temperature; SIRS/sepsis screen
Risk factors: prosthetic joint, IVDU, immunosuppression, recent procedure
Pain score; immobilize joint
nursing: IV access; draw blood cultures before antibiotics
Immobilize and support affected joint
Prepare sterile arthrocentesis tray
Sepsis bundle if septic physiology (fluids, monitoring)
keyOrders: Arthrocentesis for synovial fluid analysis (before antibiotics if no delay)
IV access, IV fluids; cardiac monitor if septic
labs: Synovial fluid: cell count w/ diff, Gram stain, culture, crystals
Procalcitonin*  if indicated; GC/chlamydia NAAT if gonococcal suspected*  if indicated
imaging: Plain radiograph of affected joint
Ultrasound to confirm effusion and guide aspiration*  if indicated
MRI if osteomyelitis or prosthetic/deep joint involvement*  if indicated
meds: Vancomycin 15-20 mg/kg IV (MRSA coverage)
Plus ceftriaxone 2 g IV (or cefepime if GNR/immunocompromised)
Ceftriaxone 1 g IV if gonococcal arthritis suspected
IV crystalloid 30 mL/kg if septic per Surviving Sepsis
Analgesia: acetaminophen 1000 mg PO; opioids for severe pain*  if indicated
reassess: Trend vitals and lactate; sepsis reassessment
Review synovial fluid Gram stain/cell count
Reassess pain and perfusion before/while arranging transfer
disposition: Admit/transfer to facility with orthopedics for surgical drainage/washout
Initiate sepsis management and antibiotics before transfer
ICU-level transfer if septic shock
discharge: Discharge inappropriate; requires admission and operative drainage
Communicate cultures, synovial results, and antibiotics given to receiving team
Transfer with IV access and monitoring
followup: Inpatient orthopedic surgical drainage and IV antibiotics
Infectious disease consultation for tailored therapy
Source/STI partner follow-up if gonococcal

redFlags: Hot swollen joint with fever and inability to bear weight/move joint
Synovial WBC >50,000 or positive Gram stain
Prosthetic joint infection
Sepsis/septic shock physiology (hypotension, tachycardia, elevated lactate)
Rapidly progressive joint destruction
compliance: EMTALA: provide MSE, stabilize (antibiotics, fluids, sepsis bundle), then appropriate transfer to facility with orthopedic surgery
FSED lacks operative washout capability - document medical necessity and risk/benefit of transfer/admission
Initiate Surviving Sepsis bundle measures if septic; send cultures and meds-given with patient
Gonococcal arthritis is a reportable STI to Texas DSHS

name: Seizure
summary: Stabilize active seizure, identify provoked vs unprovoked cause, and recognize status epilepticus.

triage: If actively seizing, treat as ESI 1 and start time clock for status (>5 min)
Check capillary glucose immediately; protect airway
Obtain seizure history, AED adherence, alcohol/drug use, pregnancy
Postictal patients ESI 2-3; assess for trauma and recurrence risk
nursing: Position laterally, suction, protect head; do not restrain
Cardiac monitor, continuous SpO2, supplemental O2
Establish IV access; have benzodiazepines and airway equipment ready
Seizure precautions and padded rails
keyOrders: IV access x2 and continuous monitoring
Airway adjuncts/BVM at bedside; NPO if ongoing AMS
labs: AED levels if on therapy (e.g., phenytoin, valproate, levetiracetam)*
Lactate, ammonia, tox screen if etiology unclear*
imaging: Non-contrast head CT for first seizure, focal deficit, trauma, or AMS
MRI brain for new-onset focal seizure workup (often outpatient/transfer)* if indicated
CXR if aspiration suspected* if indicated
meds: Lorazepam 4 mg IV (repeat once in 5-10 min) for active seizure/status
Midazolam 10 mg IM if no IV access
Levetiracetam 60 mg/kg IV (max 4500 mg) as second-line
Fosphenytoin 20 mg PE/kg IV or valproate 40 mg/kg IV alternative second-line
Dextrose 50% 25 g IV + thiamine 100 mg IV if hypoglycemic/alcoholic*
Magnesium sulfate 4-6 g IV for eclamptic seizure*
reassess: Confirm seizure termination; monitor for recurrence and respiratory depression
Recheck glucose and electrolytes; correct abnormalities
Serial neuro exam through postictal recovery
Escalate to RSI/anesthetic infusion if refractory status*
disposition: Admit/observe for status, refractory seizures, or unresolved AMS
Transfer for ICU, neurology, or neurosurgical needs not available on site
Discharge known epileptic with single typical seizure who returns to baseline*
Neurology consult for first unprovoked seizure
discharge: Driving restrictions per Texas law and physician counseling
AED resumption/initiation instructions and adherence counseling
Avoid alcohol, sleep deprivation, and known triggers
Return for recurrent seizures, injury, prolonged confusion, or fever
followup: Neurology within 1-2 weeks (sooner for new-onset)
Outpatient EEG and MRI arrangement
PCP for medication monitoring and levels

redFlags: Continuous or recurrent seizures >5 min (status epilepticus)
Failure to return to baseline mental status (nonconvulsive status)
Focal deficit, fever with seizure (CNS infection), or head trauma
Pregnancy/postpartum seizure (eclampsia)
Hypoglycemia, toxic ingestion, or severe electrolyte derangement
compliance: EMTALA screening and stabilization required; transfer if ICU/neurology capability lacking
Document accepting facility and stable airway/hemodynamics before transfer
Counsel and document Texas driving restrictions after seizure
Report suspected abuse if seizure linked to nonaccidental trauma

name: Altered Mental Status
summary: Broad differential (metabolic, toxic, infectious, structural, hypoxic) requiring rapid reversible-cause screen.

triage: ABCs and GCS; capillary glucose immediately
Vital signs including temperature and SpO2; check for hypoxia/hypercarbia
Obtain collateral history, medication/toxin exposure, onset/tempo
Assign ESI 1-2 if depressed consciousness or unstable vitals
nursing: Cardiac monitor, continuous SpO2, frequent vitals
IV access; protect airway and position to prevent aspiration
Fall and aspiration precautions; reorient frequently
Foley/urine sample if infection suspected* if indicated
keyOrders: Empiric thiamine before glucose in malnourished/alcoholic; consider naloxone if opioid suspected
labs: Urinalysis, urine and blood cultures if infection*
TSH, troponin, tox screen, acetaminophen/salicylate levels*
Blood alcohol and pregnancy test as indicated*
imaging: Non-contrast head CT for focal deficit, trauma, anticoagulation, or unexplained AMS
CXR for pneumonia/hypoxia* if indicated
LP if CNS infection suspected after imaging* if indicated
meds: Thiamine 100-500 mg IV before glucose if Wernicke risk
Dextrose 50% 25 g IV if glucose <60 mg/dL
Naloxone 0.04-0.4 mg IV/IM titrated if opioid toxicity suspected
Empiric antibiotics (e.g., ceftriaxone 2 g IV) + acyclovir 10 mg/kg IV if meningoencephalitis*
Lorazepam 1-2 mg IV for agitation/withdrawal* if indicated
Isotonic fluids for hypotension/dehydration*
reassess: Serial GCS/mental status and vitals
Recheck glucose and oxygenation after interventions
Reassess airway protection continuously; intubate if unable to protect*
Track response to reversal agents and fluids
disposition: Admit/observe for most AMS pending cause resolution
ICU transfer for airway compromise, sepsis, or intracranial pathology
Transfer for neurosurgery, dialysis, or specialty care not on site
Discharge only if fully reversible cause corrected and back to baseline*
discharge: Only after return to baseline with caregiver and clear cause
Medication reconciliation to remove offending agents
Caregiver return precautions for recurrence or worsening
Address dehydration, infection follow-up as relevant
followup: PCP within days for delirium workup and med review
Specialty follow-up per cause (neurology, nephrology, endocrine)
Cognitive evaluation if persistent deficits

redFlags: GCS decline, inability to protect airway, or hypoxia/hypercarbia
Focal neuro deficit or signs of increased ICP
Fever with meningismus (CNS infection)
Hypoglycemia, severe sodium derangement, or toxic ingestion
Sepsis or hemodynamic instability
compliance: EMTALA screening and stabilization mandatory; secure airway before transfer if at risk
Document accepting physician, capability gap, and transfer mode for ICU/neurosurgical needs
Mandatory reporting for suspected elder abuse/neglect if AMS from neglect or intoxication of vulnerable adult
Observation only when monitoring and reversal can be safely delivered on site

name: Dizziness / Vertigo
summary: Distinguish benign peripheral vertigo from dangerous central causes (posterior stroke) and non-vestibular dizziness.

triage: Characterize timing/triggers; screen for neuro deficits, gait failure
Orthostatic vitals; capillary glucose
Identify cardiac/stroke risk factors and anticoagulation
ESI 2 if neuro signs/inability to walk; otherwise ESI 3-4
nursing: Fall precautions; assist with ambulation
Cardiac monitor and SpO2 if cardiac/presyncope features*
IV access if antiemetics/fluids planned*
Quiet positioning to minimize symptoms
keyOrders: HINTS exam in acute continuous vestibular syndrome with nystagmus
imaging: MRI brain with DWI for suspected central/posterior stroke (often via transfer)* if indicated
Non-contrast head CT if acute and MRI unavailable (low sensitivity early)* if indicated
CTA head/neck if vertebrobasilar disease/dissection suspected* if indicated
meds: Meclizine 25 mg PO for peripheral vertigo
Ondansetron 4 mg IV/ODT for nausea
Prochlorperazine 10 mg IV or promethazine 12.5-25 mg IV for vomiting
Diazepam 2-5 mg PO/IV short-term vestibular suppressant*
IV crystalloid for dehydration/orthostasis*
Epley maneuver (procedure) for confirmed posterior canal BPPV
reassess: Reassess gait and ability to ambulate safely
Repeat HINTS/neuro exam if symptoms persist
Confirm symptom control after antiemetics/maneuvers
Re-evaluate orthostatics after fluids
disposition: Discharge benign peripheral vertigo (BPPV, vestibular neuritis) if ambulatory
Transfer/admit for suspected central cause or inability to ambulate
Neurology consult for central HINTS or new neuro deficit
Cardiology evaluation if presyncope/arrhythmia
discharge: Vestibular suppressant short-term only; home Epley/Brandt-Daroff instructions
Fall precautions and hydration guidance
Return for new weakness, double vision, slurred speech, severe headache, or inability to walk
Avoid driving while symptomatic
followup: PCP or ENT for recurrent peripheral vertigo
Neurology if central features or persistent symptoms
Vestibular rehab referral as needed

redFlags: Inability to walk or truncal ataxia (central until proven otherwise)
Direction-changing or vertical nystagmus; abnormal HINTS (central pattern)
Associated diplopia, dysarthria, dysphagia, or focal weakness
Sudden severe headache or neck pain with vertigo (dissection)
New deficit in patient with vascular risk factors
compliance: EMTALA screening and documentation distinguishing peripheral vs central cause
Transfer for MRI/neurology when posterior stroke suspected and unavailable on site; document accepting physician
Observation reasonable for symptomatic but ambulatory peripheral vertigo
Document ambulation assessment before discharge to mitigate fall risk

name: Weakness / Numbness
summary: Localize deficit (central vs peripheral vs neuromuscular) and exclude stroke, cord compression, and ascending paralysis.

triage: Determine onset, distribution, and progression; screen for stroke (BE-FAST)
Assess for bowel/bladder dysfunction, saddle anesthesia, respiratory effort
Capillary glucose; vitals including SpO2
ESI 1-2 if acute focal deficit, ascending pattern, or respiratory compromise
nursing: Cardiac monitor and SpO2 if acute/diffuse
IV access
Fall precautions; assist mobility
Bladder scan/Foley if retention suspected* if indicated
keyOrders: Serial motor/sensory exam with documented level; NPO if surgical pathology likely*
labs: Negative inspiratory force/vital capacity if neuromuscular weakness*
imaging: Non-contrast head CT if stroke pattern (then per stroke pathway)
MRI spine emergently if cord compression/cauda equina suspected* if indicated
MRI brain for central localization (often via transfer)* if indicated
CTA if vascular cause suspected* if indicated
meds: Follow stroke pathway meds if acute central deficit (see Suspected Stroke)*
Dexamethasone 10 mg IV for suspected cord compression from tumor*
IV crystalloid for hypotension*
Correct electrolytes (e.g., potassium, calcium, magnesium) per labs*
Analgesia per protocol for radicular pain* if indicated
reassess: Serial neuro exam tracking motor strength and sensory level
Monitor respiratory status/vital capacity if ascending weakness
Reassess for stroke progression and need for reperfusion transfer
Recheck glucose/electrolytes after correction
disposition: Emergent transfer for stroke, cord compression, or cauda equina (neurosurgery/spine)
Admit for Guillain-Barre/myasthenic crisis monitoring (ICU)
Neurology/neurosurgery consult for new central or cord findings
Discharge only for clearly benign/peripheral, resolving cause* if indicated
discharge: Return precautions: spreading weakness, breathing difficulty, bladder/bowel changes
Activity and safety counseling; mobility aids if needed
Address reversible cause (electrolytes, medication)
Provide specialist appointment details
followup: Neurology for new or progressive deficits
Spine surgery if disc/cord pathology
PCP for metabolic/nutritional causes

redFlags: Acute focal weakness consistent with stroke
Saddle anesthesia, urinary retention, bilateral leg weakness (cauda equina)
Ascending weakness with respiratory compromise (Guillain-Barre)
Fluctuating weakness with bulbar/respiratory symptoms (myasthenic crisis)
Sensory level on the trunk (spinal cord lesion)
compliance: EMTALA screening and stabilization; emergent transfer for stroke/cord emergencies with documented accepting specialist
Time-sensitive cord compression and stroke should not be observed if definitive care unavailable on site
Document neuro exam, respiratory status, and transfer mode
Report suspected abuse if deficits from inflicted trauma
