name: Head Injury / Concussion
summary: Risk-stratify traumatic brain injury and identify intracranial hemorrhage, especially in anticoagulated patients.

triage: GCS, mechanism, LOC/amnesia, anticoagulant/antiplatelet use
Apply validated decision rule (Canadian CT Head Rule)
Assess for c-spine tenderness; maintain spinal precautions if indicated
ESI 2 for GCS <15, anticoagulation, or severe mechanism
nursing: Spinal motion restriction until c-spine cleared* if indicated
Cardiac monitor and SpO2 if moderate-severe TBI*
IV access for symptomatic or high-risk patients*
Serial GCS/neuro checks
keyOrders: GCS documentation and serial neuro exams
IV access if imaging contrast or intervention likely*
labs: CBC and type and screen if significant trauma*
Ethanol/tox screen if intoxication confounds exam*
imaging: Non-contrast head CT per decision rule, anticoagulation, or GCS <15
CT cervical spine if midline tenderness or high-risk mechanism* if indicated
Repeat/delayed CT in anticoagulated patients with initial negative scan*
CT angiography if vascular injury suspected* if indicated
meds: Acetaminophen 1000 mg PO for headache (avoid NSAIDs if bleeding risk)
Ondansetron 4 mg IV for nausea/vomiting
Reverse anticoagulation (PCC, vitamin K, idarucizumab, andexanet) if ICH*
Hypertonic saline 3% or mannitol 0.5-1 g/kg IV for herniation signs*
Levetiracetam 60 mg/kg IV for post-traumatic seizure prophylaxis in severe TBI*
reassess: Serial GCS and pupil checks
Re-evaluate for delayed deterioration, especially if anticoagulated
Reassess for vomiting, worsening headache, or new deficit
Confirm c-spine clearance clinically/radiographically before removing precautions
disposition: Emergent neurosurgical transfer for intracranial hemorrhage or GCS decline
Observe anticoagulated patients with negative initial CT per protocol
Discharge minor concussion with normal exam and reliable caregiver
Admit/transfer for moderate-severe TBI
discharge: Concussion return-to-activity and return-to-play/work guidance with cognitive rest
Caregiver awakening/observation instructions and head-injury return precautions
Avoid alcohol, anticoagulants per provider, and high-risk activity
Symptoms of worsening: repeated vomiting, severe/worsening headache, confusion, seizure, unequal pupils
followup: PCP or concussion clinic within 1 week
Neurology/neurosurgery if persistent or severe symptoms
Repeat imaging only if clinically indicated

redFlags: GCS <15 or declining, focal deficit, or seizure
Anticoagulation/antiplatelet use with head trauma
Repeated vomiting, severe headache, or post-traumatic amnesia
Signs of basilar skull fracture (raccoon eyes, Battle sign, CSF leak)
Unequal/fixed pupils or Cushing response (herniation)
compliance: EMTALA screening and stabilization; emergent transfer to trauma/neurosurgery for ICH with documented accepting physician
Observation acceptable for anticoagulated patients with negative CT if monitoring and repeat imaging available
Mandatory reporting of suspected assault, intimate partner violence, or child/elder abuse-related head injury per Texas law
Document mechanism, decision rule applied, and transfer mode

name: Acute Vision Changes
summary: Differentiate vascular/neuro causes (CRAO, stroke, GCA, ICP) from primary ocular emergencies requiring rapid action.

triage: Determine monocular vs binocular, painless vs painful, onset/tempo
Check visual acuity each eye, pupils (RAPD), and confrontation fields
Screen for stroke signs, GCA symptoms (age >50, jaw claudication, scalp tenderness)
ESI 2 for sudden painless vision loss or neuro deficit
nursing: Quiet positioning; assist ambulation
Cardiac monitor and SpO2 if stroke suspected*
IV access if treatment/imaging planned*
Avoid pressure on a potentially ruptured globe* if indicated
keyOrders: Bedside intraocular pressure if acute angle-closure suspected*
labs: ESR and CRP STAT if GCA suspected*
Coags if anticoagulated or thrombolysis considered*
imaging: MRI brain with DWI if stroke/optic pathway lesion suspected (often transfer)* if indicated
CT/CTA head and orbits for vascular or compressive lesion* if indicated
Carotid imaging if amaurosis fugax/CRAO* if indicated
meds: High-dose corticosteroids (methylprednisolone 500-1000 mg IV) immediately if GCA suspected*
Acetazolamide 500 mg IV plus topical agents for acute angle-closure glaucoma*
Aspirin 325 mg for retinal artery occlusion/TIA after evaluation*
Antiemetic (ondansetron 4 mg IV) for associated nausea*
Treat hypertensive emergency if end-organ involvement*
reassess: Recheck visual acuity after interventions
Reassess for evolving neuro deficits (stroke)
Confirm IOP response in angle-closure*
Document GCA treatment started while awaiting biopsy*
disposition: Emergent ophthalmology consult/transfer for CRAO, angle-closure, retinal detachment, endophthalmitis
Stroke-center transfer if central/neurologic cause
Admit/transfer for GCA with vision loss for IV steroids and biopsy
Discharge benign causes with rapid ophthalmology follow-up*
discharge: Specific ophthalmology/neurology appointment timing
Return precautions: worsening or new vision loss, eye pain, neuro symptoms
Eye protection and medication instructions
Avoid driving while vision impaired
followup: Ophthalmology within 24 h for urgent ocular causes
Neurology/stroke clinic for neuro-visual causes
Rheumatology for confirmed/suspected GCA

redFlags: Sudden painless monocular vision loss (CRAO, retinal detachment)
Age >50 with vision loss, headache, jaw claudication (GCA)
Painful red eye with halos, fixed mid-dilated pupil (angle-closure)
New diplopia with neuro deficit or severe headache (aneurysm/stroke)
RAPD or visual field cut suggesting optic/retrochiasmal pathology
compliance: EMTALA screening; emergent transfer when ophthalmology or stroke care is unavailable on site
Document time-critical interventions (GCA steroids, CRAO measures) and accepting specialist
Observation generally not appropriate for vision-threatening emergencies needing specialty care
Record visual acuity at arrival and disposition

name: New Tremor
summary: Identify dangerous causes (withdrawal, toxicity, hypoglycemia, serotonin syndrome, thyroid storm) vs benign tremor.

triage: Characterize tremor (rest vs action), onset, medications, alcohol/substance history
Vitals including temperature; capillary glucose
Screen for autonomic instability, agitation, and AMS
ESI 2 if hyperthermia, autonomic instability, or AMS
nursing: Cardiac monitor and SpO2 if toxic/withdrawal etiology*
IV access if symptomatic or unstable*
Fall precautions; cooling measures if hyperthermic*
Frequent vitals and neuro checks
keyOrders: IV access and monitoring if unstable*
Temperature monitoring; institute cooling if hyperthermic*
imaging: Non-contrast head CT if focal deficit or AMS accompanies tremor* if indicated
Generally no imaging for isolated benign tremor
Further neuroimaging typically outpatient (MRI)* if indicated
meds: Dextrose 50% 25 g IV if hypoglycemic
Lorazepam 2-4 mg IV for alcohol withdrawal or agitation
Cyproheptadine 12 mg PO and cooling for serotonin syndrome*
Beta-blocker (propranolol 20-40 mg PO) for thyrotoxic/essential tremor*
Active cooling and supportive care for hyperthermic syndromes*
Thiamine 100 mg IV in alcohol-related presentations*
reassess: Reassess temperature, mental status, and autonomic stability
Monitor response to benzodiazepines in withdrawal (CIWA-guided)*
Recheck glucose and electrolytes after correction
Watch for progression to seizure or instability
disposition: Admit/transfer for severe withdrawal, serotonin syndrome, NMS, or thyroid storm (ICU)
Discharge benign essential/medication-related tremor that is stable
Neurology referral for new parkinsonian or unexplained tremor
Transfer if ICU-level monitoring unavailable on site
discharge: Medication review and removal of offending/serotonergic agents
Alcohol-cessation resources and outpatient withdrawal plan if applicable
Return precautions: fever, confusion, seizures, worsening shaking, palpitations
Avoid stimulants/caffeine if exacerbating
followup: Neurology for persistent or progressive tremor
PCP for medication adjustment and thyroid management
Substance-use treatment referral if applicable

redFlags: Hyperthermia with rigidity/clonus (serotonin syndrome or NMS)
Autonomic instability with tremor (thyroid storm, severe withdrawal)
Altered mental status or seizure accompanying tremor
Hypoglycemia as cause
Asterixis with confusion (hepatic/uremic encephalopathy)
compliance: EMTALA screening and stabilization; transfer for ICU-level toxidromes/withdrawal with documented accepting physician
Observation acceptable for mild withdrawal if monitoring and CIWA protocol available on site
Document substance-use history and any mandated reporting (e.g., vulnerable adult neglect)
Record vitals trend and treatment response before disposition

name: Gait Disturbance / Ataxia
summary: Determine acute vs chronic and central vs peripheral cause; exclude posterior stroke, intoxication, and cord pathology.

triage: Assess onset/tempo, associated neuro symptoms, fall history, toxin/alcohol use
Capillary glucose; vitals including SpO2
Screen for stroke (BE-FAST) and ability to ambulate safely
ESI 2 if acute ataxia with neuro deficit or inability to walk
nursing: Fall precautions; assist all transfers/ambulation
Cardiac monitor and SpO2 if central cause suspected*
IV access if treatment/imaging anticipated*
Document gait assessment and assistive needs
keyOrders: Detailed neuro exam (cerebellar testing, Romberg, gait)
IV access and monitoring if unstable*
labs: Ethanol level and tox screen if intoxication suspected*
imaging: MRI brain with DWI for suspected posterior circulation stroke (often transfer)* if indicated
Non-contrast head CT if acute and hemorrhage/mass suspected* if indicated
MRI spine if myelopathy/sensory ataxia suspected* if indicated
CTA head/neck if vertebrobasilar disease/dissection* if indicated
meds: Thiamine 100-500 mg IV if Wernicke suspected (give before glucose)*
Dextrose 50% 25 g IV if hypoglycemic*
Antiemetic (ondansetron 4 mg IV) for associated nausea*
Treat underlying cause per workup (e.g., correct electrolytes)*
Follow stroke pathway meds if acute central deficit*
reassess: Reassess gait and fall risk after interventions
Repeat neuro/cerebellar exam to track progression
Re-evaluate for evolving stroke needing transfer
Recheck glucose/electrolytes after correction
disposition: Emergent transfer/admit for acute cerebellar stroke or hemorrhage
Admit for Wernicke encephalopathy or unsafe gait without support
Neurology consult for new central ataxia
Discharge chronic/benign ataxia with safe gait and support*
discharge: Fall-prevention measures and assistive device (walker/cane) and PT referral
Address reversible causes (medication, electrolytes, B12)
Return precautions: sudden worsening, weakness, speech/vision changes, headache
Avoid driving until cleared
followup: Neurology for new or progressive ataxia
PCP for metabolic/nutritional contributors
Physical therapy and home-safety evaluation

redFlags: Acute ataxia with vertigo, diplopia, dysarthria (cerebellar stroke)
Inability to walk or truncal ataxia
Confusion, ophthalmoplegia, ataxia triad (Wernicke encephalopathy)
Sensory level or bilateral leg involvement (cord pathology)
Severe headache or neck pain with ataxia (hemorrhage/dissection)
compliance: EMTALA screening; emergent transfer for cerebellar stroke/hemorrhage when neuro/neurosurgery unavailable on site
Document ambulation/fall-risk assessment before any discharge
Observation acceptable for stable chronic ataxia with safe disposition support
Report suspected elder abuse/neglect if gait failure linked to neglect, and document accepting physician/transfer mode

name: Vaginal Bleeding (Pregnant)
summary: Bleeding at any gestational age; differential spans ectopic, threatened/incomplete/complete miscarriage, molar pregnancy, subchorionic hemorrhage, and late-pregnancy placenta previa/abruption.

triage: Confirm pregnancy and estimate gestational age (LMP, prior dating US)
Quantify bleeding (pads/hr) and pass of tissue/clots
Vitals with orthostatics; flag tachycardia/hypotension as shock
Assess pain, syncope, shoulder tip pain (ectopic concern)
Triage ESI 2 if hemodynamically unstable or >20 wks bleeding
nursing: Place on monitor if unstable; large-bore IV x2
Pad count and weigh pads for blood loss estimate
Obtain blood type/Rh status early
Save any passed tissue/products for pathology
Reassure and keep patient NPO until disposition clear
keyOrders: Cardiac monitor and pulse oximetry if unstable
Type & screen / type & crossmatch if heavy bleeding
labs: Coagulation studies if heavy bleeding or >20 wks
Type & crossmatch 2 units if hemorrhage*if indicated
imaging: Pelvic ultrasound (TVUS) for IUP vs ectopic vs products
Compare beta-hCG to discriminatory zone (~1500-2000) if no IUP seen
Late pregnancy: NO digital/speculum exam until previa excluded by US
Repeat/serial US per OB if indeterminate*if indicated
meds: Rho(D) immune globulin (RhoGAM) 300 mcg IM if Rh-negative
Ondansetron 4 mg IV for nausea*if indicated
Crystalloid bolus 1-2 L for hypotension
Tranexamic acid 1 g IV for severe hemorrhage*if indicated
Analgesia: acetaminophen 1 g PO or fentanyl 25-50 mcg IV titrated
reassess: Recheck vitals/orthostatics after fluids
Reassess ongoing blood loss (pad count)
Trend hemoglobin if active bleeding
Confirm fetal viability/heart tones if viable gestation
Escalate to OB immediately if unstable or ectopic confirmed
disposition: OB consult for ectopic, molar, incomplete/septic abortion, or hemorrhage
Admit/transfer for surgical or unstable cases
Discharge stable threatened abortion with confirmed IUP and reliable follow-up
Transfer to OB-capable facility if no OB/surgical coverage on site
discharge: Strict return precautions: heavy bleeding (>1 pad/hr), severe pain, fever, syncope
Pelvic rest (no intercourse/tampons) until follow-up
Document RhoGAM given if Rh-negative
Provide serial beta-hCG instructions if pregnancy of unknown location
followup: OB/Gyn within 48-72 hrs for repeat beta-hCG and US
Early pregnancy assessment unit referral if available
Emotional support / pregnancy loss resources as indicated

redFlags: Hemodynamic instability / hypovolemic shock
Confirmed or suspected ruptured ectopic
Heavy bleeding >20 wks (previa/abruption)
Fever + bleeding suggesting septic abortion
Beta-hCG >discriminatory zone with empty uterus
compliance: EMTALA medical screening exam required for any pregnant bleeder regardless of payment
Stabilize hemorrhage before any transfer; document appropriate-transfer criteria and accepting OB facility
FSED must arrange EMTALA-compliant transfer for surgical/OB cases beyond capability
Observation acceptable for stable threatened abortion with reliable follow-up; transfer if deterioration
Document Rh status and RhoGAM administration per standard of care

name: Vaginal Bleeding (Non-Pregnant)
summary: Abnormal uterine bleeding in confirmed non-pregnant patient; differential includes structural (PALM) and non-structural (COEIN) causes; postmenopausal bleeding is cancer until proven otherwise.

triage: Confirm negative pregnancy test before classifying as non-pregnant
Quantify bleeding (pads/hr, clots, duration)
Vitals with orthostatics; flag instability
Assess for anemia symptoms (fatigue, dyspnea, lightheadedness)
Note menopausal status and anticoagulant use
nursing: IV access if heavy bleeding or symptomatic anemia
Pad count for blood loss tracking
Obtain orthostatic vitals
Prepare pelvic exam tray and chaperone
Type & screen if transfusion possible
keyOrders: IV access with crystalloid if symptomatic
labs: Coagulation panel and TSH if heavy/recurrent
Ferritin/iron studies if chronic anemia*if indicated
imaging: Transvaginal pelvic ultrasound for fibroids/polyps/endometrial stripe
Endometrial thickness assessment in postmenopausal bleeding
Refer for endometrial biopsy (outpatient) for postmenopausal or high-risk*if indicated
meds: Tranexamic acid 1.3 g PO TID or 1 g IV for acute heavy bleeding
Medroxyprogesterone 10 mg PO daily or norethindrone for hormonal control
Combined estrogen-progestin (high-dose OCP taper) for acute AUB
Conjugated estrogen 25 mg IV q4-6h for severe acute hemorrhage*if indicated
Iron supplementation (ferrous sulfate 325 mg PO) for anemia
reassess: Recheck orthostatics and bleeding after intervention
Trend hemoglobin if active loss
Assess response to hormonal/TXA therapy
Escalate to Gyn for uncontrolled hemorrhage
disposition: Discharge most stable AUB with hormonal therapy and Gyn follow-up
Transfuse and admit/transfer for hemodynamic instability or severe anemia
Urgent Gyn consult for uncontrolled bleeding
Transfer to Gyn-capable facility if procedural intervention needed
discharge: Return precautions: soaking >1 pad/hr, lightheadedness, syncope
Hormonal medication instructions and adherence counseling
Iron repletion guidance
Stress importance of endometrial evaluation if postmenopausal
followup: Gyn within 1-2 weeks (urgent if postmenopausal for cancer workup)
Outpatient endometrial biopsy / hysteroscopy referral
Hematology if suspected bleeding disorder*if indicated

redFlags: Hemodynamic instability / hypovolemic shock
Hemoglobin <7 or symptomatic severe anemia
Postmenopausal bleeding (malignancy risk)
Bleeding refractory to medical therapy
Signs of coagulopathy
compliance: EMTALA medical screening exam applies regardless of insurance/payment
Stabilize and transfuse before transfer; document appropriate-transfer paperwork
Observation acceptable for stable patients responding to therapy
Transfer to facility with Gyn/OR capability for uncontrolled hemorrhage
Document chaperone presence for pelvic exam

name: Pelvic Pain
summary: Acute lower abdominal/pelvic pain in females; must exclude ectopic, ovarian torsion, tubo-ovarian abscess/PID, ruptured cyst, and appendicitis.

triage: Pregnancy test status and LMP
Pain onset, character, laterality, and severity
Vitals; flag peritoneal signs or sepsis criteria
Screen for sudden severe unilateral pain (torsion) or syncope (ectopic)
ESI 2 for suspected torsion, ruptured ectopic, or sepsis
nursing: IV access and analgesia early
Obtain vitals and reassess pain score
Prepare pelvic exam tray and chaperone
Collect urine for hCG and UA
NPO if surgical pathology suspected
labs: Gonorrhea/chlamydia NAAT and wet mount if PID suspected
imaging: Transvaginal + transabdominal pelvic US with Doppler (torsion, cyst, TOA)
CT abdomen/pelvis if appendicitis or non-gyn source suspected*if indicated
Urgent US for suspected torsion (do not delay surgical consult)
meds: Analgesia: ketorolac 15-30 mg IV or morphine 0.1 mg/kg IV
Ondansetron 4 mg IV for nausea
PID outpatient: ceftriaxone 500 mg IM + doxycycline 100 mg PO BID x14d + metronidazole 500 mg BID
PID inpatient: cefotetan 2 g IV q12h + doxycycline 100 mg IV/PO q12h
IV crystalloid bolus for hypotension*if indicated
reassess: Reassess pain after analgesia
Recheck vitals and peritoneal signs
Confirm US findings with Gyn for torsion
Escalate for worsening exam or instability
disposition: Emergent Gyn/surgery for torsion or ruptured ectopic
Admit/transfer TOA or sepsis for IV antibiotics
Discharge mild PID or simple cyst with follow-up
Transfer to surgical-capable facility if no Gyn/OR on site
discharge: Complete full PID antibiotic course; abstain from intercourse until treated
Return precautions: worsening pain, fever, vomiting, syncope
Partner treatment and STI counseling for PID
Pain control plan and Gyn follow-up
followup: Gyn within 48-72 hrs for PID re-evaluation
Repeat US for cyst surveillance*if indicated
STI test-of-cure and partner notification per CDC

redFlags: Suspected ovarian torsion (sudden severe pain, adnexal mass)
Ruptured ectopic / hemodynamic instability
Tubo-ovarian abscess or septic PID
Peritonitis / surgical abdomen
Pregnancy with pelvic pain
compliance: EMTALA MSE required; stabilize before transfer
Time-sensitive transfer for torsion (ovarian salvage) or ruptured ectopic to surgical facility
Mandatory Texas reporting of gonorrhea/chlamydia to DSHS
Document chaperone for pelvic exam
Observation appropriate for indeterminate stable cases with serial exams

name: First-Trimester Abdominal Pain
summary: Abdominal/pelvic pain at <13 weeks gestation; ectopic pregnancy must be excluded; also consider corpus luteum cyst, miscarriage, UTI, and appendicitis.

triage: Confirm pregnancy and gestational age
Assess pain location, severity, syncope, shoulder pain
Vitals with orthostatics; flag instability
Quantify any vaginal bleeding
ESI 2 if unstable or high ectopic concern
nursing: Large-bore IV access x2
Continuous monitoring if unstable
Obtain blood type/Rh
Collect urine for hCG and UA
NPO pending evaluation
keyOrders: NPO pending US and surgical clearance
labs: Coagulation/crossmatch if bleeding or unstable*if indicated
imaging: Transvaginal ultrasound to confirm IUP vs ectopic
Correlate beta-hCG with discriminatory zone if no IUP
FAST exam for free fluid if unstable
OB consult for indeterminate/pregnancy of unknown location
meds: Rho(D) immune globulin 300 mcg IM if Rh-negative
Analgesia: acetaminophen 1 g PO; fentanyl 25-50 mcg IV if severe
Ondansetron 4 mg IV for nausea
Crystalloid bolus for hypotension*if indicated
Methotrexate per OB protocol for confirmed unruptured ectopic*if indicated
reassess: Recheck vitals/orthostatics and pain
Reassess for evolving peritoneal signs
Confirm US interpretation with OB
Escalate immediately if rupture suspected
disposition: Emergent OB/surgery for ruptured or confirmed ectopic
Discharge confirmed viable IUP with benign exam and follow-up
Serial beta-hCG protocol for pregnancy of unknown location
Transfer to OB/surgical facility if no coverage
discharge: Return precautions: worsening pain, heavy bleeding, dizziness, syncope
Document RhoGAM if Rh-negative
Pelvic rest until follow-up
Clear instructions on serial beta-hCG timing
followup: OB within 48 hrs for repeat beta-hCG and US
Early pregnancy assessment clinic if available
Strict ectopic precautions reviewed and documented

redFlags: Hemodynamic instability / syncope
Free fluid on US or peritoneal signs
Beta-hCG above discriminatory zone with empty uterus
Confirmed ectopic pregnancy
Severe unrelenting unilateral pain
compliance: EMTALA MSE mandatory regardless of payment status
Stabilize suspected ruptured ectopic before EMTALA-compliant transfer to surgical facility
Document appropriate-transfer criteria and accepting physician
Observation/serial labs acceptable for stable PUL with reliable follow-up
Document Rh status and RhoGAM administration

name: Hyperemesis Gravidarum
summary: Severe persistent nausea/vomiting of pregnancy with dehydration, ketosis, electrolyte derangement, and >5% weight loss; exclude other causes of vomiting.

triage: Confirm pregnancy and gestational age
Assess dehydration (tachycardia, dry mucosa, weight loss)
Quantify emesis frequency and oral intake
Check for warning signs (fever, abdominal pain, hematemesis)
Vitals with orthostatics
nursing: IV access and begin rehydration
Obtain finger-stick glucose and urine ketones
Strict intake/output monitoring
Antiemetics per orders
Dim, calm environment to reduce stimuli
keyOrders: Urine dip for ketones and specific gravity
labs: TSH/free T4 if severe or hyperthyroid signs*if indicated
imaging: Obstetric US to confirm viability and exclude molar/multiple gestation
RUQ US if hepatobiliary cause suspected*if indicated
meds: IV normal saline or LR 1-2 L bolus then maintenance
Pyridoxine (B6) 10-25 mg + doxylamine 12.5 mg PO
Ondansetron 4-8 mg IV (counsel re: first-trimester data)
Promethazine 12.5-25 mg IV or metoclopramide 10 mg IV
Thiamine 100 mg IV before dextrose if prolonged vomiting
Methylprednisolone for refractory cases per OB*if indicated
reassess: Reassess hydration, orthostatics, and symptom relief
Recheck electrolytes if abnormal
Confirm tolerance of oral fluids before discharge
Escalate if persistent vomiting despite therapy
disposition: Discharge once tolerating PO and rehydrated
Admit/transfer for refractory vomiting, severe electrolyte abnormality, or weight loss
OB consult for refractory or molar pregnancy
Transfer to OB-capable facility if admission needed
discharge: Antiemetic regimen and small frequent bland meals
Return precautions: inability to keep fluids down, dizziness, decreased urination
Prescribe pyridoxine/doxylamine and oral ondansetron as needed
Hydration and rest guidance
followup: OB within 2-3 days to reassess and trend weight
Nutrition/dietitian referral if persistent*if indicated
Consider home health/IV hydration program if recurrent

redFlags: Severe electrolyte abnormality (hypokalemia, hyponatremia)
Persistent vomiting despite aggressive therapy
Hematemesis or severe abdominal pain
Signs of Wernicke encephalopathy (confusion, ataxia)
Weight loss >5% with ketosis and dehydration
compliance: EMTALA MSE required regardless of ability to pay
Stabilize dehydration/electrolytes before transfer; thiamine before dextrose
Observation appropriate for fluid/antiemetic trial; transfer if requiring admission and no OB beds
Document appropriate-transfer criteria for refractory cases
Exclude and document molar pregnancy on US

name: Preeclampsia / Hypertension in Pregnancy
summary: Hypertension at >=20 weeks (or postpartum) with possible end-organ damage; spectrum includes preeclampsia, severe features, eclampsia, and HELLP - obstetric emergency.

triage: Confirm gestational age (>=20 wks or postpartum up to 6 wks)
Repeat BP; severe range >=160/110
Screen for headache, visual changes, RUQ pain, seizure
Immediate ESI 1-2 for severe-range BP or eclampsia
Continuous monitoring and call OB early
nursing: Cardiac monitor, automated BP cycling q5-15 min
IV access x2
Seizure precautions and quiet environment
Strict I/O and seizure-watch
Prepare magnesium sulfate per protocol
keyOrders: Fetal heart tones / continuous fetal monitoring if viable
Magnesium sulfate ready for seizure prophylaxis
labs: Coagulation studies if HELLP/low platelets*if indicated
imaging: Obstetric US for fetal status if time permits
Head CT if focal deficit or atypical seizure*if indicated
RUQ assessment if hepatic involvement*if indicated
meds: Magnesium sulfate 4-6 g IV load then 1-2 g/hr for seizure prophylaxis/eclampsia
Labetalol 20 mg IV, escalate 40-80 mg q10min (max 300 mg)
Hydralazine 5-10 mg IV q20min for severe BP
Nifedipine 10 mg PO if no IV access
Calcium gluconate 1 g IV available as magnesium antidote
reassess: Recheck BP q10-15 min targeting <160/<110
Monitor magnesium toxicity (reflexes, RR, urine output)
Reassess neuro status and fetal heart tones
Escalate to OB/MFM emergently; delivery is definitive treatment
disposition: Emergent OB consult; transfer to L&D-capable facility for definitive care
Admit all severe features / eclampsia / HELLP
Do NOT discharge severe-range hypertension
Stabilize then EMTALA transfer if no obstetric services on site
discharge: Only mild gestational HTN without severe features may be discharged with OB
Return precautions: severe headache, visual changes, RUQ pain, seizure, decreased fetal movement
Home BP monitoring instructions
Strict short-interval OB follow-up
followup: OB/MFM within 24-48 hrs (sooner per severity)
Daily-to-weekly BP and lab surveillance per OB
Postpartum BP follow-up (preeclampsia can present postpartum)

redFlags: Severe-range BP >=160/110
Eclamptic seizure
HELLP syndrome (hemolysis, low platelets, elevated LFTs)
Neurologic symptoms (severe headache, visual changes)
Pulmonary edema or oliguria
compliance: Obstetric emergency under EMTALA - immediate MSE and stabilization (Mg, antihypertensives)
FSED without L&D must stabilize then perform appropriate transfer to obstetric facility
Document accepting OB physician, treatments given, and maternal-fetal stability for transfer
Severe preeclampsia/eclampsia is NOT appropriate for outpatient observation
Treat severe-range BP within 30-60 minutes per ACOG

name: Imminent Delivery
summary: Active labor with delivery imminent or in progress; manage controlled delivery, anticipate neonatal resuscitation and postpartum hemorrhage; under EMTALA delivery is an emergency medical condition.

triage: Assess contractions, urge to push, and crowning
Gestational age, gravidity/parity, prenatal complications
Maternal vitals and fetal heart tones
If crowning - do NOT transfer; prepare for delivery
Activate OB/neonatal team and notify receiving facility
nursing: IV access x2 and prepare delivery/precipitous kit
Set up infant warmer and neonatal resuscitation equipment
Continuous fetal heart tone monitoring
Position mother; prepare suction and clamps
Call additional staff and pediatric/EMS support
labs: Group B strep status from prenatal records if available
Cord blood gas/type at delivery*if indicated
imaging: Bedside US for fetal presentation/position if time permits*if indicated
Do not delay delivery for imaging if crowning
meds: Oxytocin 10 units IM or 10-40 units in 1 L IV after delivery (active third stage)
Tranexamic acid 1 g IV for postpartum hemorrhage
Misoprostol 800-1000 mcg PR for atony
Methylergonovine 0.2 mg IM for atony (avoid if hypertensive)
Neonatal resuscitation per NRP; warm, dry, stimulate, vitamin K + erythromycin eyes
reassess: Assess maternal bleeding and uterine tone post-delivery
Apgar scoring and neonatal status at 1 and 5 min
Estimate blood loss; massage fundus
Reassess for retained placenta or lacerations
Escalate hemorrhage to massive transfusion protocol if needed
disposition: Stabilize mother and neonate; transfer both to OB/NICU-capable facility
Postpartum hemorrhage or distressed neonate - emergent transfer with EMS
Notify accepting facility before/during transfer
Do not transfer during active crowning/delivery
discharge: Discharge from FSED not appropriate; arrange transfer/admission
If imminent transfer delayed, continue maternal/neonatal monitoring
Document delivery time, Apgars, blood loss, placenta delivery
followup: Inpatient OB and newborn care at receiving hospital
Postpartum and newborn follow-up arranged by receiving facility
Social work/lactation support as available

redFlags: Postpartum hemorrhage / uterine atony
Abnormal presentation (breech, shoulder dystocia, cord prolapse)
Fetal distress / bradycardia
Maternal hypotension or seizure
Retained placenta or significant lacerations
compliance: Active labor is an EMTALA emergency medical condition - cannot refuse or delay for payment
A woman in active labor is NOT in stable condition for transfer until after delivery (mother and baby stabilized)
FSED must attempt delivery if birth is imminent; transfer only if delivery not imminent and benefits outweigh risks
Document EMTALA active-labor assessment, delivery details, and appropriate-transfer paperwork
Mandatory birth reporting and newborn handoff to receiving facility

name: Sexual Assault
summary: Acute care of sexual assault survivor; prioritize safety, medical stabilization, trauma-informed care, forensic evidence collection, prophylaxis, and mandated reporting.

triage: Ensure physical safety and private, secure area
Trauma-informed, patient-led approach; obtain consent at every step
Address acute injuries first (ABCs, trauma)
Activate SANE (Sexual Assault Nurse Examiner) if available
Offer victim advocate / support person
nursing: Maintain chain of custody for all evidence
Offer SANE exam and evidence kit with explicit consent
Provide clean clothing; preserve clothing as evidence
Document injuries with body maps/photography per consent
Provide private, calm, supportive environment
keyOrders: Address and treat acute traumatic injuries first
Offer law-enforcement notification (patient's choice unless mandated)
labs: HIV, hepatitis B/C, RPR (syphilis) baseline serologies
Toxicology if drug-facilitated assault suspected*if indicated
Baseline labs prior to HIV PEP (CBC, CMP)
imaging: Imaging only as needed for traumatic injuries*if indicated
Document genital/extragenital injuries via exam and photography
meds: Emergency contraception: levonorgestrel 1.5 mg PO or ulipristal 30 mg PO
GC/CT/trich prophylaxis: ceftriaxone 500 mg IM + doxycycline 100 mg BID x7d + metronidazole 2 g PO
Hepatitis B vaccine +/- HBIG if unvaccinated
HIV nPEP (tenofovir-emtricitabine + dolutegravir) x28d if indicated within 72 hrs
Tetanus prophylaxis and analgesia as needed
reassess: Reassess for occult injuries and emotional status
Confirm prophylaxis offered/given and documented
Verify safety plan before discharge
Confirm chain of custody intact
disposition: Discharge to safe environment with advocate when medically stable
Admit/transfer for serious traumatic injuries
Arrange SANE/forensic transfer if no SANE on site
Transfer to facility with SANE/trauma capability if needed
discharge: Provide written follow-up, prophylaxis instructions, and crisis hotline numbers
Safety planning and connection to advocacy services
STI/HIV follow-up testing schedule
Counseling/mental health referral
followup: STI re-testing and HIV PEP follow-up at 2 weeks and serologies at 4-6 weeks/3 months
Pregnancy test follow-up
Sexual assault advocacy and mental health services
Primary care / Gyn follow-up

redFlags: Significant physical trauma or hemodynamic instability
Strangulation injury (delayed airway/vascular risk)
Suicidal ideation or acute psychological crisis
Drug-facilitated assault with altered mental status
Ongoing safety threat from perpetrator
compliance: EMTALA MSE and stabilization of any emergency medical condition required
Texas: mandatory reporting if victim is a child, elderly, or disabled adult (suspected abuse)
Preserve chain of custody; do not condition care on reporting to police (adult competent victim chooses)
Offer SANE exam; transfer to SANE-capable facility if unavailable, documenting appropriate transfer
Texas hospitals/FSEDs must provide or arrange emergency contraception and forensic exam information to survivors

name: Vaginal Discharge
summary: Abnormal vaginal discharge; differential includes candidiasis, bacterial vaginosis, trichomoniasis, gonorrhea/chlamydia cervicitis, and PID - exclude upper-tract infection.

triage: Assess discharge color/odor/itching and duration
Screen for fever, pelvic/abdominal pain (PID concern)
Pregnancy status and LMP
Vitals; usually stable/low acuity
Sexual history and STI risk factors
nursing: Prepare pelvic/speculum exam tray and chaperone
Collect wet mount, pH, and NAAT swabs
Obtain urine for hCG and UA
Ensure privacy and comfort
Provide patient education materials
keyOrders: Wet mount microscopy (clue cells, hyphae, trichomonads)
labs: HIV/syphilis screening if STI risk*if indicated
imaging: Generally none required
Pelvic US only if PID/TOA suspected*if indicated
meds: Bacterial vaginosis: metronidazole 500 mg PO BID x7d or metronidazole gel
Candidiasis: fluconazole 150 mg PO single dose or topical azole
Trichomoniasis: metronidazole 500 mg PO BID x7d (treat partners)
Gonorrhea: ceftriaxone 500 mg IM; Chlamydia: doxycycline 100 mg BID x7d
Counsel: avoid alcohol with metronidazole
reassess: Confirm exam findings match diagnosis
Reassess for PID signs warranting escalation
Ensure pregnancy-safe regimen if pregnant
Review treatment and partner-management plan
disposition: Discharge nearly all with appropriate antimicrobials
Escalate/treat as PID if cervical motion or adnexal tenderness
Gyn referral if recurrent/refractory
Transfer rarely needed (only if complicated PID/TOA)
discharge: Complete full medication course; abstain from intercourse during treatment
Return precautions: fever, worsening pelvic pain, vomiting
Partner treatment and STI counseling
Safe-sex education and condom use
followup: PCP/Gyn for persistent or recurrent symptoms
STI test-of-cure where indicated (e.g., pregnancy, persistent symptoms)
Partner notification and treatment per CDC guidance

redFlags: Signs of PID (cervical motion/adnexal tenderness, fever)
Tubo-ovarian abscess or sepsis
Pregnancy with ascending infection
Toxic-appearing patient
Refractory infection despite appropriate therapy
compliance: EMTALA MSE applies even for low-acuity complaints
Texas mandatory DSHS reporting of gonorrhea, chlamydia, syphilis, and HIV
Expedited partner therapy permitted in Texas for chlamydia/gonorrhea per protocol
Observation/transfer rarely needed; transfer only complicated PID/TOA with appropriate-transfer documentation
Document chaperone presence for pelvic exam

name: Suicidal Ideation
summary: Suicidal thoughts with or without plan/intent/means; differentiate passive ideation from active plan with imminent risk.

triage: Bring directly back; do not leave in open waiting room
Columbia-Suicide Severity Rating Scale (C-SSRS) or PHQ-9 screen
Ask directly about plan, intent, access to means, prior attempts
Assess for intoxication/coingestion as contributing factor
Flag for 1:1 continuous observation
nursing: Initiate 1:1 sitter / continuous line-of-sight observation
Search patient and belongings; remove sharps, cords, belts, medications, lighters
Place in safe room (ligature-resistant) in patient gown
Document mood, affect, and statements verbatim
Secure personal effects and any brought-in medications
keyOrders: IV access* if indicated (suspected coingestion/medical workup)
Capture and lock up any weapons/medications brought by patient
Notify behavioral health / on-call psychiatry early
labs: Beta-hCG in persons of childbearing potential
imaging: Generally none for isolated SI
CT head* if indicated for trauma, altered mental status, or focal deficit
meds: Treat agitation only if needed: lorazepam 1-2 mg PO/IM/IV
Haloperidol 5 mg IM + lorazepam 2 mg IM* if indicated for severe agitation
Continue home psychiatric meds if clinically appropriate
Avoid PRN opioids/sedatives that may worsen disinhibition
reassess: Repeat suicide risk assessment after intoxication clears
Reassess intent, plan, protective factors, and support system
Document collateral from family/EMS/outpatient providers
Stratify risk: low (passive) vs high (active plan/intent/means)
disposition: Low risk with safety plan and support: discharge to outpatient follow-up
Moderate-high risk: psychiatric evaluation and inpatient admission
FSED has no psych beds: arrange appropriate transfer to psychiatric facility
Consider emergency detention (peace-officer or physician application) if refusing and high risk
discharge: Only if cleared by formal risk assessment and intoxication resolved
Provide written safety plan and crisis resources (988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline)
Means restriction counseling with family/caregiver
Ensure responsible adult escort and lethal-means removal at home
Document capacity and shared decision-making
followup: Outpatient psychiatry / counseling within 7 days
Primary care or behavioral health warm handoff
Crisis line and local mental health authority contact info
Return precautions for worsening thoughts or new plan

redFlags: Active plan with intent and access to lethal means
Recent attempt or rehearsal behavior
Command hallucinations to self-harm
Refusal to engage with inability to contract for safety
Coingestion or unstable vitals requiring medical stabilization
compliance: EMTALA: perform medical screening exam to rule out organic cause before psych disposition; stabilize coingestion/medical emergency first
FSED with no psychiatric capability must arrange appropriate transfer with accepting facility, qualified personnel, and records per EMTALA
Document observation-vs-transfer decision and continuous monitoring
Texas emergency detention: physician may execute application for emergency detention or notify peace officer if patient is a danger and refuses voluntary care (Texas Health & Safety Code Ch. 573)
Report suspected abuse/neglect if disclosed during evaluation

name: Homicidal Ideation
summary: Thoughts/threats of harming others; assess plan, intent, identifiable target, means, and underlying psychosis or intoxication.

triage: Bring to secure area; alert staff and security immediately
Assess for plan, identifiable target, intent, and access to weapons
Screen for intoxication, psychosis, and prior violence
Limit access to objects usable as weapons
Flag for continuous observation and de-escalation
nursing: Initiate 1:1 observation with security present
Search and remove weapons, sharps, and dangerous items
Patient in gown in safe/ligature-resistant room
Document statements verbatim and named targets
Maintain de-escalating, low-stimulation environment
keyOrders: IV access* if indicated for medical/sedation needs
Notify facility risk management / legal re: duty to warn
imaging: Generally none for isolated HI
CT head* if indicated for trauma, AMS, or focal neuro findings
meds: Lorazepam 1-2 mg PO/IM/IV for agitation/anxiety
Haloperidol 5 mg IM + lorazepam 2 mg IM* if indicated for severe agitation
Olanzapine 10 mg IM or ziprasidone 10-20 mg IM as alternatives
Continue home antipsychotics if appropriate
reassess: Reassess after intoxication/psychosis treated
Re-evaluate intent, plan, and identifiability of target
Obtain collateral history
Stratify acute violence risk
disposition: Credible threat to identifiable victim: psychiatric admission and likely law-enforcement notification
FSED without psych beds: appropriate transfer to psychiatric/inpatient facility
Emergency detention if dangerous and refusing care
Low risk with no plan/intent: outpatient referral with documentation
discharge: Only after formal risk assessment clears patient
Document absence of plan, intent, and means
Provide crisis and behavioral health resources
Ensure safe environment and support; return precautions
followup: Outpatient psychiatry within 7 days
Local mental health authority / crisis services
Anger management or counseling referral as appropriate
Return precautions for escalating thoughts

redFlags: Specific plan with identifiable intended victim and means
Active psychosis with command hallucinations to harm others
History of violence with current intent
Access to firearms or weapons
Escalating agitation despite de-escalation
compliance: EMTALA: medical screening exam to exclude organic cause; stabilize before disposition

Texas Tarasoff-equivalent: limited duty to warn/protect — may disclose to medical/law enforcement and the identifiable potential victim when patient makes a serious threat of imminent physical harm (Texas Health & Safety Code 611.004)
Document notification of intended victim and/or law enforcement
FSED without psychiatric capability must arrange appropriate transfer with accepting facility per EMTALA
Texas emergency detention may apply if patient is dangerous and refuses voluntary admission (HSC Ch. 573)

name: Acute Agitation / Psychosis
summary: Severe behavioral disturbance; must exclude organic causes (hypoxia, hypoglycemia, sepsis, intoxication, head injury) before assuming primary psychiatric cause.

triage: Rapid safety assessment; call security and additional staff
Vital signs including pulse oximetry and temperature
Fingerstick glucose immediately
Attempt verbal de-escalation first
Clear room of hazards; ensure staff exit path
nursing: 1:1 observation; prepare physical restraints only if least-restrictive measures fail
Apply cardiac monitor and continuous pulse oximetry once safe
Establish IV access when feasible
Document restraint indication, type, and frequent neurovascular checks
Search for weapons and remove hazards
keyOrders: Cardiac monitor, continuous SpO2, frequent vitals
Restraint order with time limits per protocol* if indicated
labs: Beta-hCG in persons of childbearing potential
imaging: CT head* if indicated for trauma, focal deficit, new-onset psychosis, or persistent AMS
CXR* if indicated for hypoxia/aspiration
meds: Midazolam 5 mg IM or 2-5 mg IV for rapid control
Haloperidol 5-10 mg IM + lorazepam 2 mg IM (combo) for severe agitation
Olanzapine 10 mg IM (avoid combining IM with parenteral benzo) or ziprasidone 10-20 mg IM
Ketamine 4-5 mg/kg IM* if indicated for severe/excited-delirium agitation (monitor airway)
Diphenhydramine 25-50 mg + benztropine for EPS/dystonia* if indicated
reassess: Continuous monitoring of airway, sedation level, SpO2, vitals
Reassess every 15 min during restraint/sedation; release restraints as soon as safe
Recheck glucose and treat reversible causes
Repeat ECG/QTc after antipsychotics
Watch for over-sedation and respiratory depression
disposition: Identify and treat organic cause if found (medical admission/transfer)
Primary psychiatric cause stabilized: psychiatric evaluation/admission
FSED without capability: appropriate transfer once medically stabilized
ICU-level transfer if airway compromise or refractory agitation
discharge: Only if fully sober, organically cleared, and risk-assessed
Confirm return to baseline mental status
Responsible adult escort and follow-up arranged
Provide medication and return precautions
followup: Psychiatry follow-up and medication reconciliation
Outpatient or community mental health linkage
Substance use referral if applicable
Return precautions for recurrence

redFlags: Hypoxia, hypoglycemia, hyperthermia, or unstable vitals (organic emergency)
Excited delirium with extreme agitation, hyperthermia, and metabolic acidosis
New-onset psychosis with focal neurologic findings
Respiratory depression or over-sedation after chemical restraint
Rhabdomyolysis or QTc prolongation
compliance: EMTALA: agitation is a medical emergency — perform MSE and exclude/stabilize organic causes before psychiatric disposition
Restraint/seclusion documentation per CMS/Texas rules: least-restrictive means, physician order, time-limited, frequent monitoring
FSED must stabilize then arrange appropriate transfer (accepting facility, qualified personnel) if no inpatient/psych capability
Document chemical-restraint rationale and continuous monitoring
Report suspected abuse/neglect or assault if disclosed

name: Anxiety / Panic Attack
summary: Acute anxiety/panic with somatic symptoms; must exclude ACS, PE, arrhythmia, hypoglycemia, hyperthyroidism, and intoxication/withdrawal.

triage: Vital signs including SpO2 and temperature
Screen for chest pain, dyspnea, palpitations to risk-stratify cardiac/PE
Calm, low-stimulation environment; reassurance
Assess for substance use, caffeine, stimulants, or withdrawal
Screen for suicidal ideation
nursing: Place on monitor if cardiopulmonary symptoms
Coach paced breathing; avoid paper-bag rebreathing
Quiet room and supportive presence
Reassess vitals after de-escalation
Document mental status and triggers
keyOrders: Cardiac monitor* if indicated for tachyarrhythmia
labs: Troponin* if indicated for chest pain/cardiac risk
D-dimer* if indicated when PE suspected (per Wells/PERC)
imaging: Generally none
CXR* if indicated for dyspnea/abnormal exam
CT pulmonary angiogram* if indicated for PE risk after risk stratification
meds: Lorazepam 0.5-1 mg PO/IV for acute severe symptoms (limit/avoid in substance-use risk)
Hydroxyzine 25-50 mg PO as non-benzo option
Continue/start SSRI as outpatient bridge (not ED-initiated acutely)
Treat underlying cause if identified
reassess: Reassess symptoms and vitals after intervention
Confirm resolution of tachycardia/tachypnea
Re-screen for SI and capacity for outpatient care
Confirm no missed organic diagnosis
disposition: Most discharge home after symptom resolution and reassurance
Admit/transfer if cardiac, PE, or other organic emergency identified
Refer to behavioral health for recurrent panic disorder
Reassess disposition if comorbid SI present
discharge: Reassurance and education on panic/anxiety physiology
Breathing and grounding techniques handout
Limit caffeine/stimulants; avoid alcohol
Short-term anxiolytic only if appropriate; counsel dependence risk
Return precautions for chest pain, syncope, dyspnea
followup: Primary care within 1-2 weeks
Outpatient behavioral health / therapy (CBT)
Psychiatry referral for recurrent panic disorder
Reinforce safety-netting

redFlags: Chest pain, ECG changes, or elevated troponin (ACS)
Hypoxia or pleuritic pain suggesting PE
Syncope or sustained tachyarrhythmia
Signs of hyperthyroid storm or stimulant toxicity
Concurrent suicidal ideation
compliance: EMTALA: complete MSE to exclude life-threatening cardiopulmonary causes before attributing to anxiety
Document negative cardiac/PE workup or risk-stratification rationale
FSED should arrange appropriate transfer if organic emergency requires admission
Screen and document suicide risk before discharge
Counsel benzodiazepine dependence/diversion risk if prescribed

name: Substance Intoxication
summary: Acute intoxication from alcohol/stimulants/opioids/sedatives/hallucinogens; protect airway, exclude trauma and coingestion, and ensure safe sobering.

triage: Vital signs, SpO2, temperature, fingerstick glucose
Assess airway, breathing, and level of consciousness
Identify substance(s), time, and route if possible
Evaluate for trauma and concurrent medical illness
Screen for SI/HI once able to participate
nursing: Continuous observation; position to protect airway (recovery position)
Cardiac monitor and continuous pulse oximetry
IV access and fall precautions
Frequent reorientation and reassessment
Search and secure belongings/substances
keyOrders: Naloxone at bedside* if indicated for opioid component
labs: Acetaminophen and salicylate levels (rule out coingestion)
Serum osmolality / osm gap* if indicated (toxic alcohols)
Beta-hCG in persons of childbearing potential
imaging: CT head* if indicated for trauma, AMS out of proportion, or focal deficit
CXR* if indicated for aspiration/hypoxia
meds: Naloxone 0.04-0.4 mg IV titrated (or 2-4 mg IN) for opioid intoxication/respiratory depression
Lorazepam 1-2 mg IV for stimulant-related agitation/seizure
IV isotonic fluids for dehydration
Thiamine 100 mg IV before glucose if alcohol/malnutrition suspected
Antiemetic (ondansetron 4 mg IV)* if indicated
reassess: Serial neuro and respiratory assessments until clinically sober
Trend mental status toward baseline
Re-screen for SI/HI and capacity when sober
Watch for sedative re-narcotization (e.g., long-acting opioids)
disposition: Observe until clinically sober, ambulatory, and tolerating PO
Admit/transfer if airway risk, persistent AMS, or serious coingestion
Psychiatric evaluation if SI/HI persists after sobering
Refer to substance use treatment
discharge: Only when clinically sober with stable vitals and intact gait/judgment
Confirm no SI/HI and decision-making capacity
Responsible adult escort; no driving
Provide substance use resources and naloxone kit if opioid-related
Return precautions for recurrence/decompensation
followup: Substance use disorder / addiction medicine referral
Primary care follow-up
Behavioral health if comorbid psychiatric illness
Harm-reduction and naloxone education

redFlags: Respiratory depression or airway compromise
Persistent or worsening altered mental status
Hyperthermia, seizures, or unstable arrhythmia (stimulants/serotonin syndrome)
Suspected toxic alcohol or large coingestion
Trauma with intoxication masking injury
compliance: EMTALA: MSE and stabilization; intoxicated patients lack capacity to refuse — cannot discharge until clinically sober and safe
Document capacity assessment and clinical sobriety prior to discharge
FSED must arrange appropriate transfer for ICU/medical admission needs (airway, severe toxicity)
Offer naloxone and harm reduction; report per public health requirements where applicable
Screen for SI/HI and abuse/trafficking indicators; report suspected abuse as mandated

name: Overdose / Poisoning
summary: Intentional or accidental ingestion; identify agent and timing, support ABCs, give antidotes, and consult Poison Control early.

triage: Immediate ABC assessment; vitals, SpO2, temperature, glucose
Identify substance(s), amount, time, and intent
Call Poison Control (1-800-222-1222) early
Continuous observation; assume worsening course
Bring pill bottles/containers for identification
nursing: Cardiac monitor, continuous pulse oximetry, frequent vitals
Two large-bore IVs
Prepare airway equipment and antidotes
1:1 observation (intentional ingestion = self-harm risk)
Document time of ingestion and interventions
keyOrders: Cardiac monitor, continuous SpO2, frequent neuro checks
Supplemental O2 / airway management as needed
IV access x2 with isotonic fluids
Activated charcoal* if indicated within ~1 hr, airway protected
labs: Acetaminophen and salicylate levels (mandatory in all intentional ODs)
Serum osmolality and osm gap (toxic alcohols)
Specific drug levels (digoxin, lithium, valproate, etc.)* if indicated
Beta-hCG in persons of childbearing potential
imaging: CXR* if indicated for aspiration/pulmonary edema
CT head* if indicated for AMS/trauma
Abdominal XR* if indicated for radiopaque tablets/body packing
meds: N-acetylcysteine per protocol for acetaminophen toxicity (150 mg/kg load)
Naloxone 0.04-2 mg IV titrated for opioid toxicity
Sodium bicarbonate 1-2 mEq/kg IV bolus for wide-QRS TCA toxicity
Flumazenil generally avoided (seizure risk); benzos for stimulant/sympathomimetic toxicity
Agent-specific antidotes per Poison Control (e.g., glucagon, calcium, fomepizole, lipid emulsion)
reassess: Serial vitals, mental status, ECG, and respiratory status
Trend repeat acetaminophen/salicylate levels and labs
Anticipate delayed toxicity (sustained-release, late-peaking agents)
Continuous re-evaluation per Poison Control guidance
Reassess psychiatric risk once medically stabilized
disposition: Most intentional ODs require admission/observation and psychiatric evaluation
ICU transfer for hemodynamic instability, airway, dysrhythmia, or antidote infusions
FSED: stabilize and arrange appropriate higher-level transfer
Discharge only for low-risk minimal ingestion after adequate observation and psych clearance
discharge: Rare; only after toxicologic and psychiatric clearance
Document Poison Control concurrence and observation window completed
Means restriction and safety planning
Responsible adult escort and follow-up
Detailed return precautions
followup: Psychiatry / behavioral health (intentional ingestion)
Toxicology / Poison Control as advised
Primary care for ongoing monitoring
Substance use treatment referral if applicable

redFlags: Airway compromise, respiratory depression, or hypoventilation
Wide-complex dysrhythmia or hemodynamic instability
Severe acidosis or rising acetaminophen/salicylate levels
Seizures or rapidly declining mental status
Co-ingestant or delayed-release agent with delayed toxicity
compliance: EMTALA: high-acuity MSE and aggressive stabilization before any transfer
FSED must arrange appropriate transfer to facility with ICU/toxicology/psychiatric capability when stabilization exceeds resources
Document Poison Control consultation and stabilizing care provided
Intentional overdose triggers continuous observation and suicide risk protocol; consider Texas emergency detention if high-risk and refusing
Mandatory reporting: suspected abuse/neglect, sexual assault, or child/elder endangerment if disclosed

name: Alcohol Withdrawal
summary: Spectrum from tremor/anxiety to withdrawal seizures and delirium tremens; symptom-triggered benzodiazepines and risk stratification are key.

triage: Vital signs, temperature, fingerstick glucose
Quantify last drink, intake pattern, prior withdrawal/DTs/seizures
Initiate CIWA-Ar scoring
Assess for tremor, diaphoresis, agitation, hallucinations
Screen for concurrent trauma/infection
nursing: Continuous monitoring; fall and seizure precautions
Serial CIWA-Ar assessments
Cardiac monitor and IV access
Quiet, low-stimulation environment; frequent reorientation
Strict intake/output and neuro checks
keyOrders: Cardiac monitor, continuous SpO2, frequent vitals
labs: Acetaminophen/salicylate levels* if indicated for co-ingestion
Lipase* if indicated for abdominal pain
imaging: CT head* if indicated for seizure, trauma, or focal deficit
CXR* if indicated for suspected pneumonia/aspiration
meds: Lorazepam 2-4 mg IV symptom-triggered (preferred in hepatic impairment) per CIWA
Diazepam 5-10 mg IV q5-10 min for severe symptoms (front-loading)
Phenobarbital* if indicated for benzo-refractory withdrawal
Thiamine 100-500 mg IV before glucose; folate and multivitamin
Replete magnesium 2 g IV and correct electrolytes
Dexmedetomidine/propofol* if indicated for refractory DTs (ICU)
reassess: Repeat CIWA-Ar and titrate benzodiazepines to symptom control
Monitor for escalating agitation, hallucinations, or autonomic instability
Watch for over-sedation/respiratory depression
Reassess electrolytes and hydration
Identify progression toward delirium tremens
disposition: Mild (CIWA <8-10) with good support: discharge with outpatient detox referral
Moderate-severe or escalating CIWA: admission for symptom-triggered therapy
Delirium tremens / refractory withdrawal: ICU-level care — transfer from FSED
Withdrawal seizure or comorbid illness: admit/transfer
discharge: Only for mild, controlled symptoms with reliable follow-up and support
Thiamine and multivitamin prescription
Counsel relapse and withdrawal-recurrence risks
Provide outpatient detox/addiction resources
Return precautions for seizures, confusion, fever, worsening tremor
followup: Addiction medicine / outpatient detox program
Primary care within days for electrolyte and clinical recheck
Behavioral health for alcohol use disorder
Consider naltrexone/acamprosate referral

redFlags: Delirium tremens: confusion, severe autonomic instability, hallucinations
Withdrawal seizures (especially repeated or status)
Benzodiazepine-refractory agitation
Hyperthermia, severe tachycardia, or hemodynamic instability
Concurrent infection, pancreatitis, GI bleed, or head injury
compliance: EMTALA: MSE and stabilization; exclude concurrent emergencies (trauma, sepsis, GI bleed)
FSED must arrange appropriate transfer to ICU-capable facility for DTs or refractory withdrawal
Document CIWA-driven treatment, observation-vs-transfer decision, and capacity
Administer thiamine before glucose to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy and document
Mandatory reporting: suspected abuse/neglect or impaired-driving concerns where applicable; offer SUD resources

name: Cough
summary: Acute vs chronic cough; differentiate benign viral/post-nasal/GERD from pneumonia, TB, PE, CHF, or malignancy.

triage: Vitals incl SpO2 and temperature
Duration (acute <3wk vs chronic), fever, hemoptysis, weight loss, TB risk/travel
Mask patient if febrile cough or TB concern; place in respiratory isolation if indicated
ESI 4 if well-appearing/afebrile; escalate if hypoxic or toxic
nursing: Pulse oximetry
Offer mask and tissues; hand hygiene
IV/labs only if systemic illness suspected
Reassess if febrile or distressed
keyOrders: CXR* if indicated (focal findings, fever, hemoptysis, smoker, >3wk)
12-lead ECG* if indicated (cough with cardiac features)
labs: Usually none for simple acute cough
Respiratory viral / influenza / COVID PCR* if indicated
Sputum AFB x3* if indicated (TB suspicion)
imaging: Chest x-ray* if indicated (abnormal exam, hemoptysis, chronic, immunocompromised)
CT chest* if indicated (suspected malignancy/persistent infiltrate)
meds: Dextromethorphan 10-20 mg PO q4h prn (symptomatic)
Guaifenesin 200-400 mg PO q4h prn (expectorant)
Albuterol inhaler 2 puffs q4-6h prn* if indicated (bronchospastic/post-viral)
Intranasal fluticasone* if indicated (post-nasal drip)
Antibiotics only if bacterial pneumonia/pertussis confirmed* if indicated
reassess: Reassess SpO2 and work of breathing
Confirm no red-flag features before discharge
Review CXR if obtained
disposition: Discharge most simple acute cough
Admit/transfer if pneumonia with hypoxia, hemoptysis, or systemic toxicity
Isolation/public-health pathway if active TB suspected
discharge: Antibiotic stewardship counseling (most acute cough is viral)
Smoking cessation resources if smoker
Return for hemoptysis, dyspnea, high fever, or cough >3 weeks
followup: PCP within 1-2 weeks for chronic/persistent cough
Pulmonology referral if chronic or abnormal imaging

redFlags: Hemoptysis, unintentional weight loss, or night sweats
Hypoxia or focal consolidation
Cough with syncope or chest pain suggesting cardiac/PE cause
Immunocompromised host with new infiltrate
compliance: EMTALA MSE for all; assess and stabilize hypoxia before discharge
Suspected active tuberculosis is a reportable condition to Texas DSHS / local health authority; initiate airborne precautions and report
Report reportable communicable diseases (e.g., pertussis, novel influenza) per Texas notifiable conditions list
Document stewardship rationale; arrange appropriate transfer if isolation-level admission cannot be provided at FSED

name: Hemoptysis
summary: Coughing blood ranging from blood-streaked sputum to massive hemoptysis; etiologies include infection, malignancy, PE, bronchiectasis, and coagulopathy.

triage: Estimate volume; massive (>=100-200 mL/24h or hemodynamic compromise) = ESI 1-2
Vitals + SpO2; assess airway patency
Position bleeding-side down if lateralizing source known
Mask/isolate if TB risk
nursing: Continuous cardiac + pulse ox monitoring
Two large-bore IVs; type & screen
Suction at bedside; airway cart available
Strict quantification of expectorated blood
Prepare for possible intubation
imaging: Chest x-ray (initial localization)
CT chest with contrast / CT angiography (source and cause)
CTPA* if indicated (suspected PE)
meds: Reverse anticoagulation* if indicated (vitamin K 10 mg IV, PCC, FFP per agent)
Tranexamic acid 1 g IV or 500 mg-1 g nebulized* if indicated
Antibiotics* if indicated (infectious source)
Cough suppressant (codeine 10-20 mg PO)* if indicated (minor hemoptysis)
Blood products to transfusion targets* if indicated
reassess: Continuously reassess airway and bleeding rate
Trend Hgb and vitals
Early consult IR/bronchoscopy/thoracic surgery for massive bleed
Low threshold to intubate to protect airway
disposition: Massive or ongoing hemoptysis: stabilize and transfer to facility with IR/bronchoscopy/thoracic surgery
Admit moderate hemoptysis or concerning imaging
Discharge only minor blood-streaking with benign cause and reliable follow-up
discharge: Strict return precautions for increasing blood volume or dyspnea
Smoking cessation counseling
Ensure outpatient imaging/scope is scheduled
followup: Pulmonology within 1-2 weeks (bronchoscopy/CT correlation)
Oncology referral if malignancy suspected

redFlags: Massive hemoptysis (>=100-200 mL/24h) or hemodynamic instability
Hypoxia or inability to clear airway
Anticoagulated or coagulopathic patient
Suspected active TB or malignancy
compliance: EMTALA MSE and stabilization; FSED lacks bronchoscopy/IR/OR so massive hemoptysis requires emergent appropriate transfer after airway/hemodynamic stabilization
Document transfer acceptance, mode, and risk-benefit; send copies of imaging/labs
Suspected active TB is reportable to Texas DSHS; institute airborne isolation
Capacity assessment: FSED is freestanding (no on-site OR/ICU); arrange higher level of care proactively

name: Asthma Exacerbation
summary: Acute reversible bronchospasm; severity-stratified treatment per GINA/NAEPP with early bronchodilators and systemic steroids.

triage: Vitals + SpO2; assess severity (speech, accessory muscles, RR)
ESI 2 if severe distress, SpO2 <90%, or silent chest (ESI 1 if peri-arrest)
Peak flow if able (% predicted/personal best)
Begin O2 to keep SpO2 >=94%
nursing: Continuous pulse oximetry +/- cardiac monitor
Position upright; coach breathing
Establish IV access for moderate-severe
Reassess after each bronchodilator treatment
labs: VBG/ABG* if indicated (severe, fatigue, rising CO2)
CXR* if indicated (focal findings, fever, first presentation)
imaging: Chest x-ray* if indicated (suspected pneumonia, pneumothorax, or atypical)
meds: Albuterol 2.5-5 mg + ipratropium 0.5 mg nebulized; continuous neb if severe
Prednisone 40-60 mg PO or methylprednisolone 125 mg IV
Magnesium sulfate 2 g IV over 20 min* if indicated (severe)
Epinephrine 0.3-0.5 mg IM* if indicated (near-fatal/no air movement)
Terbutaline or IV/SC beta-agonist* if indicated (refractory)
reassess: Reassess RR, SpO2, peak flow, work of breathing after each treatment
Watch for fatigue/rising CO2 signaling failure
Trial BiPAP* if indicated; prepare for intubation if deteriorating
Determine response category (good/incomplete/poor)
disposition: Discharge if sustained good response, SpO2 >=94% RA, peak flow >=70% predicted
Observe incomplete responders 1-3h post-steroid
Admit/transfer poor responders, hypercarbia, or need for ICU/BiPAP
discharge: Prednisone 40-60 mg daily x5 days (or burst)
Resume/initiate inhaled corticosteroid + reliever; review technique
Written asthma action plan and return precautions
followup: PCP/asthma follow-up within 2-7 days
Pulmonology if frequent/severe exacerbations

redFlags: Silent chest, exhaustion, or altered mental status
SpO2 <90% or rising/normalizing PaCO2 with fatigue
Inability to speak, cyanosis, or bradycardia
Poor response to maximal therapy
compliance: EMTALA MSE and stabilization regardless of payment
FSED can fully treat most exacerbations; status asthmaticus needing ICU/intubation/BiPAP-not-available requires stabilization and appropriate transfer
Document response, ambulatory SpO2, and action plan if discharging
Provide Texas-required FSED billing/insurance disclosures

name: COPD Exacerbation
summary: Acute worsening of dyspnea/sputum in COPD; GOLD-guided bronchodilators, steroids, antibiotics, and controlled oxygen with NIV for hypercapnic failure.

triage: Vitals + SpO2; titrate O2 to target 88-92% to avoid CO2 narcosis
ESI 2 if severe distress/hypercarbia signs; ESI 1 if obtunded
Assess sputum change, fever, cardiac comorbidity
Look for accessory muscle use, pursed-lip breathing
nursing: Continuous cardiac + pulse ox monitoring
Controlled O2 (Venturi/NC) to SpO2 88-92%
IV access; draw labs
Prepare NIV (BiPAP) setup
keyOrders: BiPAP at bedside* if indicated (hypercapnic failure)
imaging: Chest x-ray (rule out pneumonia, pneumothorax, CHF)
Bedside POCUS* if indicated (cardiac/lung)
CTPA* if indicated (suspected PE)
meds: Albuterol 2.5 mg + ipratropium 0.5 mg nebulized, repeat q20min prn
Prednisone 40 mg PO daily x5 days (or methylprednisolone 125 mg IV)
Antibiotic (azithromycin 500 mg or doxycycline 100 mg BID, or amox-clav) for increased/purulent sputum
Magnesium sulfate 2 g IV* if indicated (severe bronchospasm)
BiPAP for respiratory acidosis (pH <7.35, rising CO2)* if indicated
reassess: Reassess work of breathing, SpO2, mental status
Repeat VBG after NIV initiation
Watch for CO2 narcosis with over-oxygenation
Escalate to intubation if NIV fails or patient obtunded
disposition: Discharge mild exacerbation with good response and baseline O2 status
Admit/transfer for hypercarbia, NIV need, hypoxia, or comorbid decompensation
Observe borderline responders
discharge: Steroid course + antibiotics as prescribed; inhaler technique review
Smoking cessation counseling and resources
Confirm home O2 plan; strict return precautions for worsening dyspnea/confusion
followup: PCP/pulmonology within 1-2 weeks
Pulmonary rehab and vaccination (influenza, pneumococcal) referral

redFlags: Worsening hypercapnia, respiratory acidosis, or altered mental status
Failure of/intolerance to NIV
Hemodynamic instability or new arrhythmia
SpO2 persistently below patient baseline despite controlled O2
compliance: EMTALA MSE and stabilization; controlled oxygenation begun before transfer discussion
FSED must arrange appropriate transfer for ICU-level/prolonged NIV or intubated patients (accepting MD, qualified transport)
Document O2 titration rationale and observation-vs-transfer decision
Report reportable respiratory infections (e.g., influenza outbreaks, TB) per Texas DSHS

name: Pneumonia
summary: Lower respiratory tract infection; risk-stratify with CURB-65/PSI and screen for sepsis to guide antibiotics and disposition.

triage: Vitals + SpO2; screen for sepsis (qSOFA/SIRS)
ESI 2 if hypoxic/septic; ESI 3 if stable
Mask if febrile cough; isolate if TB/COVID risk
Calculate CURB-65 risk
nursing: Continuous pulse oximetry +/- cardiac monitor
IV access; draw labs incl lactate if septic
Supplemental O2 to SpO2 >=94% (88-92% if COPD)
Obtain cultures before antibiotics if feasible
keyOrders: Blood cultures x2 before antibiotics* if indicated (severe/septic)
labs: Respiratory viral / COVID / influenza PCR* if indicated
Sputum gram stain & culture* if indicated
imaging: Chest x-ray (PA/lateral or portable)
Bedside lung POCUS* if indicated
CT chest* if indicated (complication, equivocal, immunocompromised)
meds: Outpatient CAP: amoxicillin 1 g TID or doxycycline 100 mg BID (+/- macrolide per risk)
Inpatient CAP: ceftriaxone 1-2 g IV + azithromycin 500 mg IV (or respiratory fluoroquinolone)
30 mL/kg balanced crystalloid for sepsis-induced hypoperfusion* if indicated
Antipyretics: acetaminophen 1 g PO/IV
Antivirals (oseltamivir 75 mg BID)* if indicated (influenza)
reassess: Reassess vitals and lactate after fluids/antibiotics
Confirm timely antibiotic administration
Reassess O2 requirement and work of breathing
Re-stratify disposition with CURB-65/clinical course
disposition: Discharge low-risk (CURB-65 0-1) with reliable follow-up and normal SpO2
Admit/transfer CURB-65 >=2, hypoxia, sepsis, or comorbidity
ICU-level (septic shock, respiratory failure): stabilize and transfer
discharge: Complete oral antibiotic course; antipyretics; hydration
Return precautions for worsening dyspnea, persistent fever, confusion
Confirm ambulatory SpO2 acceptable before discharge
followup: PCP within 48-72h to confirm improvement
Repeat CXR in 6-8 weeks for smokers/>50 to ensure resolution

redFlags: Septic shock, hypotension, or lactate >2-4
SpO2 <90% / respiratory failure
Altered mental status or multilobar involvement
Immunocompromise or failure to respond to therapy
compliance: EMTALA MSE and stabilization; begin sepsis bundle and antibiotics before transfer when indicated
FSED stabilizes and arranges appropriate transfer for admission/ICU (accepting facility, antibiotics started, records sent)
Report reportable pneumonias/pathogens (TB, Legionella, novel influenza, COVID per current rules) to Texas DSHS
Document CURB-65, O2 status, first-dose antibiotic time, and disposition rationale

name: Upper Respiratory Infection
summary: Self-limited viral infection of nose/throat/sinuses; goal is symptom relief, stewardship, and excluding bacterial or serious mimics.

triage: Vitals + SpO2; usually ESI 4-5 if well-appearing
Screen for red flags: dyspnea, drooling, stridor, neck stiffness
Mask patient; hand hygiene
Brief symptom and duration history
nursing: Pulse oximetry if any respiratory complaint
Comfort measures (fluids, lozenges)
No routine IV/labs
Reassess if febrile/toxic
keyOrders: Rapid strep / influenza / COVID test* if indicated
Throat exam to exclude peritonsillar abscess/epiglottitis
labs: Rapid strep + reflex culture* if indicated (Centor criteria)
imaging: None routinely
Soft-tissue neck x-ray or CT* if indicated (suspected abscess/epiglottitis)
meds: Acetaminophen 650-1000 mg PO q6h or ibuprofen 400-600 mg PO q6h prn
Saline nasal spray and oral hydration
Pseudoephedrine 30-60 mg PO q4-6h or intranasal oxymetazoline (<=3 days)* if indicated
Throat lozenges / benzocaine spray for sore throat
Oseltamivir 75 mg PO BID x5d* if indicated (early influenza, high risk)
reassess: Confirm no airway/red-flag features before discharge
Reassess hydration and ability to tolerate PO
Review any rapid test results
disposition: Discharge nearly all uncomplicated URIs
Reconsider/admit if bacterial complication, dehydration, or airway concern
Transfer if deep-space neck infection or epiglottitis suspected
discharge: Antibiotic stewardship counseling (viral, no antibiotics)
Symptom timeline (typically 7-10 days) and supportive care
Return for difficulty breathing/swallowing, high or prolonged fever, neck swelling
followup: PCP if symptoms persist >10 days or worsen
ENT referral for recurrent/complicated cases

redFlags: Stridor, drooling, muffled voice, or tripoding (epiglottitis)
Trismus or unilateral severe sore throat (peritonsillar abscess)
Neck stiffness, severe headache, or rash (meningitis)
Hypoxia or significant dyspnea
compliance: EMTALA MSE for every patient even for minor complaints before any payment discussion
Document stewardship counseling; avoid unnecessary antibiotics
Report notifiable infections (influenza of public-health concern, COVID per current rules) to Texas DSHS
Airway emergencies (epiglottitis) require stabilization and appropriate transfer to facility with airway/ENT/OR capability

name: Suspected Pulmonary Embolism
summary: Suspected venous thromboembolism to the pulmonary arteries; risk-stratify with Wells/PERC, confirm with CTPA, and assess hemodynamic stability for thrombolysis.

triage: Vitals + SpO2; ESI 2 (ESI 1 if hypotensive/arrest)
Apply continuous monitoring and O2 to SpO2 >=94%
Calculate Wells score; apply PERC if low-risk
Screen for hemodynamic instability
nursing: Continuous cardiac + pulse ox monitoring
Two IVs; draw labs
Frequent BP/vitals (watch for obstructive shock)
Prepare for possible thrombolysis/code
labs: D-dimer (if Wells low/intermediate and not PERC-negative)
imaging: CT pulmonary angiogram (confirmatory)
V/Q scan* if indicated (contrast allergy/renal failure)
Bedside echo/POCUS for RV strain (massive PE)
Lower-extremity venous duplex* if indicated
meds: Therapeutic anticoagulation: enoxaparin 1 mg/kg SC q12h or UFH bolus 80 U/kg then 18 U/kg/h
Apixaban 10 mg PO BID* if indicated (confirmed low-risk PE)
Thrombolytic (alteplase 100 mg IV over 2h)* if indicated (massive PE / arrest)
Norepinephrine for obstructive shock* if indicated
Cautious fluid bolus 250-500 mL* if indicated (avoid RV overload)
reassess: Reassess hemodynamics frequently; escalate for shock
Risk-stratify (sPESI, RV strain, troponin) to guide disposition
Initiate empiric anticoagulation if high pretest probability and no contraindication while awaiting CTPA
Reassess oxygenation and perfusion
disposition: Admit confirmed PE; ICU/transfer for massive/submassive PE
Stabilize and transfer if thrombolysis/embolectomy/ECMO needed and unavailable
Outpatient anticoagulation* if indicated (low-risk sPESI 0, shared decision, reliable follow-up)
discharge: Anticoagulant teaching, dosing, adherence, bleeding precautions
Strict return for worsening dyspnea, chest pain, syncope, bleeding
Confirm low-risk criteria and prompt follow-up before any discharge
followup: Anticoagulation clinic / PCP within 24-72h
Hematology if unprovoked/recurrent VTE

redFlags: Hypotension/obstructive shock (massive PE)
Hypoxia refractory to O2 or syncope
RV strain on echo/ECG with elevated troponin (submassive)
Cardiac arrest or peri-arrest state
compliance: EMTALA MSE and stabilization; begin anticoagulation/resuscitation before transfer when indicated
FSED must stabilize and arrange appropriate transfer for massive/submassive PE needing thrombolysis, IR, embolectomy, or ICU (accepting MD, qualified transport)
Document Wells/PERC/sPESI, contraindications to anticoagulation, and shared decision-making
Observation-vs-transfer: low-risk confirmed PE may be candidate for transfer to admitting facility vs outpatient pathway per protocol

name: Airway Foreign Body
summary: Aspirated or impacted airway foreign body; ranges from complete obstruction (true emergency) to partial obstruction or distal lodgment requiring bronchoscopy.

triage: Immediate airway assessment; complete obstruction = ESI 1
Assess air movement, stridor, phonation, cyanosis, SpO2
If complete obstruction and conscious: abdominal thrusts immediately
If unconscious: begin CPR, inspect airway on ventilations
nursing: Continuous cardiac + pulse ox monitoring
Bring difficult-airway/code cart and suction to bedside
Establish IV access
Position of comfort for partial obstruction; do not agitate
Have Magill forceps and laryngoscope ready
keyOrders: Difficult-airway equipment + Magill forceps at bedside
Direct laryngoscopy for visualized FB removal* if indicated
imaging: Soft-tissue neck and chest x-ray (inspiratory/expiratory) once stable
CT neck/chest* if indicated (radiolucent FB, localization)
Bedside laryngoscopy for direct visualization* if indicated
meds: Nebulized racemic epinephrine* if indicated (laryngeal edema post-manipulation)
Dexamethasone 10 mg IV* if indicated (airway edema)
Procedural sedation/RSI agents (etomidate, ketamine, succinylcholine/rocuronium)* if indicated (definitive airway)
Heliox* if indicated (partial upper airway obstruction, temporizing)
reassess: Continuously reassess air movement, SpO2, and phonation
Confirm FB removal vs distal migration
Anticipate post-obstructive edema and complications
Early ENT/pulmonary/anesthesia consultation for bronchoscopy
disposition: Stabilize airway then transfer for rigid/flexible bronchoscopy or OR removal if FB distal/not retrievable
Admit/observe for airway edema after removal
Discharge only if FB cleared, airway normal, and observation uneventful
discharge: Counsel on aspiration prevention
Return immediately for dyspnea, stridor, fever, or recurrent symptoms
Document complete resolution and normal post-removal exam
followup: ENT/pulmonology follow-up if bronchoscopy performed or suspected retained FB
PCP for post-obstructive pneumonia monitoring

redFlags: Complete obstruction: no air movement, aphonia, cyanosis (immediate intervention)
Stridor, drooling, or progressive respiratory distress
Hypoxia or altered mental status
Distal FB not removable at bedside (needs bronchoscopy/OR)
compliance: EMTALA MSE and immediate stabilization of airway before any payment inquiry
FSED lacks bronchoscopy/OR/ENT on-site; secure airway and arrange emergent appropriate transfer (accepting facility, qualified airway-capable transport)
Document airway management, removal attempts, and transfer risk-benefit
Suspected abuse/neglect (e.g., intentional or supervision-related in vulnerable adult) is reportable to Texas DFPS/APS; report if indicated

name: Major Trauma / MVC
summary: High-energy blunt mechanism with potential for occult life-threatening hemorrhage and multisystem injury.

triage: Apply primary survey ABCDE on arrival; do not delay for registration (EMTALA)
Obtain mechanism: speed, restraint, airbag, ejection, rollover, death at scene
Full set of vitals + SpO2; recheck q5min if unstable
Activate trauma protocol/transfer pathway early given FSED resource limits
nursing: Expose patient fully; maintain normothermia with warming blanket
Maintain spinal motion restriction until cleared
Two large-bore (16-18G) IVs; draw labs on access
Strict I/O; place patient on continuous monitoring
keyOrders: Cardiac monitor, continuous SpO2, automated BP q5min
Two large-bore IVs; initiate warmed isotonic fluids judiciously
12-lead ECG if blunt cardiac injury suspected
NPO; foley if no urethral injury*; if indicated
labs: VBG, ethanol, urine/serum tox, hCG in women of childbearing age
imaging: FAST ultrasound for free fluid/pericardial blood
Portable CXR and pelvic X-ray
CT pan-scan (head/c-spine/chest/abd/pelvis)* if indicated and available
Transfer for CT if not on-site
meds: Tranexamic acid 1 g IV over 10 min then 1 g over 8 h if hemorrhagic and <3 h from injury
Fentanyl 25-50 mcg IV q5-10min titrated for pain
Ondansetron 4 mg IV for nausea
Tdap/Td if wounds present and not up to date
reassess: Repeat primary survey after each intervention
Trend lactate, base deficit, hemodynamics for occult shock
Reassess GCS and pupils serially
Repeat FAST if deterioration
disposition: Emergent transfer to Level I/II trauma center for unstable or operative injury
