Initiate massive transfusion/blood product if available while arranging transfer
Air vs ground transport based on stability and distance
discharge: Discharge only if truly minor mechanism with negative workup and reliable observation
Return precautions: worsening pain, dyspnea, abdominal distension, syncope
Provide written instructions and 24-h contact
followup: Trauma clinic or PCP within 24-48 h for cleared patients
Repeat imaging if delayed injury suspected
Document advice to avoid driving while on opioids

redFlags: SBP <90, tachycardia, or rising lactate suggesting occult hemorrhage
Distended/rigid abdomen or positive FAST
GCS <=8 or rapidly declining mental status
Mechanism: ejection, rollover, death of co-occupant, prolonged extrication
compliance: EMTALA MSE and stabilization required before any transfer
FSED is non-operative: emergent appropriate-transfer with physician certification of benefit > risk
Send copies of records, imaging, and labs with patient; physician-to-physician acceptance documented
Report MVC injuries per state requirements; preserve evidence if suspected impairment/crime

name: Head & Neck Trauma
summary: Blunt or penetrating injury to head/neck with risk of intracranial bleed, c-spine injury, or airway compromise.

triage: Assess GCS and pupils immediately; recheck serially
Maintain c-spine precautions until clinically/radiographically cleared
Identify anticoagulant/antiplatelet use and bleeding disorders
Screen for LOC, amnesia, vomiting, seizure, severe headache
nursing: Elevate head of bed 30 degrees if no spinal contraindication
Neuro checks q15-30min and document trend
IV access; keep NPO pending imaging
Control external scalp bleeding with direct pressure
keyOrders: 12-lead ECG if syncope/cardiac cause of fall
IV access; O2 to keep SpO2 >=94%
labs: CBC, CMP, coagulation panel (esp. if on anticoagulants)
Glucose to exclude metabolic cause of AMS
imaging: Non-contrast head CT per Canadian CT Head Rule
CT c-spine per NEXUS/Canadian C-spine criteria
CTA neck* if penetrating or seatbelt sign; if indicated
Transfer for CT if unavailable on-site
meds: Acetaminophen 1000 mg PO/IV for headache
Ondansetron 4 mg IV for vomiting
Reverse anticoagulation if ICH (e.g., 4-factor PCC 25-50 units/kg, vitamin K 10 mg IV)* if indicated
Keppra/levetiracetam 1000 mg IV for seizure prophylaxis in significant TBI* if indicated
reassess: Serial GCS, pupils, and focal neuro exam
Watch for Cushing response (HTN, bradycardia, irregular respirations)
Reassess for delayed bleed in anticoagulated patients
Repeat CT if neuro decline
disposition: Emergent transfer to facility with neurosurgery for any ICH or unstable c-spine
Intubate for GCS <=8 prior to transfer
Admit/observe minor TBI per shared decision if observation capacity exists
discharge: Discharge if negative CT, GCS 15, normal exam, reliable observer
Concussion precautions and graded return-to-activity instructions
Strict return precautions: worsening headache, repeated vomiting, confusion, weakness
followup: PCP or concussion clinic within 1 week
Neurosurgery follow-up if any abnormal finding
Avoid driving/anticoagulant resumption until cleared* if indicated

redFlags: GCS <=8, anisocoria, or new focal deficit
Anticoagulated patient with any intracranial bleed
Suspected expanding hematoma or basilar skull fracture signs
Penetrating neck wound with expanding hematoma, bruit, or airway distress
compliance: EMTALA MSE and stabilization; appropriate-transfer with neurosurgery acceptance documented
FSED lacks neurosurgery/ICU: low threshold for transfer of any ICH
Send CT images and reversal-agent administration record with patient
Report assault-related injuries and suspected abuse per Texas mandatory reporting

name: Penetrating Trauma
summary: Stab/GSW/impalement with risk of vascular, visceral, or cavity injury and rapid exsanguination.

triage: Primary survey; control compressible hemorrhage immediately with direct pressure/tourniquet
Identify wound number, location, weapon/caliber, and time of injury
Do not remove impaled objects; stabilize in place
Activate transfer/trauma pathway immediately for torso/junctional wounds
nursing: Expose fully; log-roll to find exit/posterior wounds; count all wounds
Two large-bore IVs; draw labs and type & cross
Apply occlusive dressing to open chest wounds
Continuous monitoring and frequent vitals
keyOrders: Cardiac monitor, continuous SpO2, BP q5min
NPO; tourniquet for extremity hemorrhage* if indicated
imaging: FAST for pericardial/peritoneal blood
CXR for pneumo/hemothorax (mark wounds with radiopaque markers)
CT angiography* if hemodynamically stable and available; if indicated
Transfer for definitive imaging/OR if unstable
meds: Tranexamic acid 1 g IV over 10 min if hemorrhagic and <3 h
Fentanyl 25-50 mcg IV titrated for pain
Cefazolin 2 g IV for wound prophylaxis* if indicated
Tdap/Td if not up to date
reassess: Repeat primary survey and hemodynamics frequently
Reassess for tension pneumothorax (decompress if present)
Trend lactate and mental status
Repeat FAST if deterioration
disposition: Emergent transfer to trauma center/OR for torso, vascular, or cardiac box wounds
Needle/finger decompression and chest tube if tension pneumothorax
Initiate blood products while arranging transport
discharge: Discharge only isolated superficial extremity wounds with negative workup
Wound care, infection precautions, and return instructions
Tetanus status documented
followup: Wound recheck in 48 h
Surgery/trauma follow-up for any deep or cavity-adjacent wound
Suture/staple removal timeline given

redFlags: Hemodynamic instability or positive FAST
Wound to cardiac box, neck zones, or junctional region
Expanding hematoma, pulsatile bleeding, or absent distal pulses
Evisceration or peritonitis
compliance: EMTALA MSE and stabilization before transfer; certify benefit > risk
FSED is non-operative: emergent appropriate-transfer to trauma center is standard
Mandatory law-enforcement reporting of gunshot and stab wounds in Texas; preserve clothing/evidence
Document chain of custody and notify police; send all records with patient

name: Fall
summary: Fall with injury spectrum from minor contusion to occult fracture/ICH, especially in elderly or anticoagulated.

triage: Determine height, surface, mechanism, and whether syncope/medical cause preceded
Identify anticoagulant/antiplatelet use and osteoporosis risk
Assess GCS, focal pain, and ability to bear weight
Screen elderly for elder abuse and recurrent falls
nursing: Vitals including orthostatics if syncope suspected
C-spine precautions if axial pain or AMS
IV access if significant injury anticipated
Pain assessment and immobilize suspected fractures
keyOrders: Cardiac monitor if syncope/medical cause suspected
12-lead ECG to evaluate for arrhythmia/cardiac syncope
NPO if surgical injury likely* if indicated
labs: Troponin/BNP if cardiac syncope suspected* if indicated
UA and infection workup if precipitating illness suspected* if indicated
imaging: Targeted X-rays of injured extremity/pelvis/hip
Non-contrast head CT if anticoagulated, AMS, or head strike (per CT Head Rule)
CT c-spine per NEXUS/Canadian criteria* if indicated
Transfer for CT if unavailable
meds: Acetaminophen 1000 mg PO/IV for pain
Fentanyl 25-50 mcg IV or morphine 0.1 mg/kg IV for fracture pain
Reverse anticoagulation if ICH (4-factor PCC)* if indicated
Tdap/Td if open wound and not up to date
reassess: Serial neuro checks if head strike/anticoagulated
Reassess pain control and neurovascular status of injured limb
Re-evaluate for delayed bleed in elderly on blood thinners
Reassess gait/safety before discharge
disposition: Transfer for ortho/neurosurgery for hip fracture, unstable fracture, or ICH
Admit/observe for high fall risk or precipitating medical cause
Arrange safe discharge with assistive devices and home assessment
discharge: Discharge if negative workup, ambulatory, and safe home environment
Fall-prevention counseling and review of medications
Return precautions for worsening pain, new neuro symptoms, or repeat fall
followup: PCP within 1 week for fall risk and medication review
Orthopedics for fractures; physical therapy referral
Consider DEXA/osteoporosis evaluation in elderly

redFlags: Anticoagulated patient with head strike (delayed ICH risk)
Inability to bear weight, deformity, or neurovascular compromise
Syncope/medical cause requiring cardiac workup
Signs of elder abuse or inconsistent history
compliance: EMTALA MSE and stabilization; appropriate-transfer for operative fractures/ICH
FSED lacks ortho/neurosurgery: arrange accepting facility and send imaging
Mandatory reporting of suspected elder abuse/neglect to Texas APS
Document home-safety and fall-prevention counseling

name: Laceration
summary: Traumatic skin/soft-tissue wound requiring hemostasis, exploration, and closure with infection prevention.

triage: Control bleeding with direct pressure; elevate
Assess mechanism, contamination, time since injury, and bite/crush component
Screen for tendon/nerve/vessel involvement and retained foreign body
Document tetanus immunization status
nursing: Irrigate and clean wound; assess depth
Neurovascular and motor check distal to wound
Pain control prior to exploration
Prepare repair tray and appropriate suture
keyOrders: Wound irrigation with normal saline (tap water acceptable)
Hemostasis with direct pressure/lidocaine with epi (avoid in end-arteries)
X-ray for radiopaque foreign body* if indicated
labs: CBC if significant blood loss* if indicated
Coagulation panel if anticoagulated and bleeding* if indicated
Wound culture only if clearly infected* if indicated
imaging: Plain X-ray if glass/metal foreign body suspected
Ultrasound for radiolucent (wood/organic) foreign body* if indicated
No imaging for clean simple lacerations
CT for deep/complex facial or suspected open fracture* if indicated
meds: Lidocaine 1% (with epi except digits/tip of nose/ears/penis) for local anesthesia
Tdap/Td if >5 y (dirty) or >10 y (clean); TIG if high-risk and unimmunized
Cephalexin 500 mg PO QID for high-risk/contaminated wounds* if indicated
Acetaminophen 1000 mg PO for pain
reassess: Confirm hemostasis and approximation after closure
Recheck distal neurovascular status post-repair
Assess for adequate anesthesia before suturing
Reassess wound tension and need for layered closure
disposition: Discharge most after repair
Refer/transfer for specialist repair (deep facial, tendon, nerve, vascular)
Consider hand surgery for complex hand/flexor tendon injuries
discharge: Wound care: keep clean/dry 24-48 h, watch for infection
Suture removal schedule (face 5 d, scalp/trunk 7-10 d, extremity 10-14 d)
Return precautions: redness, pus, spreading erythema, fever
followup: PCP or wound clinic for suture/staple removal
Hand/plastic surgery for complex repairs
Recheck in 48 h if high infection risk

redFlags: Arterial bleeding or expanding hematoma
Tendon, nerve, or joint capsule involvement
Signs of deep-space infection or necrotizing process
Heavily contaminated/bite wound over hand or near joint
compliance: EMTALA MSE for all presenting patients regardless of payment
FSED appropriate-transfer if specialist repair beyond capability
Report assault- or abuse-related wounds per Texas mandatory reporting
Document tetanus status and wound-care instructions given

name: Burns
summary: Thermal/chemical/electrical injury with risk of airway compromise, fluid loss, and need for burn-center transfer.

triage: Stop the burning process; remove clothing/jewelry; brush off dry chemicals
Assess airway for inhalation injury (soot, singed hairs, hoarseness, stridor)
Estimate %TBSA (Rule of Nines) and depth; note circumferential burns
Identify mechanism: enclosed-space fire, electrical, chemical
nursing: Cover burns with clean dry sheets; maintain normothermia
Large-bore IV access through unburned skin if possible
Continuous monitoring; foley for large-TBSA fluid tracking
Irrigate chemical burns copiously; remove contact lenses for eye exposure
keyOrders: High-flow O2; consider early airway control if inhalation injury
Cardiac monitor (esp. electrical burns for arrhythmia)
Two large-bore IVs; begin lactated Ringer's per Parkland
NPO; foley for TBSA >20%* if indicated
labs: Carboxyhemoglobin and ABG/VBG if enclosed-space fire
CK and UA for myoglobinuria (electrical/deep burns)
Coagulation panel and type & screen if extensive* if indicated
imaging: CXR if inhalation injury suspected
ECG/cardiac monitoring for electrical injury
Imaging for associated trauma (e.g., blast/fall)* if indicated
No routine imaging for isolated minor burns
meds: Lactated Ringer's: Parkland 2-4 mL/kg/%TBSA, half over first 8 h from time of burn
Fentanyl 25-50 mcg IV titrated for pain
100% oxygen for suspected CO poisoning; cyanide kit (hydroxocobalamin 5 g IV)* if indicated
Tdap/Td if not up to date; topical bacitracin/silver sulfadiazine for minor burns
reassess: Reassess airway repeatedly; intubate early if progressive edema
Monitor urine output goal 0.5 mL/kg/h (adult) and titrate fluids
Recheck distal perfusion for circumferential burns (escharotomy need)
Trend lactate and acid-base status
disposition: Transfer to burn center per ABA criteria (>10% TBSA, full-thickness, face/hands/feet/perineum/joints, electrical/chemical, inhalation)
Begin resuscitation and pain control before transport
Admit/observe minor burns only if reliable outpatient care unavailable
discharge: Discharge minor superficial/partial-thickness burns with wound care
Daily dressing changes; signs of infection to watch for
Adequate oral analgesia and hydration instructions
followup: Burn clinic or PCP within 24-48 h for wound recheck
Earlier follow-up for hand/face burns
Reassess depth at 48-72 h as burns evolve

redFlags: Stridor, hoarseness, soot in airway, or facial/neck burns (impending airway loss)
Circumferential full-thickness burns with distal ischemia
TBSA >10-20%, electrical, or chemical burns
Elevated carboxyhemoglobin or suspected cyanide toxicity
compliance: EMTALA MSE and stabilization; secure airway before transfer if at risk
FSED appropriate-transfer to ABA-verified burn center with documented acceptance
Report suspected abuse (esp. inconsistent pattern, immersion scald) per Texas law
Send fluid-resuscitation record, TBSA estimate, and time of burn with patient

name: Traumatic Amputation
summary: Partial/complete loss of digit or limb with hemorrhage risk and time-sensitive replantation potential.

triage: Control hemorrhage with direct pressure then tourniquet if uncontrolled
Note time of injury and warm/cold ischemia clock for amputated part
Identify mechanism (sharp/avulsion/crush) affecting replant candidacy
Activate hand/replant center pathway immediately
nursing: Wrap amputated part in saline-moistened gauze, seal in bag, place on ice (do NOT freeze or submerge directly)
Two large-bore IVs; draw labs and type & screen
Elevate and immobilize stump; document neurovascular status
Continuous monitoring and frequent vitals
keyOrders: Two large-bore IVs; warmed fluids/blood as needed
Preserve and label amputated part per protocol
imaging: X-ray of stump and amputated part (for fracture/replant planning)
CXR if associated major trauma* if indicated
CT angiography for proximal limb vascular mapping* if indicated
Transfer for definitive imaging if unavailable
meds: Tranexamic acid 1 g IV if significant hemorrhage and <3 h
Fentanyl 25-50 mcg IV or morphine 0.1 mg/kg IV titrated
Cefazolin 2 g IV for wound prophylaxis
Tdap/Td if not up to date
reassess: Reassess hemorrhage control and tourniquet time
Monitor hemodynamics and pain control
Re-document neurovascular status of stump
Confirm amputated part properly preserved and accompanying patient
disposition: Emergent transfer to replantation/hand-surgery center with the amputated part
Coordinate rapid transport to minimize ischemia time
Surgery consult/acceptance before transfer
discharge: Rarely discharged; only for minor distal tip amputations not requiring replant after specialist input
Wound care and strict infection/return precautions
Tetanus status documented
followup: Hand/plastic surgery follow-up arranged
Wound recheck in 48 h for non-operative cases
Occupational therapy/rehab referral

redFlags: Uncontrolled hemorrhage or hemodynamic instability
Proximal/major limb amputation
Prolonged warm ischemia time threatening replant viability
Crush/avulsion mechanism with extensive tissue loss
compliance: EMTALA MSE and stabilization before transfer; certify benefit > risk
FSED is non-operative: emergent appropriate-transfer to replant center with documented acceptance
Ensure amputated part transported with patient; document ischemia times
Report work-related injuries appropriately; preserve evidence if assault-related

name: Animal / Human Bite
summary: Bite wound with infection, deep-structure, and rabies/tetanus risk; closure decisions vary by site.

triage: Identify species, provocation, animal availability for observation, and rabies vaccination status
Assess wound location, depth, and joint/tendon involvement
Note clenched-fist 'fight bite' over MCP (high-risk for septic joint)
Document tetanus immunization status
nursing: Copious irrigation with normal saline
Neurovascular and tendon function exam distal to wound
Pain control before exploration
Photograph/document wound* if indicated
keyOrders: High-pressure wound irrigation and debridement of devitalized tissue
Rabies risk assessment and PEP initiation* if indicated
X-ray for fracture/tooth fragment/air in joint* if indicated
labs: CBC and inflammatory markers if systemic infection* if indicated
Blood cultures if febrile/septic* if indicated
imaging: X-ray for suspected fracture, retained tooth, or joint air (fight bite)
Ultrasound for abscess/foreign body* if indicated
CT/MRI for deep-space or osteomyelitis concern* if indicated
No imaging for superficial uncomplicated bites
meds: Amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg PO BID (first-line prophylaxis/treatment)
Cefuroxime + metronidazole or doxycycline + metronidazole if penicillin-allergic
Rabies PEP: RIG 20 IU/kg infiltrated + vaccine days 0,3,7,14 if indicated
Tdap/Td +/- TIG per immunization status
reassess: Reassess for adequate irrigation and infection signs
Recheck neurovascular/tendon status
Determine closure vs leaving open (delayed primary closure for high-risk)
Confirm rabies/tetanus decisions documented
disposition: Discharge most low-risk bites on prophylaxis
Refer/transfer hand surgery for fight bite, joint/tendon involvement, or deep infection
Admit for IV antibiotics if cellulitis/sepsis
discharge: Wound care and prophylactic antibiotics as prescribed
Generally do not primarily close cat bites/hand bites/high-risk wounds
Return precautions: spreading redness, pus, fever, increasing pain
followup: Wound recheck in 24-48 h (sooner for hand)
Complete rabies PEP series on schedule
Hand surgery follow-up for complex wounds

redFlags: Fight-bite over MCP with joint penetration (septic arthritis risk)
Spreading cellulitis, systemic toxicity, or sepsis
Deep hand/tendon/joint involvement
High-risk rabies exposure (bat, wild carnivore, unprovoked attack)
compliance: EMTALA MSE for all bite presentations
Mandatory reporting of animal bites to Texas/local health authority for rabies surveillance
Report suspected human-bite assault or abuse per Texas mandatory reporting
FSED appropriate-transfer if surgical/specialist care exceeds capability; document rabies decisions

name: Soft-Tissue Foreign Body
summary: Embedded object (glass/metal/wood/organic) requiring localization, removal decision, and infection prevention.

triage: Identify material (glass/metal vs organic/wood), entry site, and time since injury
Assess for neurovascular/tendon involvement and joint proximity
Determine if object is high-risk (organic, near vital structure)
Document tetanus immunization status
nursing: Clean and irrigate entry site
Distal neurovascular and motor check
Pain control before exploration
Prepare removal/imaging as indicated
keyOrders: Local anesthesia for removal* if indicated
labs: CBC/inflammatory markers if infected or retained long-standing* if indicated
Wound culture if signs of infection* if indicated
Coagulation panel if anticoagulated and deep removal planned* if indicated
imaging: Plain X-ray (detects glass >2 mm and most metal)
Ultrasound for radiolucent organic material (wood, thorns, plastic)
CT for deep or non-localized foreign body* if indicated
Avoid MRI if metallic FB suspected
meds: Lidocaine 1% local anesthesia for removal
Cephalexin 500 mg PO QID for contaminated/retained organic FB* if indicated
Acetaminophen 1000 mg PO or ibuprofen 600 mg PO for pain
Tdap/Td if not up to date
reassess: Confirm complete removal (re-image if multiple/fragmenting)
Recheck neurovascular/tendon status after removal
Assess wound for closure vs leaving open
Decide on retained deep FB referral if removal unsuccessful
disposition: Discharge most after successful removal
Refer to surgery/hand/ortho for deep, periarticular, or hard-to-localize FB
Leave inert deep asymptomatic FB and refer if removal high-risk
discharge: Wound care and infection precautions
Explain that some inert FBs may be left and observed
Return precautions: redness, swelling, pus, increasing pain, fever
followup: Wound recheck in 48-72 h
Surgery/specialist follow-up for retained or deep FB
Imaging follow-up if FB intentionally left in place

redFlags: Foreign body near vessel, nerve, tendon, or joint
Signs of deep-space infection or abscess
Organic/wood FB (high infection/granuloma risk) that cannot be fully removed
Retained FB causing neurovascular compromise
compliance: EMTALA MSE for all presentations regardless of ability to pay
FSED appropriate-transfer if specialist removal beyond capability
Report assault- or abuse-related injuries per Texas mandatory reporting
Document tetanus status, removal attempts, and any retained FB disclosed to patient

name: Conjunctivitis / Red Eye
summary: Walk-in evaluation and treat-and-release of uncomplicated infectious or allergic conjunctivitis with vision preserved.

triage: Confirm no vision loss, no severe pain, no photophobia, no recent trauma/chemical exposure
Ask about contact lens use, unilateral vs bilateral, discharge type (watery vs purulent), duration
Screen for fever, recent URI, sick contacts, allergy history
Isolate/seat away from others given contagiousness; perform hand hygiene
nursing: Vitals including temperature
Document visual acuity each eye (Snellen) with current correction
Note discharge, lid swelling, periorbital erythema, preauricular node
Provide tissues; reinforce hand hygiene and avoid eye touching
keyOrders: Penlight/external eye exam for injection pattern, discharge, foreign body, corneal clarity
Fluorescein staining with cobalt blue light if pain/abrasion/contact lens suspected*
Evert lids to exclude retained foreign body if indicated*
Pupillary exam for size, reactivity, symmetry
labs: Conjunctival culture not routinely available in urgent care; defer to ophthalmology if hyperacute/severe* if indicated
Consider rapid adenovirus testing only if available* if indicated
imaging: No imaging for uncomplicated conjunctivitis
Defer CT orbit to ER if orbital cellulitis or globe injury suspected
meds: Allergic: olopatadine 0.1-0.2% ophthalmic 1 drop affected eye(s) once-twice daily; add oral cetirizine 10 mg daily
Bacterial (purulent): erythromycin 0.5% ointment QID x5-7d or trimethoprim-polymyxin B 1 drop QID x5-7d
Contact-lens wearer with bacterial signs: fluoroquinolone drop (e.g., ofloxacin 0.3%) covering Pseudomonas*
Viral: supportive care, cool compresses, preservative-free artificial tears QID
Avoid topical steroids in urgent care; defer to ophthalmology*
reassess: Recheck visual acuity stable before discharge
Confirm pain controlled and no worsening photophobia
Verify patient/caregiver can instill drops correctly
disposition: Discharge home for routine viral, bacterial, or allergic conjunctivitis
Refer to ophthalmology within 24-48h if contact-lens-related, herpetic suspicion, or no improvement
Transfer to ER or call 911 if vision loss, severe pain, photophobia with hazy cornea, chemical burn, or suspected orbital cellulitis
discharge: Hand hygiene; discard/replace eye makeup; do not share towels/pillows
Discontinue contact lens use until symptom-free and per provider
Cool compresses and artificial tears for comfort
Return precautions: worsening pain, decreasing vision, light sensitivity, no improvement in 48-72h
followup: PCP if symptoms persist beyond 7-10 days
Ophthalmology for contact-lens, recurrent, or non-resolving cases
School/work return per local guidance once discharge resolved

redFlags: Significant vision loss or marked decrease in acuity
Severe eye pain, photophobia, or ciliary flush suggesting keratitis/uveitis
Hazy or opacified cornea, hypopyon, or fixed/irregular pupil
Periorbital/orbital swelling with proptosis, pain on eye movement, or fever (orbital cellulitis)
Contact-lens wearer with corneal infiltrate or ulcer
Chemical exposure or penetrating trauma
compliance: Urgent care is not a freestanding ER; vision-threatening presentations require EMTALA-mindful transfer to ER/ophthalmology
Document visual acuity on every red-eye visit as standard of care
Avoid prescribing topical steroids without ophthalmology involvement
Provide written Texas return precautions and document referral
No controlled substances indicated for routine conjunctivitis

name: Superficial Ocular Foreign Body
summary: Removal of a superficial, non-penetrating conjunctival or corneal foreign body with abrasion care.

triage: Determine mechanism; exclude high-velocity projectile, grinding/hammering metal-on-metal, or blast (penetrating risk)
Screen for vision loss, severe pain, or visible globe deformity
Note time of injury, eye protection use, contact lenses
If chemical or high-velocity penetrating injury suspected, escalate immediately
nursing: Vitals
Document visual acuity each eye before manipulation
Prepare topical anesthetic, fluorescein strips, irrigation, sterile applicators
Position patient supine with adequate lighting
keyOrders: Penlight and slit-lamp/loupe exam; evert upper and lower lids to find FB
Fluorescein staining to identify abrasion, rust ring, or Seidel sign (positive Seidel = ER)
Foreign body removal: copious saline irrigation, then moistened cotton applicator or 25-27g needle/spud for embedded corneal FB
imaging: No imaging for confirmed superficial FB
Defer CT orbit to ER if intraocular/penetrating FB suspected (do NOT MRI metallic FB)
meds: Prophylactic antibiotic: erythromycin 0.5% ointment QID or polymyxin-trimethoprim drops QID x3-5d
Contact-lens wearers: anti-pseudomonal fluoroquinolone drop (ofloxacin/ciprofloxacin) QID
Oral analgesia: acetaminophen 650-1000 mg or ibuprofen 400-600 mg PRN
Preservative-free artificial tears for comfort
Do NOT prescribe topical anesthetic for home use (toxic to cornea)
reassess: Re-stain to confirm FB fully removed and characterize residual abrasion
Recheck visual acuity after removal
Confirm no Seidel sign, no anterior chamber abnormality
disposition: Discharge home after successful removal of superficial FB with abrasion care
Refer to ophthalmology within 24h for retained/deep corneal FB, large rust ring, or central visual-axis involvement
Transfer to ER or call 911 if penetrating/intraocular FB, positive Seidel, hyphema, or significant vision loss
discharge: No eye rubbing; no contact lenses until cleared and re-examined
Use prescribed antibiotic and artificial tears; expect foreign-body sensation 24h
Wear eye protection in future high-risk activities
Return precautions: increasing pain, decreasing vision, discharge, photophobia, or no improvement in 24-48h
followup: Recheck in 24-48h (urgent care or PCP) to confirm abrasion healing
Ophthalmology next-day for rust ring removal if not fully cleared
Sooner return if symptoms worsen

redFlags: High-velocity mechanism (grinding, hammering, blast) suggesting intraocular foreign body
Positive Seidel sign or visible globe laceration/extruding contents
Hyphema, irregular/teardrop pupil, or shallow anterior chamber
Significant or progressive vision loss
Foreign body in central visual axis or deeply embedded/unable to remove
Chemical or thermal injury accompanying the foreign body
compliance: Urgent care manages superficial FB only; suspected penetrating/intraocular injury is an EMTALA-relevant ER transfer
Never dispense topical anesthetic for home use due to corneal toxicity
Avoid MRI when metallic intraocular FB is possible
Document pre- and post-removal visual acuity and informed consent for the procedure
Report work-related eye injuries appropriately and document for workers' compensation per Texas requirements

name: Stye / Hordeolum
summary: Conservative treat-and-release management of a localized eyelid gland infection without orbital involvement.

triage: Confirm localized lid lump without vision change, fever, or spreading facial swelling
Distinguish acute tender hordeolum from chronic non-tender chalazion
Screen for periorbital/orbital signs: proptosis, pain on eye movement, diffuse swelling
Note duration, prior episodes, blepharitis history
nursing: Vitals including temperature
Document visual acuity each eye
Inspect lid margin, point of tenderness, internal vs external location
Provide warm compress for comfort during wait
keyOrders: External lid exam and lid eversion to locate internal hordeolum
Penlight exam to confirm globe uninvolved and pupil normal
Lid hygiene instruction (dilute baby shampoo or lid scrubs)*
Incision and drainage only if pointing and not resolving — generally defer to ophthalmology* if indicated
labs: Consider wound culture only if drained and atypical/recurrent* if indicated
imaging: No imaging for uncomplicated stye
Defer CT orbit to ER if orbital/preseptal cellulitis suspected
meds: Primary therapy: warm compresses 10-15 min 3-4x/day plus gentle lid massage
Erythromycin 0.5% ophthalmic ointment to lid margin BID if drainage/secondary conjunctivitis*
Lid hygiene for associated blepharitis
Oral analgesia: acetaminophen or ibuprofen PRN
Oral antibiotics (e.g., doxycycline/dicloxacillin) only if early preseptal spread — consider ER/ophthalmology referral*
reassess: Confirm no progression to periorbital/orbital cellulitis
Verify vision unchanged and globe uninvolved
Ensure patient understands warm-compress technique
disposition: Discharge home with conservative care for uncomplicated hordeolum
Refer to ophthalmology for persistent chalazion, recurrent lesions, or need for I&D/intralesional steroid
Transfer to ER or call 911 if orbital cellulitis signs: proptosis, painful/restricted eye movement, vision change, fever, or spreading erythema
discharge: Continue warm compresses and lid hygiene; do not squeeze or pop the lesion
Avoid eye makeup and contact lenses until resolved
Most resolve in 1-2 weeks; residual painless nodule may persist as chalazion
Return precautions: increasing swelling/redness, vision change, fever, or pain with eye movement
followup: PCP or ophthalmology if not improving within 1-2 weeks
Ophthalmology for chalazion excision or recurrent styes
Sooner if spreading or systemic symptoms develop

redFlags: Proptosis, pain or restriction with eye movement, or diplopia (orbital cellulitis)
Diffuse spreading periorbital/facial erythema or warmth
Fever or systemic toxicity
Vision change or afferent pupillary defect
Rapidly enlarging mass or recurrent lesion in same site (concern for sebaceous carcinoma)
compliance: Urgent care provides conservative management; orbital cellulitis is an emergency requiring EMTALA-mindful ER transfer
Eyelid I&D and intralesional steroid injection generally exceed routine urgent-care scope — refer to ophthalmology
Document visual acuity and absence of orbital signs
Provide written Texas return precautions
Prescribe oral antibiotics judiciously and only when infection extends beyond a simple stye

name: Low-Risk Fever (Adult)
summary: Stable, well-appearing adult with isolated low-risk fever and no toxic features.

triage: Confirm temperature; document onset and duration
Screen for sepsis red flags: hypotension, confusion, RR >22, SpO2 <92%
Assess hydration, mental status, general appearance
Ask travel, sick contacts, immunocompromise, recent antibiotics
nursing: Full vitals including SpO2 and orthostatics if dizzy
Recheck temperature after antipyretic
Encourage oral fluids while awaiting workup
Notify provider immediately if SIRS criteria met
keyOrders: POC glucose if altered or diabetic
labs: Rapid flu/COVID/strep as indicated by symptoms
UA if urinary symptoms or flank pain
Defer blood cultures/CBC/lactate to ER if sepsis suspected
imaging: Chest X-ray if cough, dyspnea, or focal lung findings
*No imaging if clearly viral and localized source absent
Defer CT/advanced imaging to ER if indicated
meds: Acetaminophen 650-1000 mg PO q6h (max 3 g/day)
Ibuprofen 400-600 mg PO q6-8h with food if no contraindication
Targeted antibiotics only if bacterial source confirmed (e.g., UTI, strep)
*Oseltamivir 75 mg PO BID x5d if influenza positive and within 48h
reassess: Recheck vitals and appearance before discharge
Confirm fever responds to antipyretic
Ensure tolerating oral fluids
Re-evaluate for any evolving red flags
disposition: Discharge home if well-appearing and stable
PCP follow-up for persistent or unexplained fever
Transfer to ER or call 911 if hypotension, AMS, hypoxia, or suspected sepsis
discharge: Fever control and hydration instructions
Return precautions: fever >3 days, confusion, SOB, rash, stiff neck
Antipyretic dosing limits explained
Provide written return-precaution sheet
followup: PCP within 2-3 days if fever persists
Return sooner for worsening symptoms
*Specialty referral if recurrent unexplained fever

redFlags: Hypotension, tachycardia, or signs of sepsis/shock
Altered mental status or new confusion
Hypoxia (SpO2 <92%) or respiratory distress
Petechial/purpuric rash or neck stiffness (meningitis concern)
Immunocompromised or neutropenic fever
compliance: Urgent care is not a freestanding ER; sepsis/toxic patients require EMTALA-appropriate ER transfer
Document return precautions and stable vitals at discharge
Antibiotic stewardship: treat only confirmed bacterial sources
Report reportable communicable diseases to Texas DSHS as required

name: Tension Headache
summary: Benign, bilateral pressure-type headache without neuro deficits or red-flag features.

triage: Characterize headache: onset, quality, location, severity
Screen thunderclap/worst-ever headache or sudden onset
Ask about fever, neuro deficits, head trauma, anticoagulants
Confirm prior similar headaches and usual pattern
nursing: Full vitals including blood pressure
Brief focused neuro screen (gross strength, speech, gait)
Dim lights and reduce stimulation if photophobia
Notify provider for any focal deficit or severe hypertension
keyOrders: POC glucose if dizzy or diabetic
labs: Usually none for classic tension headache
Defer labs/LP to ER if secondary cause suspected
imaging: No imaging for typical tension headache
Defer CT head to ER if red flags (thunderclap, deficit, trauma)
*Outpatient MRI referral if chronic atypical pattern
meds: Ibuprofen 400-600 mg PO or naproxen 500 mg PO
Acetaminophen 1000 mg PO if NSAID contraindicated
*Cyclobenzaprine 5-10 mg PO if significant muscle tension
Avoid opioids and butalbital due to overuse/rebound risk
reassess: Reassess pain 30-60 min after analgesia
Confirm no evolving neuro signs
Verify BP improved with pain control
Ensure ambulating safely before discharge
disposition: Discharge home once pain controlled
PCP or neurology referral for frequent/chronic headaches
Transfer to ER or call 911 if thunderclap onset, focal deficit, or AMS
discharge: Headache hygiene: hydration, sleep, stress, screen breaks
Limit analgesic use to avoid medication-overuse headache
Return precautions: worst-ever headache, weakness, vision loss, vomiting
Provide written instructions
followup: PCP within 1-2 weeks if recurrent
Neurology referral for chronic or escalating headaches
Headache diary recommended

redFlags: Sudden 'thunderclap' or worst-ever headache
Focal neuro deficit, AMS, or seizure
Headache with fever and neck stiffness
New headache age >50 or with cancer/immunosuppression
Headache after significant head trauma or on anticoagulation
compliance: Urgent care cannot perform CT/LP; refer red-flag presentations to ER
Controlled-substance prudence: avoid opioids/butalbital for benign headache
Document neuro exam and return precautions
EMTALA transfer for any emergent secondary headache concern

name: Mild Allergic Reaction
summary: Localized or cutaneous allergic reaction (hives/itching) without airway or systemic compromise.

triage: Screen IMMEDIATELY for anaphylaxis: airway, breathing, hypotension, vomiting
Identify trigger, timing, and prior reaction history
Assess extent of skin involvement and mucosal swelling
Confirm no tongue/throat swelling or wheeze
nursing: Full vitals including SpO2
Continuous observation if facial/lip involvement
Have epinephrine and airway equipment ready
Escalate immediately if any airway/respiratory change
labs: None required for mild cutaneous reaction
Defer tryptase/allergy workup to outpatient allergist
imaging: Not indicated
Defer to ER only if airway compromise
meds: Cetirizine 10 mg PO or diphenhydramine 25-50 mg PO
Famotidine 20 mg PO as adjunct H2 blocker
Prednisone 40-60 mg PO daily x3-5d for significant urticaria
*EpiPen prescription and teaching if systemic-risk history
reassess: Observe 30-60 min for progression
Recheck airway, breathing, skin spread
Confirm symptoms stable or improving
Verify no new systemic symptoms
disposition: Discharge home if isolated cutaneous reaction stable
Allergy/immunology referral for recurrent reactions
Transfer to ER or call 911 if airway swelling, wheeze, hypotension, or vomiting (anaphylaxis)
discharge: Antihistamine schedule and trigger avoidance
Anaphylaxis warning signs and EpiPen use if prescribed
Return precautions: throat tightness, SOB, dizziness, widespread swelling
Written instructions provided
followup: PCP within a few days if symptoms persist
Allergy referral for identification/testing
*Carry epinephrine if at risk

redFlags: Airway swelling, stridor, hoarseness, or throat tightness
Wheeze, dyspnea, or hypoxia
Hypotension, syncope, or tachycardia
GI symptoms (vomiting/cramping) with skin involvement = anaphylaxis
Rapidly progressive angioedema
compliance: Anaphylaxis is an emergency requiring epinephrine and 911/ER transfer per EMTALA
Urgent care should stock epinephrine and basic airway support
Document observation period and discharge stability
Provide epinephrine auto-injector script when systemic risk present

name: Mild Dehydration
summary: Mild-to-moderate volume depletion in a stable adult able to tolerate oral or short IV rehydration.

triage: Assess cause: vomiting, diarrhea, heat, poor intake
Check for orthostatic symptoms and urine output
Screen severe signs: lethargy, hypotension, tachycardia
Identify diabetes or comorbidities affecting fluid status
nursing: Full vitals with orthostatic blood pressures
Assess mucous membranes, skin turgor, cap refill
Offer oral rehydration solution trial
Notify provider if unable to tolerate PO
keyOrders: *IV normal saline bolus 500-1000 mL if PO fails
labs: *Basic metabolic panel if available and electrolyte concern
Defer extensive labs to ER if severe
imaging: Not indicated for uncomplicated dehydration
Defer to ER if surgical abdomen suspected
meds: Oral rehydration solution / electrolyte fluids
Ondansetron 4 mg ODT for nausea/vomiting
*IV NS 1 L if oral intake fails
Treat underlying cause (e.g., antidiarrheal if appropriate)
reassess: Recheck vitals and orthostatics after fluids
Confirm tolerating PO and improved symptoms
Reassess mental status and urine output
Verify resolution of tachycardia
disposition: Discharge home if rehydrated and tolerating PO
PCP follow-up for ongoing GI illness
Transfer to ER or call 911 if hypotension, AMS, anuria, or failure of rehydration
discharge: Oral rehydration plan and small frequent sips
Avoid plain water alone; use electrolyte fluids
Return precautions: no urine, dizziness, persistent vomiting, confusion
Written instructions provided
followup: PCP within 1-2 days if symptoms continue
Return for inability to keep fluids down
*Specialist referral if recurrent dehydration

redFlags: Hypotension or persistent tachycardia after fluids
Altered mental status or marked lethargy
Anuria or signs of acute kidney injury
Intractable vomiting unable to tolerate any PO
Suspected DKA or severe electrolyte disturbance
compliance: Severe dehydration/shock requires ER transfer under EMTALA
IV therapy limited to short stabilization; not for prolonged management
Document intake tolerance and hemodynamic stability at discharge
Provide clear return precautions

name: Dental Pain
summary: Localized dental/tooth pain or simple abscess without airway or deep-space spread.

triage: Localize pain; assess swelling and duration
Screen for facial cellulitis, trismus, or airway concern
Ask about fever, trauma, and prior dental work
Check ability to swallow and open mouth fully
nursing: Full vitals including temperature
Inspect oral cavity, gums, and any visible abscess
Assess for facial/submandibular swelling
Notify provider if swelling crossing facial planes
keyOrders: *Incision/drainage NOT typically performed in urgent care
labs: Defer cultures to dental/ER if deep infection
imaging: No imaging typically; dental X-ray usually outpatient
Defer CT to ER if deep-space infection suspected
*Panoramic dental film referred to dentist
meds: Ibuprofen 600 mg PO + acetaminophen 1000 mg PO alternating
Amoxicillin 500 mg PO TID x5-7d if abscess/infection
Clindamycin 300 mg PO QID if penicillin allergy
*Topical benzocaine for temporary relief; avoid opioids
reassess: Confirm pain improved with analgesia
Reassess swelling and ability to swallow
Verify no airway compromise
Confirm understanding of dental follow-up need
disposition: Discharge home with dentist referral
Urgent dental referral within 24-48h for definitive care
Transfer to ER or call 911 if airway compromise, Ludwig angina, or spreading facial infection
discharge: Analgesia plan and antibiotic instructions if prescribed
Warm saline rinses and soft diet
Return precautions: facial swelling, fever, difficulty swallowing/breathing
Stress that antibiotics are temporizing, not curative
followup: Dentist within 24-72h for definitive treatment
Return sooner if swelling spreads
*Oral surgery referral for complex abscess

redFlags: Floor-of-mouth swelling, drooling, or trouble swallowing (Ludwig angina)
Trismus or rapidly spreading facial cellulitis
Airway compromise or voice change
High fever with systemic toxicity
Periorbital extension of infection
compliance: Definitive dental care is outside urgent-care scope; refer to dentist
Deep-space/airway infections require ER transfer under EMTALA
Controlled-substance prudence: NSAIDs/acetaminophen first-line, avoid opioids
Antibiotics are a bridge to dental treatment, not a cure

name: Medication Refill
summary: Short bridge refill of a stable, non-controlled chronic medication when PCP access is delayed.

triage: Confirm medication, dose, last fill, and prescriber
Verify reason PCP cannot refill
Screen for symptoms of uncontrolled disease
Identify whether medication is controlled (generally not refilled)
nursing: Relevant vitals (e.g., BP for antihypertensives, glucose for diabetics)
Confirm current pharmacy and med list
Document adherence and side effects
Flag any abnormal vitals to provider
keyOrders: Targeted vitals based on medication class
Verify prescription via pharmacy/records if possible
labs: Generally none; condition-specific monitoring deferred to PCP
Defer INR/level monitoring to PCP or lab
imaging: Not indicated
meds: Short bridge supply (e.g., 7-30 days) of stable non-controlled medication
No initiation of controlled substances for refill requests
Confirm no contraindications/interactions before prescribing
*Defer complex titration to PCP
reassess: Confirm patient understands this is a temporary bridge
Verify vitals acceptable for the medication
Ensure PCP follow-up arranged
Document rationale for bridge refill
disposition: Discharge home with short bridge prescription
Refer to PCP to re-establish ongoing management
Transfer to ER or call 911 if signs of acute decompensation (e.g., hypertensive emergency, severe hyperglycemia)
discharge: Emphasize establishing/continuing PCP care
Provide bridge quantity and pharmacy info
Return precautions specific to the condition
Advise not to rely on urgent care for chronic refills
followup: PCP within the bridge window to assume care
*Specialist follow-up as appropriate
Assist with PCP referral if uninsured/unestablished

redFlags: Symptoms of uncontrolled/decompensated disease
Request for controlled substances (opioids, benzodiazepines, stimulants)
Abnormal vitals suggesting acute crisis
Drug-seeking or inconsistent history
Medications requiring lab monitoring not available
compliance: Urgent care provides bridge refills only; not a chronic-care home
Controlled-substance prudence: do not refill controlled meds; check Texas PMP/PDMP
Verify prescription legitimacy before refilling
Document medical necessity and PCP referral

name: Work / School Note & Physical
summary: Administrative visit for work/school notes or routine physical/preparticipation exam in a well patient.

triage: Clarify purpose: note, clearance, or physical exam
Confirm patient is well with no acute complaint
Identify any required forms to complete
Screen for symptoms that would defer clearance
nursing: Full vitals and BMI
Vision screen if required for physical
Collect history forms and immunization records
Flag abnormal vitals to provider
labs: *Urine drug screen or TB test if required
imaging: Not indicated for routine clearance
meds: None typically required
*Update immunizations if needed (see immunization protocol)
reassess: Verify exam findings support clearance
Identify any condition needing PCP evaluation first
Confirm forms completed accurately
Document any restrictions
disposition: Discharge home with completed note/form
Refer to PCP/cardiology if exam reveals concern (e.g., murmur, abnormal BP)
Transfer to ER or call 911 if unexpected acute finding (e.g., chest pain, severe hypertension)
discharge: Provide signed note/clearance documentation
Explain any activity restrictions
Advise PCP follow-up for any flagged findings
Return precautions for new symptoms
followup: PCP for abnormal findings or further workup
*Cardiology referral if cardiac concern on sports physical
Routine periodic exams with PCP

redFlags: Exertional chest pain, syncope, or concerning cardiac history (sports physical)
Severe uncontrolled hypertension
New murmur or abnormal cardiopulmonary exam
Findings suggesting undiagnosed serious illness
Inability to safely clear for requested activity
compliance: Provider must only sign clearances supported by exam findings
Follow employer/UIL/school documentation requirements accurately
Refer abnormal findings rather than clearing inappropriately
Maintain confidentiality of medical information

name: Tetanus / Immunization
summary: Routine or wound-indicated tetanus and other adult vaccinations in a stable patient.

triage: Determine reason: routine, wound prophylaxis, or catch-up
Obtain immunization history and last tetanus date
Screen for vaccine allergies/contraindications
Assess any wound: clean vs dirty, depth, contamination
nursing: Vitals and brief allergy review
Verify vaccine type, lot, expiration before administration
Provide Vaccine Information Statement (VIS)
Observe 15 min post-injection for reaction
keyOrders: *Tetanus immune globulin (TIG) for high-risk wound if undervaccinated
labs: Defer titer testing to PCP if needed
imaging: Not indicated unless deep foreign body in wound (then X-ray)
meds: Tdap or Td booster per indication
Tetanus prophylaxis: Tdap if dirty wound and >5y since last dose
*TIG 250 units IM for high-risk wound in undervaccinated patient
*Other adult vaccines (influenza, etc.) as available
reassess: Observe for immediate allergic reaction
Confirm wound care adequate if applicable
Document vaccines given and next due dates
Verify patient stable before discharge
disposition: Discharge home after observation period
Refer to PCP/pharmacy for full vaccine series completion
Transfer to ER or call 911 if anaphylaxis post-vaccination
discharge: Expected local soreness and low-grade fever counseling
Acetaminophen/ibuprofen for injection-site discomfort
Return precautions: signs of anaphylaxis, spreading infection
Provide updated immunization record
followup: PCP for remaining series doses or catch-up schedule
Wound recheck if applicable
Report administration to Texas immunization registry (ImmTrac2) with consent

redFlags: Anaphylaxis or severe allergic reaction post-vaccine
Severe contaminated wound with systemic signs
Signs of established tetanus (trismus, spasms)
High fever or systemic illness after vaccination
Prior severe reaction to the vaccine component
compliance: Maintain epinephrine/anaphylaxis readiness and 15-min observation
Provide VIS and document lot/site/expiration per CDC requirements
Report to Texas ImmTrac2 registry with patient consent
Anaphylaxis requires epinephrine and ER transfer under EMTALA

name: Nausea / Vomiting / Gastroenteritis
summary: Acute self-limited nausea/vomiting, typically viral or foodborne, managed with antiemetics and oral rehydration in adults.

triage: Onset, frequency, and duration of vomiting; presence of diarrhea
Assess hydration: dizziness, urine output, dry mouth, lightheadedness on standing
Screen for blood/bile in emesis, severe or localized abdominal pain
Sick contacts, recent travel, suspect food source
Pregnancy status in females of childbearing age
nursing: Vitals including orthostatic BP/HR if dehydration suspected
Weight and general appearance/mucous membrane check
Quiet area with emesis basin; limit oral intake until antiemetic given
Reassess intake tolerance after medication
keyOrders: Oral rehydration trial (small frequent sips) after antiemetic
*IV fluids if available and moderate dehydration / failed PO trial
labs: *Urine pregnancy test if female of childbearing age (if indicated)
Electrolytes/CBC typically deferred to ER/outpatient if needed
imaging: None routinely
*Defer abdominal imaging (CT/US) to ER if obstruction or surgical abdomen suspected
meds: Ondansetron 4 mg ODT/IV, may repeat once
*Promethazine 12.5-25 mg PO/PR if indicated (caution sedation)
*Metoclopramide 10 mg if indicated
Oral rehydration solution; advance to bland diet (BRAT) as tolerated
*IV normal saline bolus 500-1000 mL if dehydrated and IV available
reassess: Recheck ability to tolerate oral fluids 30-60 min after antiemetic
Reassess hydration status and vitals before discharge
Confirm no worsening or localizing abdominal pain
disposition: Discharge home if tolerating PO and stable vitals
PCP follow-up if symptoms persist beyond 48-72 h
Transfer to ER or call 911 if intractable vomiting, signs of severe dehydration/shock, bloody/bilious emesis, severe abdominal pain, or altered mental status
discharge: Oral rehydration instructions; small frequent clear fluids then bland diet
Hand hygiene to prevent spread
Continue antiemetic as prescribed; avoid dairy/fatty/spicy foods 24-48 h
Return precautions: persistent vomiting >24 h, no urine output, blood in emesis/stool, severe pain
followup: PCP if not improved in 48-72 h
Earlier follow-up for elderly, diabetic, or immunocompromised
*Stool studies via PCP if prolonged or bloody diarrhea

redFlags: Signs of severe dehydration or hemodynamic instability
Bilious or bloody (hematemesis/coffee-ground) vomiting
Severe, localized, or peritoneal abdominal pain
Altered mental status, severe headache, or stiff neck
Suspected bowel obstruction, GI bleed, DKA, or pregnancy complication
compliance: Urgent care is not a freestanding ER; arrange EMTALA-appropriate ER transfer for unstable or surgical presentations
Document hydration assessment and PO tolerance prior to discharge
Provide clear written return precautions
Report suspected foodborne outbreaks to Texas DSHS as required
Limit antiemetics to short outpatient courses; counsel on driving/sedation

name: Diarrhea
summary: Acute diarrhea in adults, usually self-limited viral/bacterial, managed with rehydration and selective antimicrobials.

triage: Duration, frequency, and character of stools (watery vs bloody)
Associated fever, abdominal cramps, vomiting
Hydration assessment: thirst, urine output, dizziness
Recent travel, antibiotics, sick contacts, suspect foods
Immunocompromise, age, comorbidities
nursing: Vitals including orthostatic BP/HR if indicated
Weight and mucous membrane/hydration check
Encourage oral rehydration during visit
Collect stool sample if studies ordered
keyOrders: *IV fluids if available and dehydrated/failed PO
labs: *Stool occult blood / fecal studies if bloody or prolonged (if indicated)
*C. difficile testing deferred to outpatient if recent antibiotics
Electrolytes deferred to ER/outpatient if significant
imaging: None routinely
*Defer abdominal CT to ER if severe pain or suspected colitis/obstruction
meds: Oral rehydration solution; advance diet as tolerated
Loperamide 4 mg then 2 mg per loose stool (max 16 mg/day) - avoid if bloody stool or high fever
*Bismuth subsalicylate if indicated
*Azithromycin 500 mg daily x3 d or ciprofloxacin for dysentery/traveler's diarrhea if indicated
*IV normal saline bolus if dehydrated and IV available
reassess: Reassess hydration and PO tolerance before discharge
Reconsider bacterial cause if high fever or bloody stools
Confirm stable vitals
disposition: Discharge home if stable and hydrated
PCP follow-up if persistent >7 days or recurrent
Transfer to ER or call 911 if severe dehydration/shock, significant GI bleeding, severe abdominal pain, high fever with toxic appearance, or altered mental status
discharge: Rehydration and hand-hygiene instructions
Avoid antidiarrheals if bloody stool or fever
Diet advice: bland foods, avoid dairy/caffeine/alcohol short term
Return precautions: bloody stools, high fever, no urine output, severe pain, dizziness
followup: PCP if no improvement in 5-7 days or recurrent
Earlier follow-up for elderly/immunocompromised
*Stool studies/referral for chronic or bloody diarrhea

redFlags: Severe dehydration or hemodynamic instability
Grossly bloody diarrhea or signs of GI bleed
Severe or localized abdominal pain / peritoneal signs
High fever with toxic appearance or altered mental status
Immunocompromised host with significant illness
compliance: Urgent care is not a freestanding ER; transfer unstable patients per EMTALA
Use antibiotics judiciously; avoid empiric antibiotics in suspected EHEC/bloody diarrhea
Report reportable enteric infections to Texas DSHS as required
Document hydration assessment and return precautions
Counsel on antidiarrheal contraindications

name: Constipation
summary: Adult functional constipation managed with bowel regimen and lifestyle counseling after excluding obstruction.

triage: Frequency and consistency of stools; duration of symptoms
Associated abdominal pain, distension, nausea/vomiting
Last bowel movement and flatus; any rectal bleeding
Medications (opioids, anticholinergics), fluid/fiber intake
Red-flag screen: weight loss, melena, new onset in older adult
nursing: Vitals
Abdominal inspection for distension
*Assist with bowel-regimen teaching
Review medication list contributing to constipation
keyOrders: *Digital rectal exam for impaction if indicated
*Manual disimpaction / enema if fecal impaction and within scope
imaging: Usually none
*Plain abdominal X-ray (KUB) if available to assess stool burden/obstruction
*Defer abdominal CT to ER if obstruction suspected
meds: Polyethylene glycol 17 g in 8 oz fluid daily
*Docusate 100 mg BID and/or senna 8.6-17.2 mg at bedtime if indicated
*Bisacodyl 10 mg PO/PR if indicated
*Glycerin suppository or saline enema for distal stool if indicated
Increase fiber, fluids, and activity; supplement with psyllium
reassess: Reassess for relief if enema/disimpaction performed
Confirm no obstructive features (vomiting, severe distension)
Verify tolerating oral intake
disposition: Discharge home with bowel regimen if stable and no obstruction
PCP follow-up for chronic/recurrent constipation or red flags
Transfer to ER or call 911 if suspected bowel obstruction, severe pain, significant rectal bleeding, vomiting, or peritoneal signs
discharge: Bowel regimen instructions and expected timeline
Increase dietary fiber, fluids, and physical activity
Review/adjust constipating medications with PCP
Return precautions: severe pain, vomiting, distension, no BM/flatus, rectal bleeding
followup: PCP for persistent or chronic constipation
GI referral for alarm features (weight loss, bleeding, anemia, age >50 new onset)
*Colorectal cancer screening if overdue

redFlags: Suspected bowel obstruction (vomiting, distension, no flatus)
Severe or peritoneal abdominal pain
Significant rectal bleeding or melena
Unintentional weight loss or new-onset constipation in older adult
Systemic signs of toxicity
compliance: Urgent care is not a freestanding ER; transfer suspected obstruction per EMTALA
Document exclusion of obstruction before laxative therapy
Refer age-appropriate patients for colorectal cancer screening and alarm-feature workup
Provide written bowel-regimen and return precautions
Avoid chronic stimulant laxative overuse counseling

name: Mild Abdominal Pain
summary: Evaluation of mild, non-acute abdominal pain in adults to identify benign causes and exclude surgical emergencies.

triage: Pain location, onset, character, radiation, and severity (mild = able to function)
Associated nausea/vomiting, fever, urinary or bowel changes
Relation to meals; last BM; trauma history
Pregnancy status in females of childbearing age
Screen red flags: severe/worsening pain, rigidity, syncope
nursing: Full vitals including temperature
Focused abdominal exam observation; note guarding/rebound for provider
*Clean catch urine collection
Pain assessment and reassessment
labs: *Urine pregnancy test if female of childbearing age
CBC/CMP/lipase typically deferred to ER if surgical cause suspected
imaging: *Plain abdominal X-ray if available for constipation/free air screen (limited)
Defer abdominal/pelvic CT or ultrasound to ER or outpatient
*Outpatient US referral for gallbladder/pelvic causes if stable
meds: Acetaminophen 650-1000 mg for analgesia
*Antacid/PPI (e.g., omeprazole 20 mg daily) if dyspepsia suspected
*Antispasmodic (dicyclomine 20 mg) for cramping if indicated
*Treat identified cause (e.g., antibiotics for confirmed UTI)
Avoid opioids; avoid NSAIDs if gastritis/ulcer suspected
reassess: Reassess pain and exam after analgesia
Confirm no progression, rebound, guarding, or worsening vitals
Verify oral tolerance and ability to ambulate
disposition: Discharge home if mild, stable, benign exam, and reassuring vitals
PCP or specialist (GI/surgery) follow-up for persistent symptoms
Transfer to ER or call 911 if severe/worsening pain, peritoneal signs, hemodynamic instability, suspected appendicitis/obstruction/ectopic, or GI bleeding
discharge: Clear diet and analgesia instructions
Specific return precautions for worsening or localized pain
Avoid NSAIDs if ulcer/gastritis suspected
Return precautions: severe/persistent pain, fever, vomiting, bleeding, fainting
followup: PCP within a few days if symptoms persist
GI/surgical referral for recurrent or unexplained pain
*Outpatient imaging or labs as arranged

redFlags: Severe, sudden, or progressively worsening pain
Peritoneal signs: rigidity, rebound, involuntary guarding
Hemodynamic instability, syncope, or pallor
Suspected appendicitis, obstruction, perforation, AAA, or ectopic pregnancy
GI bleeding (hematemesis, melena, hematochezia) or intractable vomiting
compliance: Urgent care is not a freestanding ER; transfer surgical/unstable abdomens per EMTALA
Always document pregnancy testing in reproductive-age females
Avoid masking serious pathology; do not over-medicate before exam
Provide explicit written return precautions and timely follow-up
Exercise controlled-substance prudence; avoid opioids for undifferentiated abdominal pain

name: Urinary Tract Infection / Dysuria
summary: Evaluation and treatment of lower urinary symptoms in ambulatory adults; identify uncomplicated cystitis vs. complicated/upper-tract infection needing escalation.

triage: Chief complaint: dysuria, frequency, urgency, suprapubic discomfort, hematuria
Vitals incl. temperature; flag fever >38C or hypotension
Screen for flank/back pain, rigors, vomiting (pyelonephritis features)
Pregnancy possibility and LMP in females of childbearing age
History of recurrent UTI, diabetes, immunosuppression, urinary instrumentation, indwelling catheter
nursing: Clean-catch midstream urine collection
Pain/comfort assessment; offer analgesia per protocol
Glucose check if diabetic*
Pregnancy test in childbearing-age females
Document allergies and current antibiotic/medication use
keyOrders: Point-of-care urine dipstick (leukocyte esterase, nitrites, blood)
labs: Urine culture if complicated, recurrent, pregnant, male, or treatment failure*
Defer renal function/CBC to ER or outpatient if systemic illness suspected
imaging: None routinely for uncomplicated cystitis
Defer renal ultrasound/CT to ER or outpatient urology if obstruction, stone, or pyelonephritis suspected*
meds: Nitrofurantoin 100 mg PO BID x5 days (uncomplicated, normal renal function)
TMP-SMX DS PO BID x3 days if local resistance low*
Cephalexin 500 mg PO BID x5-7 days as alternative*
Fosfomycin 3 g PO single dose alternative*
Phenazopyridine 200 mg PO TID x2 days for dysuria relief
reassess: Confirm no fever, flank pain, or systemic signs before discharge
Verify antibiotic selection matches renal function, allergies, pregnancy status
Re-screen for pyelonephritis if symptoms atypical
Counsel expected improvement within 48-72 hours
disposition: Discharge home for uncomplicated cystitis with oral antibiotics
Refer to PCP/urology for recurrent or male UTI follow-up
Transfer to ER or call 911 if high fever, rigors, intractable vomiting, sepsis signs, suspected obstruction, or pregnant with pyelonephritis
discharge: Increase fluid intake; complete full antibiotic course
Return precautions: fever, flank/back pain, vomiting, worsening despite 48-72h treatment
Note phenazopyridine turns urine orange and stains contacts/clothing
Avoid bladder irritants; voiding hygiene education
followup: PCP if symptoms persist or recur
Urine culture result follow-up if obtained
Urology referral for recurrent UTI or male patients

redFlags: Fever with rigors, flank pain, or costovertebral angle tenderness (pyelonephritis)
Sepsis signs: hypotension, tachycardia, altered mental status
Pregnancy with suspected upper-tract infection
Intractable vomiting preventing oral therapy
Known/suspected obstructing stone or urinary retention
compliance: Urgent care scope: uncomplicated lower UTI only; escalate complicated/upper-tract cases
EMTALA/ER transfer for septic or obstructed patients; urgent care is not a freestanding ER
Use guideline-concordant antibiotics; avoid fluoroquinolone overuse (antimicrobial stewardship)
Document return precautions and patient understanding
Confirm pregnancy status before prescribing; avoid contraindicated agents (e.g., nitrofurantoin near term)

name: Sexually Transmitted Infection Concern
summary: Ambulatory assessment, testing, and empiric treatment for suspected STI exposure or symptoms; counseling, partner notification, and reporting.

triage: Chief complaint: discharge, dysuria, genital lesions/sores, pelvic pain, exposure concern
Vitals; screen for fever or systemic illness
Sexual history: partners, practices, condom use, prior STIs
Females: pregnancy possibility, LMP, abdominal/pelvic pain
Assess for assault/abuse and consent issues
nursing: Provide private, nonjudgmental environment
Collect specimens: urine NAAT, genital/oral/rectal swabs as indicated
Pregnancy test in childbearing-age females
Pain assessment and comfort measures
Document allergies and last menstrual period
labs: Rapid HIV and syphilis (RPR) if available; otherwise refer*
Wet mount/trichomonas testing if discharge present*
Defer confirmatory/serology and complex testing to outpatient or public health clinic
imaging: None routinely
Defer pelvic ultrasound to ER/outpatient if PID or abscess suspected*
meds: Ceftriaxone 500 mg IM single dose for gonorrhea (1 g if >=150 kg)
Doxycycline 100 mg PO BID x7 days for chlamydia (azithromycin 1 g PO if pregnant)
Metronidazole 500 mg PO BID x7 days for trichomonas
Empiric dual therapy if symptomatic and testing pending*
Acyclovir 400 mg PO TID x7-10 days for first-episode genital herpes*
reassess: Confirm no PID features (cervical motion/adnexal tenderness, fever) needing escalation
Verify empiric coverage and allergy check before IM dosing
Counsel abstinence until therapy complete and partners treated
Offer expedited partner therapy where permitted*
disposition: Discharge home with treatment and counseling for uncomplicated STI
Refer to PCP/public health for HIV/syphilis confirmation and ongoing care
Transfer to ER or call 911 if signs of disseminated infection, tubo-ovarian abscess, severe pelvic pain with sepsis, or pregnancy with systemic illness
discharge: Complete full medication course; abstain from sex until treated and asymptomatic 7 days
Advise partner testing/treatment and condom use
Return precautions: worsening pelvic pain, fever, vomiting, spreading rash/lesions
Provide test-result follow-up plan and rescreening guidance
followup: PCP or public health clinic for results and confirmatory testing
Test-of-cure or rescreen in 3 months per CDC guidance
Partner notification and treatment coordination

redFlags: Pelvic inflammatory disease with high fever, peritoneal signs, or sepsis
Suspected tubo-ovarian abscess
Disseminated gonococcal infection (arthritis, dermatitis, systemic illness)
Pregnancy with systemic infection or severe abdominal pain
Disclosure of sexual assault requiring forensic/specialized care
compliance: Texas mandatory reporting of gonorrhea, chlamydia, syphilis, HIV to public health (DSHS)
Suspected sexual assault/abuse of minors or vulnerable adults triggers mandatory reporting and ER referral for forensic exam
Urgent care scope: empiric treatment and testing; refer complex/PID/HIV care
EMTALA/ER transfer for systemically ill patients; urgent care is not a freestanding ER
Maintain confidentiality; offer expedited partner therapy consistent with Texas law

name: Vaginal Discharge
summary: Evaluation of abnormal vaginal discharge in ambulatory adults to distinguish candidiasis, bacterial vaginosis, trichomoniasis, and STI; treat or refer.

triage: Chief complaint: discharge color/odor/amount, itching, irritation, dyspareunia
Vitals; screen for fever or pelvic pain
Pregnancy possibility and LMP
Sexual history and STI exposure risk
Recent antibiotics, new products, or prior similar episodes
nursing: Collect specimen for wet mount and pH if available
Pregnancy test in childbearing-age females
Private exam setup with chaperone offered
Document allergies and medications
Comfort/pain assessment
labs: Wet mount (clue cells, yeast, motile trichomonads)*
Defer culture/PCR panels to outpatient if diagnosis unclear
imaging: None routinely
Defer pelvic ultrasound to ER/outpatient if PID or mass suspected*
meds: Fluconazole 150 mg PO single dose for candidiasis (or topical azole)
Metronidazole 500 mg PO BID x7 days for bacterial vaginosis
Metronidazole 500 mg PO BID x7 days for trichomoniasis (treat partner)
Clotrimazole/miconazole intravaginal as alternative for yeast*
Avoid alcohol during and 72h after metronidazole
reassess: Confirm no fever, pelvic/adnexal tenderness, or PID features
Verify pregnancy-safe regimen if pregnant
Counsel on cause-specific treatment and partner therapy for trichomonas
Re-evaluate diagnosis if multiple recurrences
disposition: Discharge home with targeted therapy for uncomplicated vaginitis
Refer to PCP/gynecology for recurrent, persistent, or unclear cases
Transfer to ER or call 911 if high fever, severe pelvic pain with peritoneal signs, or sepsis
discharge: Complete medication course; avoid douching and irritants
Trichomonas: treat partner, abstain until both treated and asymptomatic
Return precautions: fever, worsening pelvic pain, no improvement in 3-7 days
Counsel cotton underwear, hygiene, and recurrence prevention
followup: PCP/gynecology for recurrent or persistent symptoms
STI test results follow-up if obtained
Rescreen for trichomonas/STI per CDC guidance

redFlags: Fever with pelvic pain or peritoneal signs (PID)
Foul purulent discharge with systemic illness
Pregnancy with bleeding, severe pain, or possible rupture of membranes
Retained foreign body with signs of toxic shock
Suspected sexual assault requiring specialized care
compliance: Urgent care scope: uncomplicated vaginitis; refer recurrent/unclear cases to gynecology
Report reportable STIs (e.g., trichomonas-associated coinfections, gonorrhea/chlamydia) per Texas DSHS rules
EMTALA/ER transfer for systemically ill or PID patients; urgent care is not a freestanding ER
Confirm pregnancy status before prescribing; use pregnancy-safe regimens
Document chaperone offer, exam findings, and return precautions

name: Sprain / Strain
summary: Acute soft-tissue ligament/muscle injury without obvious deformity or neurovascular compromise.

triage: Mechanism, time of injury, ability to bear weight/use limb
Vitals and pain score
Screen for deformity, open wound, or numbness suggesting fracture/dislocation
Apply validated rules (e.g., Ottawa ankle/knee) to gauge X-ray need
nursing: Ice pack and elevation while awaiting eval
Remove rings/constricting items from injured extremity
Document distal pulses, capillary refill, sensation, motor function
Reassure and initiate RICE education
keyOrders: Plain X-ray if fracture rules positive*
Elastic wrap or removable splint/brace for support
Crutches or sling for offloading if indicated
labs: None routinely indicated for isolated sprain/strain
imaging: Plain X-ray only if Ottawa/clinical rules suggest fracture*
Defer MRI/ultrasound to outpatient ortho if persistent instability*
meds: Ibuprofen 400-600 mg PO q6-8h with food (if no contraindication)
Acetaminophen 650-1000 mg PO q6h, max 3 g/day
Topical diclofenac gel to localized area* if indicated
Avoid opioids; reserve only for severe short-term pain
reassess: Recheck neurovascular status after splint/wrap applied
Confirm pain controlled and patient ambulating safely if applicable
Verify X-ray (if done) shows no fracture
disposition: Discharge home with RICE and activity modification
Refer to orthopedics/sports medicine if no improvement in 1-2 weeks*
Transfer to ER or call 911 if deformity, suspected open/displaced fracture, or loss of pulse/sensation
discharge: Written RICE instructions and activity restrictions
Proper use of crutches/brace and weight-bearing limits
Expected recovery timeline (days to weeks)
Return precautions: increasing pain, numbness, color change, fever
followup: PCP or sports medicine in 1-2 weeks if not improving
Physical therapy referral for moderate sprains*
Return sooner if symptoms worsen

redFlags: Obvious deformity or suspected displaced/open fracture
Absent distal pulse, pallor, or progressive numbness (compartment/vascular concern)
Inability to bear any weight with severe pain suggesting fracture
Joint dislocation requiring reduction
compliance: Urgent care manages uncomplicated soft-tissue injury; transfer per EMTALA only if emergent at a hospital-based site
Document neurovascular exam before and after splinting
Provide written return precautions; minimize opioid prescribing
Refer suspected fracture/instability to appropriate ER or outpatient ortho

name: Minor Extremity Injury / Possible Fracture
summary: Closed, stable extremity injury with possible non-displaced fracture suitable for X-ray and splinting.

triage: Mechanism, energy of injury, time elapsed
Vitals and pain score
Assess for deformity, open wound, point tenderness, swelling
Apply Ottawa ankle/foot/knee rules where applicable
nursing: Immobilize and elevate; apply ice
Document distal neurovascular status
Remove rings/jewelry distal to injury
Control any minor bleeding and clean abrasions
keyOrders: Plain X-ray of injured area (2+ views)
Sling, walking boot, or crutches as appropriate
labs: None routinely indicated for isolated closed injury
imaging: Plain X-ray on site for suspected fracture
Defer CT/MRI and complex/intra-articular imaging to ER or ortho*
meds: Ibuprofen 400-600 mg PO q6-8h with food
Acetaminophen 650-1000 mg PO q6h, max 3 g/day
Tdap/Td booster if open wound and not current
Short course low-dose opioid only if severe pain and no alternative*
reassess: Confirm fracture stability and alignment on X-ray
Verify intact neurovascular status post-splint
Ensure splint not too tight and patient comfortable
disposition: Discharge home with splint and timely ortho follow-up for stable, non-displaced fracture
Refer to orthopedics within 3-7 days for definitive care
Transfer to ER or call 911 if open, displaced, angulated, or intra-articular fracture, or neurovascular compromise
discharge: Splint care and elevation instructions
Strict non-weight-bearing or activity limits as indicated
Signs of compartment syndrome and tight-cast/splint warning
Confirmed ortho follow-up appointment or contact info
followup: Orthopedics in 3-7 days for casting/definitive management
PCP if no specialist access
Return immediately for worsening pain, numbness, or color change

redFlags: Open fracture or fracture with overlying wound
Significant deformity, angulation, or displacement
Neurovascular deficit (pulselessness, pallor, progressive numbness)
Suspected compartment syndrome (pain out of proportion, tense compartment)
Hip/femur/pelvis injury or high-energy mechanism
compliance: Urgent care handles stable closed extremity injury; emergent/displaced/open injuries go to ER (EMTALA at hospital-affiliated sites)
Document pre- and post-splint neurovascular exam
Update tetanus prophylaxis for any open wound and document
Arrange and document ortho follow-up; prudent controlled-substance use with state PMP check

name: Acute Low Back Pain
summary: Acute mechanical/musculoskeletal low back pain without neurologic deficit or systemic red flags.

triage: Onset, mechanism, radiation, prior episodes
Vitals and pain score
Screen for red flags: trauma, fever, bowel/bladder change, saddle anesthesia, weakness
Assess gait and ability to ambulate
nursing: Position of comfort; assist with ambulation
Document any motor/sensory deficit and reflexes
Ask about anticoagulation, cancer history, IV drug use, weight loss
Reassure regarding benign nature of most cases
keyOrders: Focused neuro exam including straight-leg raise
POC urinalysis if flank pain/infection suspected*
labs: None routinely for uncomplicated mechanical back pain
imaging: No imaging for uncomplicated acute back pain in first 4-6 weeks
Defer X-ray/MRI to outpatient if red flags or no improvement*; emergent MRI via ER if cauda equina suspected
meds: Ibuprofen 400-600 mg PO q6-8h or naproxen 500 mg PO BID with food
Acetaminophen 650-1000 mg PO q6h, max 3 g/day
Short course cyclobenzaprine 5-10 mg PO qHS for spasm* (caution sedation)
Avoid opioids and bed rest; encourage activity as tolerated
reassess: Confirm no evolving neuro deficit
Verify pain controlled and patient ambulating safely
Re-screen for missed red flags before discharge
disposition: Discharge home with activity, analgesia, and reassurance
Refer to PCP/physical therapy if not improved in 4-6 weeks*
Transfer to ER or call 911 if cauda equina signs, acute weakness, fever with back pain, or suspected AAA/epidural abscess
discharge: Stay active; avoid prolonged bed rest
Analgesic schedule and heat/stretching guidance
Return precautions: leg weakness, numbness, incontinence, fever
Expected gradual improvement over days to weeks
followup: PCP in 1-2 weeks if persistent
Physical therapy for recurrent/persistent pain*
Return immediately for any red-flag symptom

redFlags: Saddle anesthesia, urinary retention/incontinence, bilateral leg weakness (cauda equina)
Fever with back pain or IV drug use (epidural abscess/discitis)
Progressive or significant motor weakness
History of cancer, unexplained weight loss, or night pain
Severe trauma, anticoagulation, or suspected abdominal aortic aneurysm
compliance: Urgent care treats uncomplicated mechanical low back pain; emergent neuro/vascular causes to ER (EMTALA where applicable)
Imaging not indicated early without red flags (avoid low-value imaging)
Document neuro exam and red-flag screen
Prudent muscle relaxant use; avoid opioids; check PMP if controlled substance considered

name: Joint Pain / Swelling
summary: Painful/swollen joint from likely gout, overuse, or mild arthritis without signs of septic joint.

triage: Onset, number of joints, prior gout/arthritis, trauma, fever
Vitals including temperature
Inspect for warmth, erythema, effusion, range of motion
Screen for systemic illness or recent infection
nursing: Elevate and rest affected joint; ice for swelling
Document temperature and joint exam findings
Ask about medications, alcohol, purine intake, tick exposure
Assess ability to bear weight/use joint
keyOrders: Defer arthrocentesis to ER/specialist if septic joint suspected
labs: Limited; consider rapid strep/viral testing only if clinically relevant*
Defer CBC, ESR/CRP, uric acid, joint fluid analysis to outpatient/ER*
imaging: Plain X-ray of joint if trauma, chronic change, or effusion*
Defer ultrasound/MRI and aspiration to ER or rheumatology/ortho*
meds: Acute gout: indomethacin 50 mg PO TID or naproxen 500 mg PO BID (if no renal/GI contraindication)
Colchicine 1.2 mg then 0.6 mg 1 hr later for gout flare*
Acetaminophen 650-1000 mg PO q6h for analgesia
Short prednisone taper if NSAID/colchicine contraindicated* (avoid if infection suspected)
reassess: Confirm no fever or signs suggesting septic joint
Verify pain improving and joint mobile
Re-evaluate diagnosis if monoarticular and toxic-appearing
disposition: Discharge home with anti-inflammatory therapy for likely gout/overuse
Refer to rheumatology/PCP for recurrent or undiagnosed arthritis*
Transfer to ER or call 911 if suspected septic joint, fever, or systemic toxicity
discharge: Medication instructions and renal/GI precautions
Rest, ice, elevation, and dietary advice for gout
Return precautions: fever, spreading redness, severe pain, inability to move joint
Recheck plan if not improved in a few days
followup: PCP in 3-7 days for medication response and urate management*
Rheumatology referral for recurrent/inflammatory arthritis*
Return promptly if fever or rapid worsening

redFlags: Hot, swollen joint with fever (possible septic arthritis)
Inability to bear weight with severe pain and systemic illness
Rapidly spreading erythema or signs of cellulitis/sepsis
Recent joint surgery or prosthetic joint with new pain/swelling
Immunocompromise with acute monoarticular swelling
compliance: Urgent care manages low-risk arthralgia/gout; suspected septic joint requires ER aspiration (EMTALA where applicable)
Do not give corticosteroids if joint infection cannot be excluded
Document temperature and joint exam; arrange follow-up for definitive diagnosis
