Counsel on NSAID/colchicine risks; provide written return precautions

name: Minor Head Injury
summary: Low-risk closed head injury, GCS 15, without high-risk features or focal neurologic deficit.

triage: Mechanism, loss of consciousness, amnesia, time since injury
Vitals and GCS/mental status
Apply Canadian CT Head rule to identify high-risk features
Ask about anticoagulants/antiplatelets and intoxication
nursing: Frequent neuro and GCS checks
Inspect scalp for laceration/hematoma; control bleeding
Document pupils, gaze, and focal deficits
Monitor for vomiting, worsening headache, or confusion
keyOrders: Serial neuro exams and GCS monitoring
Scalp wound cleaning/closure if minor laceration
POC glucose if altered mental status*
No on-site head CT; arrange ER transfer if imaging indicated
labs: None routinely; POC glucose if altered*
imaging: Head CT NOT available in urgent care
Transfer to ER for CT if any high-risk feature (Canadian CT Head rule positive), anticoagulation, or repeated vomiting
meds: Acetaminophen 650-1000 mg PO q6h for headache (preferred)
Avoid NSAIDs/aspirin acutely if bleeding risk/uncertainty*
Antiemetic (e.g., ondansetron 4 mg) for nausea*
Local anesthetic and wound care for scalp laceration repair
reassess: Repeat GCS/neuro exam before discharge
Confirm no new focal deficit, persistent vomiting, or worsening headache
Verify reliable adult observer available at home
disposition: Discharge home with concussion precautions if low-risk and GCS 15
Refer to PCP/neurology for persistent post-concussive symptoms*
Transfer to ER or call 911 if any high-risk feature, anticoagulation, focal deficit, or declining mental status
discharge: Written head-injury/concussion instructions for patient and observer
24-48 hr observation by responsible adult; wake checks if advised
Cognitive/physical rest and gradual return to activity
Return precautions: worsening headache, repeated vomiting, confusion, weakness, seizure, unequal pupils
followup: PCP in a few days to assess recovery
Neurology/concussion clinic for prolonged symptoms*
Return immediately for any deterioration

redFlags: GCS < 15, focal neurologic deficit, or seizure
Repeated vomiting, worsening or severe headache
Anticoagulant/antiplatelet use or known bleeding disorder
Suspected skull fracture, signs of basilar fracture, or penetrating injury
Loss of consciousness with high-risk mechanism or age over 65 with dangerous mechanism
compliance: Urgent care lacks CT; any high-risk head injury must be transferred to ER (EMTALA at hospital-based sites)
Document GCS, neuro exam, anticoagulation status, and CT-rule decision
Provide written head-injury precautions to patient and a reliable observer
Update tetanus for open scalp wounds; report suspected abuse/assault per Texas mandatory reporting

name: Upper Respiratory Infection (Common Cold)
summary: Self-limited viral upper-airway infection managed with symptomatic care and return precautions.

triage: Confirm walk-in, ambulatory, no respiratory distress
Vital signs including SpO2 and temperature
Screen onset, duration, fever pattern, and exposures
Identify high-risk comorbidities (asthma, COPD, immunocompromise)
nursing: Document symptom timeline and severity
Assess hydration and oral intake
Offer antipyretic if febrile and uncomfortable
Reinforce hand hygiene and cough etiquette
keyOrders: Visual exam of nose, throat, ears, and chest auscultation
Rapid COVID/influenza swab* if indicated by season/exposure
labs: Usually none needed for classic viral URI
Rapid strep* if indicated by sore-throat predominance
imaging: Not indicated for uncomplicated URI
Chest X-ray* if indicated by suspected pneumonia (focal findings, high fever)
meds: Acetaminophen 650 mg PO q6h PRN or ibuprofen 400-600 mg PO q6-8h PRN
Oral/nasal decongestant (pseudoephedrine 60 mg q4-6h or oxymetazoline spray <=3 days)
Guaifenesin 200-400 mg PO q4h PRN cough/congestion
Saline nasal spray and throat lozenges; avoid antibiotics (viral)
reassess: Recheck SpO2 and work of breathing before discharge
Confirm symptom control and ability to hydrate
Reassess if any red-flag features emerge
disposition: Discharge home with symptomatic care for most patients
PCP follow-up if symptoms persist >10 days or worsen
Transfer to ER or call 911 if respiratory distress, SpO2 <92%, or chest pain
discharge: Expect 7-10 day course; symptoms peak days 2-3
Return for fever >3-4 days, ear pain, dyspnea, or worsening
Hydration, rest, and OTC dosing instructions
followup: PCP if no improvement by 10 days
Return to urgent care sooner if red flags develop

redFlags: Respiratory distress, stridor, or SpO2 <92%
Chest pain or signs of pneumonia/sepsis
Inability to maintain hydration or altered mental status
Immunocompromised host with high fever
compliance: Within Texas urgent-care scope as symptomatic treat-and-release care
Avoid unnecessary antibiotics per antimicrobial stewardship
Provide written return precautions; EMTALA-style stabilize-and-transfer if distress
Urgent care is not a freestanding ER; escalate true emergencies via 911

name: Sore Throat / Pharyngitis
summary: Evaluation of acute pharyngitis with rapid strep testing and Centor-guided antibiotic use.

triage: Confirm patent airway and ability to swallow secretions
Vital signs and temperature
Apply Centor/McIsaac criteria (fever, exudate, nodes, no cough)
Screen for drooling, trismus, or muffled voice
nursing: Assess hydration and pain level
Inspect tonsils for exudate, asymmetry, or uvular deviation
Palpate anterior cervical nodes
Offer analgesia/antipyretic
keyOrders: Rapid strep antigen test (RADT) per Centor score
Throat culture* if indicated when RADT negative but high suspicion
labs: Monospot/heterophile* if indicated (adolescent/young adult, posterior nodes, fatigue)
imaging: Not routinely indicated
Soft-tissue neck X-ray or CT* deferred to ER if indicated by suspected abscess/epiglottitis
meds: Penicillin VK 500 mg PO BID x10d or amoxicillin 500 mg PO BID x10d if strep positive
Azithromycin or cephalexin if penicillin-allergic
Ibuprofen 400-600 mg PO and/or acetaminophen for pain
Single dose dexamethasone 10 mg PO* for severe odynophagia if no contraindication
Salt-water gargles and lozenges
reassess: Confirm patient can swallow liquids before discharge
Reassess airway if any voice change or pooling secretions
Verify analgesia adequate
disposition: Discharge home with antibiotics only if strep-confirmed
PCP/ENT referral for recurrent tonsillitis
Transfer to ER or call 911 if drooling, stridor, trismus, or suspected peritonsillar/retropharyngeal abscess or epiglottitis
discharge: Complete full antibiotic course if prescribed
Return for difficulty swallowing/breathing, drooling, or neck swelling
May return to work/school 24h after antibiotics and afebrile
followup: PCP if symptoms persist >7 days
ENT for >=3-4 tonsillitis episodes/year

redFlags: Drooling, stridor, or difficulty managing secretions
Trismus, muffled hot-potato voice, or uvular deviation (peritonsillar abscess)
Severe unilateral swelling or neck stiffness
Suspected epiglottitis or airway compromise
compliance: Test-confirm strep before antibiotics per stewardship guidelines
Airway emergencies (epiglottitis, abscess) require immediate ER transfer/911
Document Centor scoring rationale and return precautions
Urgent care manages uncomplicated pharyngitis only; not a substitute for ER airway care

name: Sinusitis
summary: Diagnosis and watchful-waiting versus antibiotic management of acute rhinosinusitis.

triage: Vital signs and temperature
Characterize duration (<10 days favors viral) and double-worsening pattern
Screen for facial pain/pressure, purulent drainage, congestion
Identify orbital or neurologic warning signs
nursing: Assess facial tenderness over maxillary/frontal sinuses
Check for periorbital swelling or visual complaints
Document symptom duration and severity
Offer analgesia
keyOrders: POC glucose* if indicated in diabetic with severe infection
labs: Rapid COVID/flu* if indicated by overlapping symptoms
imaging: Plain X-ray not recommended; not useful for routine sinusitis
CT sinus/orbit* deferred to ER if indicated by orbital/intracranial complication
meds: Symptomatic first-line: saline irrigation, intranasal fluticasone, analgesics
Oral/topical decongestant short course (oxymetazoline <=3 days)
Amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg PO BID x5-7d if bacterial criteria met (>10d, severe, or double-worsening)
Doxycycline 100 mg PO BID if penicillin-allergic
Acetaminophen or ibuprofen for pain
reassess: Confirm no orbital/neurologic signs before discharge
Set watchful-waiting expectations if viral
Verify pain control
disposition: Discharge home with symptomatic care or delayed/immediate antibiotic plan
PCP/ENT referral for recurrent or chronic sinusitis
Transfer to ER or call 911 if vision change, proptosis, periorbital cellulitis, severe headache, or altered mental status
discharge: Most viral cases resolve in 7-10 days
Return for worsening after initial improvement or new orbital/neuro symptoms
Saline rinse and decongestant instructions
followup: PCP if no improvement in 7 days on antibiotics
ENT for recurrent (>=4/year) or chronic sinusitis

redFlags: Periorbital/orbital swelling, proptosis, or vision changes
Severe headache, neck stiffness, or altered mental status
High fever with toxic appearance
Suspected intracranial or orbital extension
compliance: Reserve antibiotics for bacterial criteria per stewardship
Orbital/intracranial complications require emergent ER transfer/911 and CT
Document viral-vs-bacterial decision and return precautions
Within urgent-care scope for uncomplicated acute rhinosinusitis

name: Ear Pain / Otitis
summary: Otoscopic evaluation distinguishing otitis media from externa with targeted therapy.

triage: Vital signs and temperature
Localize pain (canal vs deep), tragal tenderness, discharge
Screen for hearing loss, dizziness, recent water exposure
Identify mastoid swelling/redness or facial weakness
nursing: Otoscopic exam of canal and tympanic membrane
Assess for canal edema, discharge, or TM bulging/perforation
Document hearing complaints
Offer analgesia
keyOrders: Cerumen removal/ear cleaning* if indicated to visualize TM
labs: Culture of discharge* if indicated for refractory otitis externa
imaging: Not indicated for routine otitis
CT temporal bone* deferred to ER if indicated by suspected mastoiditis
meds: Acute otitis media: amoxicillin 875 mg PO BID x5-7d (or amox-clav if recent abx)
Otitis externa: ofloxacin or ciprofloxacin-dexamethasone otic drops
Analgesia with ibuprofen and/or acetaminophen
Keep affected ear dry for otitis externa
Doxycycline/azithromycin if penicillin-allergic for AOM
reassess: Confirm pain control
Reassess for mastoid involvement or facial nerve signs
Verify proper drop instillation technique
disposition: Discharge home with appropriate oral or topical therapy
PCP/ENT referral for recurrent or persistent cases
Transfer to ER or call 911 if mastoid swelling/redness, facial palsy, severe vertigo, or toxic appearance
discharge: Expect improvement in 48-72h
Return for worsening pain, swelling behind ear, fever, or facial weakness
Avoid water exposure; complete therapy
followup: PCP if not improving in 48-72h
ENT for recurrent otitis or persistent effusion/hearing loss

redFlags: Postauricular swelling/erythema or protruding ear (mastoiditis)
Facial nerve palsy or severe vertigo
Toxic appearance or signs of intracranial extension
Immunocompromised host with severe otitis externa (malignant otitis)
compliance: Antibiotic stewardship; consider observation for mild AOM where appropriate
Mastoiditis/complicated otitis requires ER transfer and imaging
Document otoscopic findings and return precautions
Within urgent-care scope for uncomplicated otitis media and externa

name: Acute Bronchitis / Cough
summary: Symptomatic management of self-limited cough illness while excluding pneumonia.

triage: Vital signs with SpO2 and temperature
Assess cough duration, sputum, and work of breathing
Screen for fever, focal chest pain, and comorbidities (asthma/COPD)
Identify risk factors for pneumonia or PE
nursing: Lung auscultation for focal findings, wheeze, or crackles
Assess hydration and dyspnea
Offer antipyretic/analgesic
Reinforce smoking cessation if applicable
keyOrders: Lung auscultation and respiratory effort assessment
Nebulized albuterol* if indicated for wheezing/bronchospasm
labs: POC glucose* if indicated in diabetics with illness
imaging: Chest X-ray* if indicated by abnormal vitals, focal exam, or pneumonia suspicion
No imaging for classic uncomplicated bronchitis
meds: Symptomatic: dextromethorphan/guaifenesin, honey, hydration
Albuterol inhaler 2 puffs q4-6h PRN* if wheezing/bronchospasm
Ibuprofen/acetaminophen for fever and chest-wall soreness
Avoid antibiotics (viral etiology in most cases)
reassess: Recheck SpO2 and respiratory effort before discharge
Confirm no hypoxia or focal findings suggesting pneumonia
Reassess response if neb given
disposition: Discharge home with symptomatic care for uncomplicated bronchitis
PCP follow-up for cough >3 weeks or recurrent episodes
Transfer to ER or call 911 if SpO2 <92%, respiratory distress, hemoptysis, or suspected PE/pneumonia needing admission
discharge: Cough may last 2-3 weeks even after illness resolves
Return for dyspnea, SpO2 drop, high fever, or blood in sputum
Hydration, rest, and proper inhaler use if prescribed
followup: PCP if cough persists >3 weeks or worsens
Pulmonary referral for recurrent/chronic cough

redFlags: SpO2 <92% or respiratory distress
Hemoptysis or pleuritic chest pain with dyspnea (suspect PE)
Focal consolidation, high fever, or toxic appearance
Severe COPD/asthma exacerbation not responding to nebulizer
compliance: Antibiotics not indicated for uncomplicated acute bronchitis per stewardship
Hypoxia or pneumonia needing admission requires ER transfer/911
Document SpO2, lung exam, and return precautions
Within urgent-care scope; not for unstable respiratory failure

name: Influenza-like Illness
summary: Rapid testing and antiviral decision-making for febrile respiratory illness within urgent care.

triage: Vital signs with SpO2 and temperature
Document symptom onset (antiviral window <48h) and exposures
Screen for dyspnea and dehydration
Identify high-risk groups (>=65, pregnancy, chronic disease, immunocompromise)
nursing: Assess hydration and respiratory status
Offer antipyretic
Place in respiratory precautions/mask
Document onset time for antiviral eligibility
keyOrders: Rapid COVID swab* if indicated to differentiate
Nebulizer* if indicated for wheezing in at-risk patients
labs: POC glucose* if indicated in diabetics
imaging: Not routine
Chest X-ray* if indicated by hypoxia or pneumonia suspicion
meds: Oseltamivir 75 mg PO BID x5d if within ~48h or high-risk/severe
Acetaminophen or ibuprofen for fever/myalgia
Hydration and rest; cough/congestion symptomatic care
Baloxavir single dose* as alternative if indicated
reassess: Recheck SpO2 and hydration before discharge
Confirm tolerating oral intake
Reassess for distress in high-risk patients
disposition: Discharge home with antiviral (if eligible) and supportive care
PCP follow-up for high-risk or persistent symptoms
Transfer to ER or call 911 if SpO2 <92%, respiratory distress, chest pain, or dehydration/altered mentation
discharge: Fever typically 3-5 days; isolate until afebrile 24h
Return for dyspnea, persistent high fever, or worsening after improvement
Hydration and antiviral adherence instructions
followup: PCP if not improving in 5-7 days
Encourage annual influenza vaccination

redFlags: Respiratory distress or SpO2 <92%
Chest pain, severe dehydration, or altered mental status
Secondary bacterial pneumonia or sepsis signs
High-risk host deteriorating despite care
compliance: Start antivirals promptly for eligible/high-risk per CDC guidance
Severe influenza or pneumonia needing admission requires ER transfer/911
Implement respiratory precautions; provide return precautions
Within urgent-care scope for uncomplicated ILI; report per Texas public-health requirements where applicable

name: COVID-19
summary: Outpatient testing, risk stratification, and antiviral management for SARS-CoV-2 infection.

triage: Vital signs with SpO2 and temperature
Document symptom onset (Paxlovid window <=5 days) and exposures
Screen for dyspnea and hypoxia (consider ambulatory SpO2)
Identify high-risk comorbidities and vaccination status
nursing: Mask and respiratory isolation precautions
Assess work of breathing and hydration
Offer antipyretic
Document onset for antiviral eligibility
keyOrders: Vital signs with pulse oximetry (consider ambulatory walk test)
Rapid flu swab* if indicated to differentiate
labs: POC glucose* if indicated in diabetics
imaging: Not routine for mild disease
Chest X-ray* if indicated by hypoxia or pneumonia suspicion (defer CT/advanced imaging to ER)
meds: Nirmatrelvir-ritonavir (Paxlovid) 5-day course if high-risk and <=5 days onset (check drug interactions/renal dosing)
Acetaminophen or ibuprofen for fever/myalgia
Hydration and supportive care; cough management
Molnupiravir* as alternative if indicated when Paxlovid contraindicated
reassess: Recheck resting and ambulatory SpO2 before discharge
Confirm tolerating oral intake and no distress
Reassess high-risk patients carefully
disposition: Discharge home with isolation guidance and antiviral if eligible
PCP follow-up for high-risk or persistent symptoms
Transfer to ER or call 911 if SpO2 <92%, respiratory distress, chest pain, confusion, or bluish lips
discharge: Isolate per current CDC guidance; mask around others
Return for shortness of breath, persistent chest pain, or SpO2 drop
Home pulse-ox monitoring advice for high-risk patients
followup: PCP for symptom progression or post-COVID concerns
Encourage vaccination/boosters per current recommendations

redFlags: SpO2 <92% at rest or with ambulation
Respiratory distress, persistent chest pain, or bluish lips
Confusion or inability to stay awake
Dehydration or rapid clinical deterioration
compliance: Verify antiviral eligibility, renal dosing, and drug interactions before prescribing
Hypoxia/severe disease requires ER transfer/911 and stabilization
Follow current CDC isolation guidance; provide return precautions
Reportable per Texas DSHS communicable-disease requirements where applicable; within urgent-care scope for mild-moderate disease

name: Allergic Rhinitis
summary: Symptom-based management of allergic nasal/ocular symptoms with stepwise pharmacotherapy.

triage: Vital signs and temperature (typically afebrile)
Characterize triggers, seasonality, and sneeze/itch/congestion pattern
Differentiate from infection (absence of fever/purulence)
Screen for wheezing or asthma history
nursing: Inspect nasal mucosa (pale/boggy turbinates) and conjunctiva
Assess for associated wheeze
Document triggers and prior treatments
Review inhaler/spray technique if relevant
keyOrders: Peak flow/nebulizer* if indicated when concurrent wheeze
labs: Allergy testing deferred to outpatient/allergist (not done in urgent care)
imaging: Not indicated
Defer any sinus imaging to outpatient if chronic/atypical
meds: Intranasal corticosteroid (fluticasone 2 sprays each nostril daily) first-line
Second-generation oral antihistamine (cetirizine 10 mg or loratadine 10 mg daily)
Antihistamine eye drops (olopatadine) for ocular symptoms
Intranasal antihistamine (azelastine)* if indicated for breakthrough
Saline nasal irrigation and trigger avoidance
reassess: Confirm no respiratory distress or wheeze
Verify understanding of daily controller therapy
Reassess if asthma component present
disposition: Discharge home with stepwise allergy regimen
PCP/allergist referral for refractory or perennial symptoms
Transfer to ER or call 911 if signs of anaphylaxis (airway swelling, wheeze, hypotension)
discharge: Use nasal steroid daily for best effect (may take days to peak)
Return for wheezing, facial pain/fever (possible sinusitis), or no relief
Allergen avoidance and adherence counseling
followup: PCP if symptoms uncontrolled on therapy
Allergist for testing/immunotherapy consideration

redFlags: Signs of anaphylaxis: airway swelling, stridor, wheeze, hypotension
Acute respiratory distress
Periorbital swelling with fever suggesting infection
New severe asthma exacerbation
compliance: Allergy/immunotherapy testing deferred to outpatient allergist (out of urgent-care scope)
Anaphylaxis requires epinephrine and immediate ER transfer/911
Document medication plan and return precautions
Within urgent-care scope for symptomatic management of uncomplicated allergic rhinitis

name: Rash
summary: Evaluation of acute or subacute non-emergent skin eruptions in ambulatory adults.

triage: Vitals; assess for fever or systemic symptoms
Onset, distribution, new exposures (drugs, foods, plants, products)
Screen for airway/throat swelling, wheeze, or hypotension (anaphylaxis)
Ask about mucosal involvement, blistering, or skin sloughing
nursing: Document distribution, morphology, and percent body surface area
Photograph lesion if charting allows
Review medication and allergy list
Note prior similar episodes and treatments tried
keyOrders: POC glucose if diabetic with skin findings*
Wood lamp exam if fungal suspected* if indicated
labs: Rapid strep if scarlatiniform rash with pharyngitis*
KOH prep if available for suspected tinea* if indicated
Defer biopsy and serologies to dermatology/outpatient
imaging: Not indicated for isolated rash
meds: Cetirizine 10 mg PO daily or diphenhydramine 25-50 mg PO for itch
Topical hydrocortisone 1% or triamcinolone 0.1% BID for localized inflammation
Topical antifungal (clotrimazole) BID if tinea*
Short oral prednisone taper for widespread allergic dermatitis* if indicated
reassess: Recheck for spreading, systemic symptoms, or airway change
Confirm patient tolerates first antihistamine dose
Re-evaluate working diagnosis before discharge
disposition: Discharge home with symptomatic care for most localized rashes
PCP or dermatology referral if persistent, unclear, or chronic
Transfer to ER or call 911 if airway swelling, anaphylaxis, mucosal involvement, blistering/sloughing, or petechiae with fever
discharge: Avoid suspected trigger; use fragrance-free moisturizers
Return for spreading, fever, blistering, or breathing difficulty
Take antihistamines as directed; warn re sedation with diphenhydramine
followup: PCP in 1-2 weeks if not resolving
Dermatology referral for recurrent or undiagnosed rash
Sooner if worsening

redFlags: Anaphylaxis: airway swelling, wheeze, hypotension
Mucosal involvement, blistering, or skin sloughing (SJS/TEN)
Purpura/petechiae with fever (meningococcemia)
Rapidly spreading rash with systemic toxicity
compliance: Urgent care manages low-acuity rashes; refer complex/chronic dermatoses out
EMTALA-style transfer to ER for suspected SJS/TEN, anaphylaxis, or sepsis
Document return precautions and trigger avoidance
Urgent care is not a freestanding ER

name: Mild Cellulitis
summary: Outpatient management of nonpurulent, uncomplicated cellulitis without systemic toxicity.

triage: Vitals; flag fever, tachycardia, or hypotension
Mark borders of erythema with skin marker and time
Assess for rapid spread, severe pain, or crepitus
Screen diabetes, immunosuppression, and PAD
nursing: Measure and outline area of erythema/warmth
Elevate affected limb
Check for portal of entry (tinea pedis, wound, ulcer)
Photograph and document baseline
keyOrders: Wound culture if drainage present* if indicated
labs: Defer CBC/CRP/blood cultures to ER if systemic illness suspected
imaging: Usually none
Plain X-ray if foreign body or gas in tissue suspected* if indicated
Defer ultrasound/CT to ER for abscess vs necrotizing concern
meds: Cephalexin 500 mg PO QID x 5-7 days (nonpurulent)
Dicloxacillin 500 mg PO QID as alternative
Clindamycin 300-450 mg PO QID if penicillin-allergic
Add MRSA coverage (TMP-SMX or doxycycline) if purulent features*
reassess: Recheck demarcated borders for progression
Confirm patient stable and able to take oral meds
Reassess pain out of proportion (necrotizing concern)
disposition: Discharge home on oral antibiotics with limb elevation
PCP follow-up to confirm improvement in 48 hours
Transfer to ER or call 911 if systemic toxicity, rapid spread, crepitus, pain out of proportion, or need for IV antibiotics
discharge: Complete full antibiotic course; elevate limb
Re-outline marked border; return if spreads beyond it
Return for fever, worsening pain, or no improvement in 48 hours
followup: Recheck in 48 hours (urgent care or PCP)
PCP for recurrent cellulitis workup
Treat predisposing tinea pedis

redFlags: Pain out of proportion, crepitus, bullae, or skin necrosis (necrotizing fasciitis)
Systemic toxicity: high fever, hypotension, confusion (sepsis)
Rapidly advancing erythema
Immunocompromised or facial/orbital involvement
compliance: Urgent care treats mild, nonpurulent cellulitis without systemic signs
EMTALA-style transfer to ER for sepsis or necrotizing infection
Document border marking and 48-hour recheck plan
Antibiotic stewardship per local resistance patterns

name: Cutaneous Abscess
summary: Incision and drainage of simple superficial skin abscesses in ambulatory adults.

triage: Vitals; screen for fever or systemic signs
Assess size, fluctuance, and location
Flag high-risk sites (face, hand, genital, perirectal)
Screen diabetes and immunosuppression
nursing: Prepare I&D tray and local anesthetic
Document size and surrounding cellulitis
Photograph baseline
Verify tetanus status
keyOrders: Incision and drainage with packing if cavity large
labs: Defer CBC/blood cultures to ER if systemic illness
imaging: Usually none
Bedside ultrasound to confirm fluid collection if available* if indicated
Defer CT for deep/complex collections to ER
meds: Local anesthetic (lidocaine 1% with epinephrine, avoid digits/end-arteries)
I&D is primary therapy; antibiotics not always needed
TMP-SMX DS 1-2 tabs PO BID or doxycycline 100 mg BID if surrounding cellulitis or risk factors
Acetaminophen/ibuprofen for pain
reassess: Confirm adequate drainage and hemostasis
Verify patient understands packing/wound care
Reassess for spreading cellulitis
disposition: Discharge home after I&D with wound-care instructions
Wound recheck/packing removal in 24-48 hours
Transfer to ER or call 911 if deep/complex abscess, perirectal/facial danger zone, systemic toxicity, or requires OR drainage
discharge: Keep dressing clean and dry; warm compresses
Return for fever, increasing redness, or worsening pain
Complete antibiotics if prescribed
followup: Wound check in 24-48 hours for packing removal
PCP for recurrent abscesses (consider MRSA decolonization)
Surgery referral if complex

redFlags: Perirectal, facial danger triangle, or hand/deep space abscess
Systemic toxicity or surrounding necrosis
Immunocompromised host with rapidly enlarging collection
Suspected deep or septic-joint involvement
compliance: Urgent care performs simple I&D within scope; refer complex sites out
Verify and update tetanus prophylaxis
EMTALA-style transfer to ER for deep/danger-zone abscess or sepsis
Document procedure, consent, and follow-up plan

name: Insect Bite / Sting
summary: Management of local reactions to insect bites/stings and screening for systemic allergy.

triage: Vitals; screen immediately for anaphylaxis
Identify insect/exposure and time of sting
Assess for airway/throat tightness, wheeze, or hives spreading
Note prior systemic reactions
nursing: Remove retained stinger if present
Clean site; apply cold compress
Mark area of erythema/swelling
Monitor for evolving systemic symptoms
keyOrders: Tetanus update if skin broken* if indicated
labs: Defer allergy testing to allergist outpatient
imaging: Not indicated
meds: Cetirizine 10 mg PO daily or diphenhydramine 25-50 mg PO for itch
Topical hydrocortisone 1% or triamcinolone for local swelling
Acetaminophen/ibuprofen for pain
IM epinephrine 0.3 mg if anaphylaxis develops, then transfer*
reassess: Observe for delayed systemic reaction (15-30 min)
Recheck airway and vitals if any allergic symptoms
Confirm large local reactions are not cellulitis
disposition: Discharge home with antihistamines and wound care
Prescribe epinephrine auto-injector and allergist referral if systemic reaction history
Transfer to ER or call 911 if anaphylaxis, airway compromise, or hypotension
discharge: Cold compress, antihistamine, avoid scratching
Return for spreading redness, fever, or breathing difficulty
Carry epinephrine auto-injector if prescribed
followup: PCP if signs of secondary infection
Allergist if systemic reaction occurred
Return precautions reviewed

redFlags: Anaphylaxis: airway swelling, wheeze, hypotension, diffuse hives
Signs of secondary cellulitis or necrotic spider bite
Multiple stings with systemic toxicity
Suspected venomous bite needing antivenom (ER)
compliance: Urgent care manages local reactions and gives epinephrine for anaphylaxis then transfers
EMTALA-style transfer to ER/911 for anaphylaxis or systemic envenomation
Prescribe auto-injector and document return precautions
Update tetanus when skin barrier broken

name: Contact Dermatitis / Poison Ivy
summary: Treatment of localized to moderate allergic/irritant contact dermatitis in adults.

triage: Vitals; assess distribution and percent body surface area
Identify exposure (plants, metals, chemicals, cosmetics)
Screen for facial/genital or extensive involvement
Note prior episodes and treatments
nursing: Advise washing skin and clothing to remove allergen
Document distribution and severity
Photograph affected areas
Review medication/allergy history
imaging: Not indicated
meds: Mid/high-potency topical steroid (triamcinolone 0.1% or clobetasol) BID for localized disease
Oral prednisone taper (e.g., 40-60 mg tapered over 14-21 days) for severe/extensive
Cetirizine 10 mg PO daily or hydroxyzine for itch
Cool oatmeal baths and emollients
reassess: Confirm extent and rule out superinfection
Ensure understanding of allergen avoidance
Reassess if facial/periorbital swelling present
disposition: Discharge home with topical/oral steroids and antihistamines
PCP/dermatology if severe, recurrent, or unclear trigger
Transfer to ER if airway involvement, severe facial swelling, or signs of systemic infection
discharge: Wash exposed clothing/tools to remove urushiol
Complete full steroid taper; avoid identified trigger
Return for spreading infection, fever, or worsening swelling
followup: PCP in 1-2 weeks if not improving
Dermatology/allergist for recurrent dermatitis
Sooner if secondary infection

redFlags: Periorbital/facial swelling threatening airway or vision
Signs of secondary bacterial infection (impetiginization, fever)
Extensive blistering mimicking burn
Systemic symptoms suggesting another diagnosis
compliance: Urgent care manages routine contact dermatitis within scope
Use short steroid courses prudently; counsel on tapering
EMTALA-style transfer for airway-threatening facial involvement
Document trigger avoidance and return precautions

name: Sunburn
summary: Supportive care of first/superficial second-degree UV burns without systemic compromise.

triage: Vitals; assess hydration and percent body surface area
Screen for dehydration, syncope, or heat illness
Assess blistering (superficial partial thickness)
Note medications that increase photosensitivity
nursing: Cool compresses; encourage oral fluids
Document BSA and degree of burn
Assess for signs of heat exhaustion
Photograph extensive areas
keyOrders: POC glucose if syncopal/dehydrated* if indicated
labs: Defer electrolytes to ER if severe dehydration/heat illness
imaging: Not indicated
meds: Ibuprofen 400-600 mg PO q6-8h for pain/inflammation
Aloe vera or fragrance-free emollients topically
Topical hydrocortisone 1% for mild inflammation
Oral fluids; avoid breaking blisters
reassess: Recheck hydration status and vitals
Reassess for heat illness if febrile/dizzy
Confirm pain controlled with oral analgesics
disposition: Discharge home with supportive care
PCP if extensive blistering or slow healing
Transfer to ER if signs of heat stroke, large-BSA partial-thickness burn, or severe dehydration
discharge: Cool compresses, moisturizers, hydration, NSAIDs
Avoid sun; use SPF 30+ once healed
Return for fever, infection of blisters, or dizziness
followup: PCP if not improving in several days
Wound check if blisters become infected
Counsel on future sun protection

redFlags: Heat stroke: confusion, high core temperature, collapse
Severe dehydration or syncope
Extensive partial-thickness burn (large BSA)
Signs of secondary infection of blistered skin
compliance: Urgent care manages minor sunburn; large/deep burns or heat stroke go to ER
EMTALA-style transfer to ER/911 for heat stroke or severe dehydration
Document BSA, hydration, and return precautions
Counsel on photosensitizing medications

name: Laceration Repair
summary: Closure of simple, uncomplicated lacerations with intact neurovascular and tendon function.

triage: Vitals; control active bleeding with pressure
Time and mechanism of injury (clean vs contaminated)
Screen for arterial bleeding, deep structure injury
Verify tetanus status
nursing: Apply direct pressure; assess distal neurovascular function
Irrigate copiously with saline
Prepare closure tray and local anesthetic
Document wound length, depth, and contamination
keyOrders: Wound irrigation and exploration for foreign body/tendon involvement
Local anesthesia and primary closure (sutures, staples, adhesive, or strips)
labs: POC glucose if diabetic with wound* if indicated
imaging: Plain X-ray if radiopaque foreign body (glass/metal) suspected*
Defer to ER for suspected deep/joint/tendon involvement requiring imaging
meds: Lidocaine 1% (with epinephrine except digits/end-arteries) for local anesthesia
Acetaminophen/ibuprofen for pain
Topical antibiotic ointment under dressing
Oral antibiotics only for high-risk/contaminated wounds* if indicated
reassess: Confirm hemostasis and wound edge approximation
Recheck distal neurovascular status post-repair
Verify tetanus given if due
disposition: Discharge home with wound-care and suture-removal timeline
Hand/plastics referral for cosmetic or functional concerns
Transfer to ER or call 911 if uncontrolled bleeding, arterial injury, tendon/nerve involvement, or deep contaminated wounds beyond scope
discharge: Keep wound clean and dry 24-48 hours; then gentle cleansing
Suture removal timeline (face 3-5d, scalp/trunk 7-10d, extremities 10-14d)
Return for redness, drainage, fever, or wound dehiscence
followup: Wound check or suture removal at urgent care/PCP per site
Specialist referral if function or cosmesis at risk
Sooner if infection signs

redFlags: Uncontrolled or pulsatile (arterial) bleeding
Tendon, nerve, joint, or deep structure involvement
Heavily contaminated, bite, or crush wounds
Wounds over 8-12 hours old or with significant tissue loss
compliance: Urgent care repairs simple lacerations; refer functional/cosmetic-critical sites
Mandatory tetanus assessment and documentation
EMTALA-style transfer to ER for vascular/tendon/nerve injury
Document neurovascular exam before and after repair; obtain consent

name: Minor Burn
summary: Care of small superficial or superficial partial-thickness burns in non-critical areas.

triage: Vitals; estimate BSA and depth
Mechanism (thermal, chemical, electrical) and time
Screen for airway/inhalation injury, large BSA, or critical-area burns
Verify tetanus status
nursing: Cool burn with room-temp water; remove jewelry/clothing
Gentle cleansing; do not rupture intact blisters
Document depth, BSA, and location
Photograph baseline
labs: Defer labs to ER for large/electrical/chemical burns
imaging: Not indicated for minor thermal burns
meds: Acetaminophen/ibuprofen for pain
Topical antibiotic (bacitracin) or silver sulfadiazine (avoid on face) under dressing
Non-adherent dressing with petrolatum gauze
Oral opioid for short-term severe pain* if indicated
reassess: Reassess depth (may evolve) and pain control
Confirm BSA remains within urgent-care scope
Verify tetanus addressed
disposition: Discharge home with dressing and wound-care plan
Burn-clinic referral for deeper or slow-healing burns
Transfer to ER or burn center if large BSA, full-thickness, face/hands/feet/perineum/joints, circumferential, electrical/chemical, or inhalation injury
discharge: Daily dressing changes; keep clean and dry
Do not pop blisters; use prescribed topical
Return for increasing pain, drainage, fever, or spreading redness
followup: Wound check in 24-48 hours
Burn clinic/plastics if not healing or deep
PCP as needed

redFlags: Burns to face, hands, feet, perineum, or over joints
Large BSA, full-thickness, or circumferential burns
Electrical, chemical, or suspected inhalation injury
Signs of infection or inadequate pain control
compliance: Urgent care treats small superficial burns; critical-area/large burns go to ER or burn center
Mandatory tetanus assessment
EMTALA-style transfer to ER/burn center per ABA referral criteria
Report suspected abuse-related burns per Texas mandatory reporting

name: Wound Check / Suture Removal
summary: Routine follow-up of healing wounds and removal of sutures/staples in stable patients.

triage: Vitals; screen for fever or signs of infection
Confirm original injury date and closure type
Assess pain, drainage, and wound integrity
Review tetanus status from index visit
nursing: Inspect wound for approximation, erythema, drainage
Prepare suture/staple removal kit
Clean wound with saline
Document healing progress
keyOrders: Wound culture if purulent drainage* if indicated
labs: Wound culture only if infected* if indicated
POC glucose if diabetic with poor healing* if indicated
imaging: Not indicated unless retained foreign body suspected* if indicated
meds: Topical antibiotic ointment after removal if needed
Adhesive strips to support wound edges post-removal
Acetaminophen/ibuprofen for mild discomfort
Oral antibiotics if early infection identified* if indicated
reassess: Confirm wound edges stable after removal
Reassess for dehiscence or infection
Verify patient comfortable with ongoing care
disposition: Discharge home after removal and wound check
PCP/specialist referral if delayed healing or complication
Transfer to ER if deep infection, abscess, or significant dehiscence beyond scope
discharge: Keep area clean; apply adhesive strips until they fall off
Protect scar from sun; use SPF once healed
Return for redness, drainage, fever, or wound reopening
followup: PCP if scar concerns or delayed healing
Recheck if infection signs develop
Specialist if functional/cosmetic issue

redFlags: Spreading erythema, purulent drainage, or fever (infection)
Significant wound dehiscence
Underlying abscess or deep-space infection
Poor healing in immunocompromised/diabetic patient
compliance: Routine wound checks and suture removal are within urgent-care scope
EMTALA-style transfer for deep infection or significant dehiscence
Document healing status and return precautions
Coordinate with original treating provider when appropriate

name: Animal / Human Bite (Low-Risk)
summary: Care of superficial low-risk bites without deep-structure injury or systemic infection.

triage: Vitals; screen for systemic infection
Biting source, provoked vs unprovoked, vaccination/rabies risk
Assess depth, location, and neurovascular function
Verify tetanus status
nursing: Copious irrigation with saline
Assess for joint, tendon, or bone involvement
Document wound, source animal, and exposure history
Photograph baseline
keyOrders: Wound culture if signs of infection* if indicated
labs: Wound culture if infected* if indicated
Defer extensive workup to ER if systemic illness
imaging: Plain X-ray if fracture, deep puncture, or retained tooth/foreign body suspected*
Defer advanced imaging to ER for deep-structure concern
meds: Amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg PO BID x 5-7 days (first-line prophylaxis/treatment)
Doxycycline + metronidazole or clindamycin if penicillin-allergic
Acetaminophen/ibuprofen for pain
Generally close cosmetically important wounds loosely; leave most puncture/cat bites open
reassess: Recheck neurovascular and tendon function
Confirm rabies and tetanus risk addressed
Reassess for early infection
disposition: Discharge home on antibiotics with wound care
Wound recheck in 24-48 hours
Transfer to ER or call 911 if deep hand/joint bite, signs of established infection/sepsis, high rabies risk needing PEP, or extensive injury
discharge: Elevate; keep wound clean; complete antibiotics
Return for redness, swelling, drainage, fever, or reduced movement
Watch for early infection (cat bites high risk)
followup: Wound check in 24-48 hours
PCP/health dept for rabies follow-up and animal observation
Hand surgery referral if function affected

redFlags: Deep hand, joint, or tendon involvement (high infection risk)
Established infection or systemic toxicity
High rabies risk requiring post-exposure prophylaxis
Crush injury or significant tissue loss
compliance: Urgent care manages low-risk bites; refer deep hand/joint bites and rabies PEP needs
Texas mandatory animal-bite reporting to local health authority
Update tetanus; assess rabies risk and arrange PEP/animal observation
EMTALA-style transfer to ER for high-risk or infected bites

name: Chest Pain / Chest Discomfort
summary: Cardiac risk, ACS, PE, dissection
redFlags: Crushing pain or radiation
Diaphoresis or syncope
Abnormal vitals or ECG
Known CAD or stents

imaging: Chest X-ray
CTA chest if PE/dissection concern
meds: Aspirin 324 mg chewable
Nitroglycerin SL if appropriate
GI cocktail or reflux suspicion
Morphine or fentanyl PRN
disposition: Discharge with cards follow-up when low risk
Observation or transfer if ACS

name: Migraine / Headache
summary: Red flags, cocktail, neuro screen
redFlags: Worst headache of life
Neuro deficits or altered mental status
Fever or meningismus
Trauma or anticoagulation

keyOrders: Fall precautions when sedating meds are used
imaging: CT head without contrast if red flag
CTA/MRI selectively
meds: Migraine cocktail: IV fluids
Metoclopramide
Diphenhydramine
Ketorolac
Magnesium sulfate PRN
Dexamethasone
disposition: Neurology referral if recurrent
Return precautions for neurologic symptoms

name: URI / Shortness of Breath
summary: Respiratory distress and infection
redFlags: Severe respiratory distress
Hypoxia
Chest pain with dyspnea
Sepsis signs or altered mental status

imaging: Chest X-ray
CTA chest if PE concern
meds: DuoNeb/albuterol
Steroids, magnesium
Oxygen therapy
disposition: Home with instructions when stable
Admission or transfer if severe
Asthma action plan

name: GI / Stomach Issues
summary: Abdominal pain, N/V/D, pancreatitis
redFlags: Peritoneal signs
GI bleeding
Persistent hypotension
Severe dehydration
Concern for surgical abdomen

imaging: CT abdomen/pelvis
RUQ ultrasound
Pelvic ultrasound
meds: Ondansetron
Ketorolac
GI cocktail
Antibiotics if indicated
disposition: Transfer if surgical concern
Clear return precautions

name: UTI
summary: Cystitis, pyelonephritis, complicated UTI
redFlags: Sepsis
Pregnancy
Obstructing stone
Immunocompromised
Persistent vomiting

labs: Lactate and blood cultures if febrile
imaging: CT if obstruction or stone concern
Renal ultrasound
meds: Oral antibiotics for simple UTI
IV ceftriaxone when complicated or pyelo
Pyridium PRN
IV fluids
disposition: PCP follow-up
Culture callback workflow
Return if worsening

name: Kidney Stones / Flank Pain
summary: Renal colic and infected stone screen
redFlags: Obstructed infected stone
AKI or solitary kidney
Fever/sepsis
Uncontrolled pain

imaging: CT stone protocol
Ultrasound
meds: Ketorolac
IV fluids
Tamsulosin
Antiemetics
Opioids sparingly
disposition: Urology referral
Stone passage instructions
Return if worse or fever

name: General Body Pain / Fatigue / Dehydration
summary: Viral syndrome, weakness, electrolyte imbalance
redFlags: Sepsis
Rhabdomyolysis
AKI
Cardiac symptoms
Neurologic deficits

meds: IV fluids
Ketorolac or acetaminophen
Electrolyte replacement
Antipyretics
disposition: Hydration education
PCP follow-up
Return if worse

name: Stroke / Suspected Stroke
summary: FAST, door-to-needle, transfer readiness
redFlags: Face drooping
Arm weakness
Speech difficulty
Time to call 911 or activate protocol

imaging: CT head without contrast STAT
CTA head/neck
CT perfusion if available
meds: tPA eligibility: door-to-needle 60 min if eligible
Blood pressure management per protocol
disposition: Transfer to comprehensive stroke center
Admit if post-tPA
Neurology consult

eligibility: Mechanical thrombectomy <=24 hours when LVO suspected

name: Orthopedic Injury / Musculoskeletal
summary: Fracture, sprain, back pain, neurovascular checks
redFlags: Open fracture
Neurovascular compromise
Compartment syndrome
Spine injury
Severe deformity

imaging: X-ray appropriate area
CT/MRI if needed
Ultrasound for soft tissue
meds: Acetaminophen
NSAIDs
Opioids for severe pain only
Muscle relaxants
disposition: Splint/immobilization
Ortho referral
Crutches/instructions
Return precautions
